Bruno Bettelheim, director of the University of Chicago's Orthogenic School, took the idea further still, comparing autistic children's situation to that of concentration camp prisoners and casting the mother as the guard. The theoretical foundation was psychoanalytic: Freud and Adolf Meyer had trained a generation of clinicians to look for the emotional wound beneath every symptom, and the mother was the most convenient place to look. In the postwar period, genetic explanations carried the stain of the eugenics catastrophe, which made environmental accounts, even poorly evidenced ones, seem not only plausible but morally preferable. Researchers like Gregory Bateson, Theodore Lidz, and Lyman Wynne extended the framework to schizophrenia through studies of family dynamics, particularly Bateson's "double bind" theory. The DSM-II classified autism as a form of childhood schizophrenia, embedding the psychogenic assumption into the official diagnostic system of American psychiatry.

The theory spread through every channel available to medical orthodoxy: textbooks, journal articles, professional conferences, medical school curricula, and the clinical training pipeline controlled by the American Psychoanalytic Association, which required psychoanalytic training for membership and thereby ensured that the psychodynamic framework was the default lens for a generation of psychiatrists. The Child Guidance clinic movement, funded from 1922 by the Commonwealth Fund, had already built the institutional infrastructure for mother-focused intervention across the United States, and Kanner's work at the Henry Phipps Psychiatric Clinic at Johns Hopkins gave it an academic home. Bettelheim, a gifted popularizer, carried the theory to the general public through magazine articles and television appearances. The result was a set of policies that followed logically from the premise: if the mother caused the condition, the treatment was to remove the child from the mother. Bettelheim implemented "parentectomy" at the Orthogenic School, where children lived in residential care while their families were kept at a managed distance. Psychiatric hospitals treating schizophrenia conducted intensive psychoanalytic therapy and structured family studies designed to document pathogenic parenting. Early family therapy for schizophrenia aimed to correct the mother's behavior, not to reduce stress or provide practical support. These were not fringe practices; they were the standard of care at elite institutions including Chestnut Lodge in Maryland.

The harm was extensive and compounding. Mothers of autistic children were told by credentialed psychiatrists that their emotional coldness had produced their child's condition. Mothers of schizophrenic patients were told their pathological communication patterns had driven their children into psychosis. The guilt, shame, and marital discord this produced were not side effects of the theory; they were its direct and predictable consequences, imposed on families already under severe strain. Beyond the emotional damage, the theory directed resources toward treatments that did not work. Families spent years and substantial money on psychoanalytic therapies premised on the refrigerator mother hypothesis. At the Orthogenic School, former residents later came forward with accounts of physical abuse, and Bettelheim's academic credentials were found to have been substantially fabricated. The mother-blaming framework also provided cover for later dangerous interventions, including extreme "cures" like bleach enemas promoted within the broader autism cure culture that the original theory had helped create. The focus on correcting parental behavior systematically diverted attention from developing services for autistic adults, a gap in provision that persisted long after the theory itself had lost credibility.

The first serious challenge came in 1964, when Bernard Rimland, a research psychologist and father of an autistic son, published "Infantile Autism: The Syndrome and Its Implications for a Neural Theory of Behavior." Rimland argued that the psychogenic case rested on a post hoc fallacy: clinicians had observed withdrawn parents and autistic children, assumed the former caused the latter, and never considered that the same genetic factors might produce both traits, or that raising a severely disabled child might make any parent seem cold. He proposed neurological origins and backed the argument with a systematic literature review. Kanner himself recanted at the 1969 meeting of the National Society for Autistic Children, telling parents they were "off the hook." Lorna Wing, a British psychiatrist and mother of an autistic daughter, conducted the careful epidemiological research that the theory's proponents had never bothered with, consistently finding no evidence that parental behavior was responsible. A 1977 twin study produced a heritability estimate for autism above 80 percent, with subsequent research pushing the figure to approximately 90 percent and monozygotic twin correlations near .98, leaving essentially no room for shared family environment as a causal factor. Twin and adoption studies for schizophrenia produced parallel results. Thomas McGlashan's 1983 outcome analysis at Chestnut Lodge showed that decades of psychoanalytic treatment had failed schizophrenia patients, while Carol Anderson and Gerard Hogarty's psychoeducation model demonstrated that supporting families without blaming them reduced relapse rates. Gordon Parker's 1982 methodological review exposed the foundational studies as riddled with nonrandom samples, absent controls, rater bias, and systematic failure to account for the child's own deficits.

The institutional response was slow but eventually decisive. The DSM-III in 1980 separated autism from schizophrenia and classified it as a developmental disorder, removing the diagnostic scaffolding for the psychogenic interpretation. The National Alliance on Mental Illness, formed in 1979 by families who had spent years being blamed, allied with biological psychiatry to demand a different approach. E. Fuller Torrey's 1983 handbook for families of people with schizophrenia treated the illness as a brain disease and the family as a resource rather than a cause, and it sold widely. Growing evidence now suggests that schizophrenia originates in fetal brain development, before any postnatal parenting could take effect, and that autism is among the most heritable of all developmental conditions. By the mid-1980s, the refrigerator mother theory had lost its institutional footing in mainstream psychiatry. Its legacy, in the form of parental guilt, inadequate adult services, and the cultural habit of blaming mothers for their children's neurology, has proved considerably more durable than the theory itself.
---

## Racial Demographic Change Will Not Cause Upheaval 

*Racial and ethnic demographic change in Western nations is inherently manageable and will not produce significant social disruption.*

For most of the postwar period, Western governments operated on the premise that racial and ethnic demographic change could be absorbed without serious social disruption. The belief rested on several pillars: contact theory, which held that proximity between groups would breed mutual understanding; census projections treated as neutral fact rather than politically charged narrative; and the assurances of lawmakers who promised that reforms like the 1965 Hart-Celler Act would not alter the ethnic composition of their nations. Ted Kennedy told the Senate the bill would "not flood our cities with immigrants" or "upset the ethnic mix." President Johnson called it "not a revolutionary bill." Attorney General Robert Kennedy predicted Asian immigration would "virtually disappear" after a brief initial wave. Emanuel Celler assured colleagues that few Africans or Asians would arrive because they had few relatives already in the country. These men were not lying in the conventional sense; they appear to have believed what they said. They were simply wrong. Chain migration from Asia and Latin America produced over eighteen million legal immigrants in the following three decades, triple the prior thirty years, and the foreign-born share of the American population rose from five percent to fifteen.

The assumption that this transformation would proceed smoothly was reinforced by institutions at every level. The U.S. Census Bureau released projections showing non-Hispanic whites falling below fifty percent by 2042; media outlets stripped away the "non-Hispanic" qualifier and presented a simple story of white decline, using disaster metaphors and binary graphics. Brookings published William Frey's work celebrating the "diversity explosion" as good news for an aging nation. The Vatican under Pope Francis made generous migrant welcome a central teaching, with Cardinal Krajewski refusing a papal blessing to Italy's Salvini over his restrictive stance. In France, Didier Leschi, head of the immigration integration office, publicly rejected the term "submersion migratoire" as extreme-right rhetoric, while Insee statistics showing foreigners at 8.2 percent of the population were cited as proof that levels remained manageable. In Germany, Angela Merkel told her country "Wir schaffen das" and opened the door to over a million refugees from Muslim countries. In Britain, governments of both parties maintained high net migration through relaxed visas, peaking at 906,000, while housing 32,000 asylum seekers in 210 hotels at £1.3 billion annually. The intellectual class, meanwhile, treated any objection as evidence of bigotry. American Renaissance, Jared Taylor's publication examining diversity outcomes, was labeled supremacist. Nigel Farage endured decades of abuse, including beer thrown at him, for warning of consequences. In Portugal, Afonso Gonçalves of Reconquista faced ridicule and threats for raising the subject of remigration. The message from polite society was clear: demographic change was happening, it was good, and only racists said otherwise.

Robert Putnam's research should have been the first crack in the foundation. His 2000 survey of 30,000 people across 41 American communities found that ethnic diversity reduced trust, volunteering, voting, charitable giving, and community cooperation. Neighbors in the most diverse settings trusted one another about half as much as those in homogeneous ones. Neither contact theory nor conflict theory predicted the actual result: a general civic malaise in which people withdrew from public life without forming either cross-group bonds or intergroup hostilities. Putnam, a liberal who supported diversity in principle, sat on the findings for years, running additional checks because he disliked the implications. When he published in 2007, Scott Page of the University of Michigan called the results "shocking," and Edward Glaeser of Harvard linked American diversity to roughly half the gap in social welfare spending compared to Europe. Fifteen additional studies reviewed by Kahn and Costa corroborated the pattern, connecting diversity to lower school funding, reduced census response rates, diminished trust, and even higher Civil War desertion rates. Survey experiments later showed that media framing of a "white minority" future increased anxiety and anger in nearly sixty percent of white respondents and seventy-five percent of Republicans, while reducing support for redistribution. The narrative itself was generating the polarization it claimed to lament.

The human costs accumulated across continents. In Rotherham, authorities allowed the organized sexual exploitation of at least 1,400 children, mainly white girls as young as eleven, over sixteen years because police and council staff feared being called racist for confronting the ethnic dimension of the abuse. Professor Alexis Jay's independent report exposed how councillors believed discussing racial patterns would "give oxygen to racists." In Cologne on New Year's Eve 2015, inadequate police deployment based on the assumption that recent migrants posed no special crowd risk led to over 1,200 criminal charges, including roughly 500 for sexual assault; the governing coalition later allegedly tried to remove criticism of state administration from the parliamentary report. In Britain, twenty-five terror suspects entered via small boats in 2024 alone, six of them Iranian-backed fugitives who went missing or remained under loose surveillance, raising the prospect of attacks on government buildings. In Belgium, native mothers accounted for just 52.3 percent of births by 2020, native fertility stood at 1.38 versus 2.05 for foreigners, and the native population share collapsed from 81.8 percent in 2001 to 67.3 percent two decades later, while foreigners at 7.6 percent of the population were two to four times more likely to commit theft, sexual assault, and murder. In France, sixty-five percent of young Muslim high-schoolers told pollsters they prioritized Islamic norms over the laws of the Republic; Islamist threats led to event cancellations, professor attacks, death threats requiring permanent police protection for anthropologist Florence Bergeaud-Blackler, and, at the extreme, the Charlie Hebdo massacre and the beheading of Samuel Paty. In Ireland, the designation of a hotel in a village of 165 as housing for 280 asylum seekers triggered seventy-five days of protests, police road closures, and similar standoffs with arson in Roscrea and Clonmel.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption is now collapsing under the weight of its own predictions. A 2024 BFMTV-Elabe poll found sixty-seven percent of French citizens, including majorities from nearly every political party, shared the sentiment of migratory submersion. In Germany, INSA polling showed sixty-one percent supporting a ban on Muslim migration and seventy-seven percent believing newcomers resented Western society; the AfD surged as the political expression of that disillusionment. Donald Trump improved his share of the Latino vote by roughly twenty-five points in 2024 and won decisively in heavily Hispanic states, demolishing the theory that non-white voters constituted a permanent progressive bloc and exposing the demographic-fatalism school promoted by analysts like John Judis and Ruy Teixeira in their influential "Emerging Democratic Majority" thesis. In Portugal, Reconquista's investigative reportage turned "remigration" from a term of ridicule into a central axis of national debate, adopted by politicians including Chega leader André Ventura. In Britain, Reform UK polled at thirty-four percent against Labour's twenty-five, driven partly by what commentators called the "mum vote" over safety fears. Demographers David Coleman and Stuart Basten at Oxford published research showing that the decline narrative had been exaggerated all along, that fertility had ticked upward in several Western countries, and that the real demographic crises lay in China and elsewhere. None of this means the assumption has been fully discredited; an influential minority of scholars and a growing share of the public now reject it, but institutional inertia remains powerful. The policies built on the premise that demographic transformation required no special precautions are still in force in most Western nations. The question is no longer whether disruption will occur but how much has already been locked in.
---

## Students Benefit from Integrated Schools

*Students benefit academically and socially from racially integrated schools.*

For decades, the assumption that students benefit academically and socially from racially integrated schools guided American education policy at every level, from local school boards to the Supreme Court. The idea drew its force from the moral clarity of Brown v. Board of Education and the civil rights movement that followed. If segregation was wrong, integration must be right, and the benefits would flow naturally once children of different races shared classrooms. Post-Brown momentum and subsequent court rulings legitimized forced busing and racial balancing as tools to achieve this vision. The assumption seemed so self-evident that questioning it was treated not as a policy disagreement but as a moral failing.

The assumption produced an extraordinary range of policies. In New York City, the Department of Education reserved 25 percent of middle school seats in District 3 for low-income, low-performing students from outside the zone, and Chancellor Richard Carranza championed the plan as self-evidently beneficial. When parents objected, Carranza retweeted a headline accusing wealthy white parents of ranting against desegregation and told a supportive white parent to take anti-bias training. The city budgeted $23 million for implicit-bias training of teachers, treating resistance as prejudice to be corrected rather than a legitimate concern about school quality. In Minnesota, the St. Cloud school district hired bilingual liaisons, built prayer rooms, offered pork-free lunches, and adjusted sports uniforms for hijabs, all on the conviction that such accommodations would make integration work for Somali refugee students, many of whom arrived with no prior schooling, malnutrition, and trauma. In Seattle and Jefferson County, Kentucky, school districts classified children in binary racial categories (white or nonwhite, black or other) and used racial tiebreakers for school assignments, believing this achieved the integration that would deliver academic and social gains. Lower courts upheld these plans by extending the diversity rationale from the Grutter higher-education case to K-12 settings, even though the original decision had emphasized holistic review rather than race as the determinative factor.

The harm was varied and, in some cases, severe. In late-1960s New York, administrators at Lane High School interpreted the integration mandate to mean they should avoid disciplining black students, lest they face accusations of racism. The central board advised teachers not to enforce rules about ID cards, the pledge, or hats. Leslie Campbell, vice president of the Afro-American Teachers Association, used the permissive atmosphere to promote militant ideology among black students through the Afro-American Students Association, distributing propaganda and organizing boycotts. Over a decade, Lane saw fifteen assaults on white teachers by black students, attacks on white students, invasions by groups of up to 150 armed youths, and repeated class cancellations. On January 20, 1969, teacher Frank Siracusa was beaten, kicked in the groin, sprayed with lighter fluid, and set on fire. He required hospitalization. The incident made front pages nationwide. In St. Cloud, Talahi Elementary, 45 percent Somali, was classified as a state priority school with test scores in the bottom 5 percent, despite years of accommodations and federal recognition. Protests erupted in 2015 over bullying and anti-Muslim incidents. In Seattle and Jefferson County, parents saw their children denied preferred schools on the basis of race while districts refused to consider race-neutral alternatives. White flight to suburbs further undermined the effectiveness of mandatory busing. Carranza's rhetoric alienated even supporters of integration, and he was eventually forced to apologize for the inflammatory retweet.

The assumption began to crack from multiple directions. The Siracusa burning and a 1970 U.S. Office of Education report linking integration efforts to increased fights, extortion, and bullying broke the spell that had surrounded claims about busing's automatic benefits. In 2007, the Supreme Court reversed the lower courts in Parents Involved in Community Schools v. Seattle School District, holding that the Seattle and Jefferson County plans were not narrowly tailored and that racial balancing for its own sake was not a compelling government interest. Race-neutral alternatives had been available and ignored. In St. Cloud, persistent low test scores and student protests exposed the gap between policy promises and classroom reality. Growing evidence suggests that the association between racial segregation and achievement gaps may be better explained by economic segregation, and that the academic and social benefits attributed to integration were overstated. Some research indicates that desegregation increased educational attainment for black students without harming white students, but an influential minority of analysts now argues that the blanket assumption, that integration reliably produces academic and social gains, was far too simple to justify the sweeping and sometimes destructive policies it generated.
---

## Lobotomy Cures Psychiatric Disorders

*Lobotomy is an effective neurosurgical treatment for psychiatric disorders by severing prefrontal cortex connections.*

For roughly two decades in the middle of the twentieth century, tens of thousands of psychiatric patients had the connections in their prefrontal cortex severed on the theory that this would cure their mental illness. The assumption rested on an associationist model of brain pathology: mental disorders arose from abnormal excitations in the brain's association centers, and surgical lesions could disrupt those excitations and restore proper function. The idea had antecedents as far back as 1888, when the Swiss psychiatrist Gottlieb Burckhardt removed sections of cerebral cortex from chronic asylum patients, but it gained real momentum at the 1935 International Neurological Congress in London. There, John Fulton and Carlyle Jacobsen of Yale presented chimpanzees whose frustration behavior had been attenuated by frontal lobectomy, and Richard Brickner described a single human case, one of Walter Dandy's patients, whose bilateral frontal lobectomy for a meningioma had produced personality changes but preserved basic cognition. In the audience sat António Egas Moniz, the Portuguese neurologist who left the congress convinced he could treat psychosis by cutting the frontal lobes. He performed his first leucotomies on asylum patients in late 1935, published results the following year claiming the procedure was simple and safe, and set in motion a catastrophe.

Walter Freeman, an American neurologist, modified the technique into the transorbital lobotomy, performed the first U.S. operation in 1936, and spent the next two decades traveling the country with an ice pick and a missionary's zeal. He performed roughly 3,500 lobotomies himself; an estimated 490 of those patients died as a result. Worldwide the total reached approximately 100,000 by 1971. The procedure spread through channels that gave it an air of scientific legitimacy. The 1949 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine went to Moniz for discovering the therapeutic value of leucotomy; Professor H. Olivecrona, presenting the award, called it one of the most important discoveries in psychiatric therapy. Early results from the Burden Neurological Institute in Bristol and Warlingham Park Hospital were published in The Lancet, prompting mental hospitals across Britain to adopt the operation. William Sargant, a British psychiatrist who had met Freeman in the United States, encouraged its introduction at the Burden Institute, where Frederick Golla collaborated on the first British leucotomies in 1940. A 1962 follow-up study in the Canadian Medical Association Journal reported that sixty-seven percent of 116 lobotomized patients had improved enough to leave the hospital, lending peer-reviewed authority to the claim that the procedure compared favorably with existing treatments.

The policies that followed were grim in their efficiency. By 1951 nearly twenty thousand lobotomies had been performed in the United States, most in state mental hospitals where administrators welcomed anything that made patients more manageable and reduced the burden on overcrowded wards. In Britain, prefrontal leucotomy was offered as an alternative to therapeutic nihilism, to the straitjackets and ice baths and insulin shock that preceded it. Hospitals such as Crichton Royal, Graylingwell, Belmont, and Runwell adopted the procedure for chronic and violent patients. Over twenty thousand psychosurgical operations were eventually carried out in the United Kingdom alone. The surgery was sometimes performed on young adults and even children. Consent, where it existed at all, was a formality.

The harm was enormous and largely irreversible. Patients suffered stupor, incontinence, seizures, and what can only be described as personality erasure. Freeman himself described one patient as having "the personality of an oyster." The Canadian study that endorsed selective use of the procedure also found personality defects in ninety-one percent of cases and epilepsy in twelve percent. Twenty-six percent of initially improved patients relapsed and required re-institutionalization. Early British surveys showed a six percent death rate. The procedure reduced the complexity of psychic life, leaving survivors with infantile personalities, inertia, and detachment. Moniz's own results were modest at best: one-third of his patients significantly improved, one-third somewhat improved, one-third unchanged, and many were rendered apathetic. Moniz himself was shot by one of his psychosis patients, a detail that carries its own bleak irony.

The assumption began to collapse in the mid-1950s with the introduction of chlorpromazine and other antipsychotic medications, which provided symptom relief without destroying frontal lobe tissue. The Soviet Union had already banned psychosurgery in 1950. European countries followed as pharmacological alternatives proved superior. Growing evidence suggested the entire enterprise had been, as Nobel laureate Torsten Wiesel later put it, "a terrible mistake that caused permanent damage to thousands of patients." The Mental Health Act 1983 restricted psychosurgery in Britain to consenting patients with commission approval, a belated acknowledgment of what had been done without consent for decades. Wilder Penfield, the famed neurosurgeon who had warned Freeman early on that the procedure was dangerous, was proved right, though not before the damage was done on an industrial scale. A small number of neurosurgeons continued to defend highly selective psychosurgery into the 1960s and beyond, and the debate over its precise place in medical history is not entirely settled. But the broad consensus that once embraced lobotomy as a major therapeutic advance has evaporated, replaced by growing recognition that it stands as one of medicine's most consequential failures of judgment.
---

## Parents Primarily Shape Children

*Children turn out the way they do primarily because of their parents' child-rearing styles.*

For most of the twentieth century, the Western world operated on a premise so deeply embedded in professional psychology and popular culture that it was rarely stated as a hypothesis: children turn out the way they do primarily because of how their parents raise them. Sigmund Freud placed the mother at the center of every neurosis. B. F. Skinner arrived with different terminology but the same conclusion: the child's environment, meaning chiefly the family, determined the child's character. Diana Baumrind gave the assumption its most rigorous and durable scientific form in the 1960s, classifying parents as authoritative, authoritarian, or permissive and correlating these styles with child outcomes in ways that seemed to confirm the premise. Maccoby and Martin extended her typology in the 1980s. For half a century, Baumrind's framework served as the conceptual backbone of parenting research, and the field built an elaborate edifice of findings on top of it. The correlations were real and replicable. What the framework never adequately addressed was the possibility that those correlations were not causal but genetic: warm, consistent parents tend to have children genetically predisposed to the same traits, and the style and the outcome share a common cause rather than standing in a cause-and-effect relationship.

The assumption spread through the most ordinary channels. Child-rearing books, which proliferated enormously in the postwar decades, were built entirely on the premise that the right technique, applied consistently, would produce the desired child. Introductory psychology textbooks treated parental influence as established fact. Graduate programs trained researchers to study parenting effects without controlling for genetics. The phrase "nature versus nurture" did its own damage by implying that nurture meant parenting, when in fact the non-genetic influences on children include peers, schools, neighborhoods, random events, and prenatal conditions, none of which parents fully control. Peer-reviewed journals published parenting-effects research that did not separate genetic from environmental transmission well into the 2000s, and contemporary review articles still describe Baumrind's typology as strongly supported, citing the correlational literature without adequately flagging its methodological limitations. John Money, a psychologist at Johns Hopkins, took the assumption to its most consequential extreme, claiming that gender identity was entirely a product of social learning. His reports of the David Reimer case, a genetic male surgically reassigned and raised as female, circulated in medical literature for thirty years as proof that parental rearing could override biology. The case became the basis for a clinical standard applied to an unknown number of XY infants at multiple hospitals.

The policies that followed were wide and varied. Over seven decades, professional consensus shifted parenting norms substantially: physical punishment declined, praise and affection increased, and parents were instructed to prioritize children's self-esteem. Multicultural education programs, bilingual instruction, school busing, and various social interventions were designed on the assumption that the right environmental inputs could override innate differences and peer influences. Money's theory produced the most direct policy application: surgical reassignment of XY infants with penile abnormalities, justified by a single case that had been falsely reported as successful. The harm was correspondingly direct. David Reimer was subjected throughout childhood to humiliating examinations and forced sexual rehearsals conducted by Money under the guise of therapy. He never accepted a female identity, was suicidally depressed by thirteen, and died by suicide at thirty-eight. His twin brother Brian, subjected to the same examinations, died by suicide two years earlier. For parents in the general population, the harm was less dramatic but pervasive: a culture of anxiety and guilt built on the premise that every parental decision determined the child's future, sustained by a social science literature that gave them every reason to believe it. The massive shift in parenting norms over the postwar decades left no clear positive trace in population-level data on adult psychological wellbeing, aggression, or confidence.

The evidentiary case against the assumption had been building for decades before it reached a general audience. Twin studies from the 1970s onward consistently found that identical twins reared apart were nearly as similar in personality and intelligence as those reared together. Adopted children raised in the same home by the same parents resembled their biological parents far more than their adoptive ones. Tinca Polderman's 2015 meta-analysis, synthesizing virtually all twin research published over fifty years and covering nearly fifteen million twin pairs, found that the shared environment, the category that includes everything parents do deliberately, accounted for a small and often negligible fraction of variance in psychological outcomes. Genome-wide complex trait analysis confirmed these heritability estimates directly from DNA, removing the last methodological objection. Robert Plomin, a behavioral geneticist at King's College London, stated the conclusion without qualification in his 2018 book "Blueprint": parents do not environmentally shape their children's psychological traits in any consistent or lasting way. The correlations that had sustained the nurture assumption were almost entirely reflections of shared genes, not shared environments. Research even showed that maternal positivity toward children was itself heritable, while maternal control and negativity were largely responses to adolescents' heritable behaviors. The arrow of causation ran in the opposite direction from what the field had assumed.

Judith Rich Harris, a New Jersey grandmother and former textbook author, brought the behavioral genetics literature to a general audience with her 1998 book "The Nurture Assumption." She argued that peers, not parents, are the primary environmental socializers of children, which explained why children of immigrants adopt the accents of their peers rather than their parents, and why siblings raised in the same home turn out so differently. Steven Pinker summarized her thesis with characteristic economy: "Genes matter and peers matter, but parents don't matter." The formulation overstated the case slightly, as Pinker himself noted that parents can damage development through abuse and neglect even if they do not construct it, but it captured the shock of the argument. Milton Diamond published the true outcome of the Reimer case in 1997, and John Colapinto's subsequent book "As Nature Made Him" ended Money's professional influence. Brian Nosek's large-scale replication efforts demonstrated that a substantial fraction of published findings in social psychology could not be reproduced, casting further doubt on the literature that had sustained the parenting-effects consensus. The assumption that parents primarily shape their children was not overturned by a single study or a single critic. It was eroded by four decades of converging evidence from twin studies, adoption studies, molecular genetics, and replication failures, all pointing to the same conclusion: the most important thing parents give their children is their genes.
---

## School Spending Closes Racial Gaps

*Racial gaps in school achievement are primarily caused by lower spending on black students, and increasing spending will narrow those gaps.*

For sixty years, the governing assumption of American education policy was plain enough: black students scored lower because less money was spent on their schooling, and more money would fix it. The belief had surface plausibility. Under Jim Crow, southern states openly shortchanged black schools. Property tax funding meant poor districts spent less per pupil than rich ones. When the Civil Rights Act of 1964 funded what became the Coleman Report, its authors expected to document how unequal resources produced unequal results. The Great Society programs that followed, above all the Elementary and Secondary Education Act of 1965, poured federal dollars into poor districts on the explicit premise that money was the missing ingredient. Lyndon Johnson signed the bill as a matter of faith. So did nearly everyone else.

The Coleman Report, published in 1966, should have ended the discussion before it began. It found that school spending explained remarkably little of the variation in student achievement. Family background swamped everything else. But the report's implications were unwelcome, and the policy apparatus moved on without absorbing them. Instead, a secondary belief took hold: if equal spending was not enough, then more spending was needed. State courts, beginning in the 1990s, shifted from "equity" lawsuits to "adequacy" claims, forcing legislatures in nearly every state to boost funding for high-poverty districts. The Century Foundation published a widely cited study claiming schools were underfunded by $150 billion annually, with districts serving Black and Latino students facing average shortfalls above $5,000 per pupil. Bruce Baker of Rutgers built the cost model; Halley Potter and Mark Zuckerman promoted it with interactive maps timed to coincide with the pandemic recession and racial protests of 2020. The Education Law Center graded states annually on whether they directed enough money to poor districts. The NAACP blamed "inequitable school funding" for threats to quality education. Linda Darling-Hammond said unequal resources created "the bell curve of achievement differences." Gaston Caperton, president of the College Board, blamed SAT gaps on an unfair funding system. Even the conservative writer John McWhorter conceded the funding disadvantage was real. Teachers unions pushed for higher salaries on the same premise. Politicians from Elizabeth Warren to Cory Booker built platforms around closing racial gaps through targeted spending, whether on housing grants, baby bonds, or early childhood programs. Walmart committed $100 million over five years to its Center for Racial Equity. The Association of American Medical Colleges set numerical targets for minority physicians. Pennsylvania mandated "culturally responsive" competencies in all educator training. The assumption was not merely widespread; it was structural.

The money flowed. U.S. K-12 spending doubled in real terms after 1970, reaching $600 billion annually. Federal education spending tripled. New York State increased aid 76 percent since 2012 even as enrollment fell. Baltimore maintained one of the highest per-pupil expenditures in the nation. The School Improvement Grants program alone spent $3 billion. By the 2000s, U.S. Department of Education data showed per-pupil spending was roughly equal across racial groups, with non-white students receiving slightly more. An Urban Institute study confirmed that minority spending had exceeded white spending since the 1980s. The old funding gap, to the extent it had existed at the national level, was closed. The achievement gap was not. NAEP scores remained flat or declined over decades. The black-white SAT math gap held steady at roughly 0.9 standard deviations from 1996 to 2015. In 2016, Baltimore's state assessments revealed zero proficient students in math and reading at six schools. New York ranked 46th nationally in NAEP math despite record outlays. A Cato Institute study across all fifty states found no correlation between spending increases since the 1970s and outcomes in NAEP scores or graduation rates. Poland, Finland, and South Korea outperformed the United States while spending considerably less per student. One-third of schools receiving federal School Improvement Grants actually got worse. The 2024 NAEP results documented continued stagnation and declines despite the largest infusions of education money in American history.

The harm was not merely fiscal, though the fiscal waste was staggering. Billions went to compliance bureaucracies; states spent 7.8 million hours yearly and $235 million just on Title I paperwork. Billions more went to programs that produced no measurable gains, while the lowest-performing students lost ground. In Baltimore, 200,000 adults were functionally illiterate, libraries closed, and the city had the lowest economic mobility in the state. In Baton Rouge, court-ordered busing produced 61 arrests at Woodlawn High in a single year, filmed racial brawls, and a teacher caught choking a student, eventually driving a majority-white community to vote for secession and costing the parish $48 million in annual tax revenue. Across the country, the assumption that spending would close gaps diverted attention from factors the Coleman Report had identified in 1966: family structure, home environment, and the stubborn heritability of cognitive ability documented by twin and adoption studies. Affluent black children still scored below affluent white children. Gaps appeared before school entry and persisted in desegregated settings. The data were consistent and unwelcome, so they were mostly ignored. Florida was sued by the Southern Poverty Law Center for setting race-specific achievement goals that acknowledged real performance differences, on the theory that acknowledging gaps constituted discrimination. Researchers at USC published a paper arguing that even the phrase "achievement gap" triggered racist stereotypes, and advocates on Twitter promoted the linguistic change as though renaming the problem might solve it.

The assumption did not collapse in a single moment. It eroded under the accumulated weight of evidence that no one wanted to compile into a single narrative. The Coleman Report was the first crack, in 1966. Decades of flat NAEP scores despite rising budgets widened it. International comparisons showed that money was neither necessary nor sufficient. The equalization of per-pupil spending across racial groups removed the factual premise without removing the policy. Court-ordered adequacy funding produced no adequacy. Corporate philanthropy like Walmart's Center for Racial Equity quietly expired after five years without renewal. College enrollment data revealed that the sharpest declines were among white students, not minorities, upending the standard narrative. The 2024 NAEP results, arriving after the largest emergency spending in education history, left little room for the old excuse that the check simply had not been large enough. What remains is a policy infrastructure built on a premise that was testable, was tested, and failed, repeatedly, across six decades, at a cost of trillions of dollars and two generations of students who deserved something better than a theory.
---

## Israel Serves US Strategic Interests

*Unwavering US support for Israel advances American national security and interests in the Middle East.*

For the better part of sixty years, American foreign policy operated on the premise that unwavering support for Israel advanced US national security and strategic interests in the Middle East. The assumption had roots in the Cold War, when Israel's intelligence services delivered tangible goods like a stolen Soviet radar in 1969 and captured weapons that gave American analysts insight into Soviet military technology. After the Cold War ended, the rationale shifted seamlessly to counterterrorism: Israel's enemies were America's enemies, and the two countries were fighting the same war by different means. A parallel moral argument, portraying Israel as a beleaguered democracy surrounded by authoritarian regimes and carrying a special post-Holocaust obligation, proved even more durable than the strategic case. John F. Kennedy coined the phrase "special relationship" in 1962. Joe Biden, across a career spanning the Senate, the vice presidency, and the presidency, described the commitment as "naked self-interest" and declared that "if there were no Israel, we'd have to invent one." The formulation was repeated so often, by so many officials, that it acquired the status of self-evident truth.

The assumption was propagated through a dense institutional network. AIPAC cultivated relationships on both sides of the aisle and made the strategic asset argument a bipartisan article of faith, hosting speeches by presidential candidates and sitting officials who competed to demonstrate the depth of their commitment. Think tanks provided the intellectual scaffolding: the Washington Institute for Near East Policy published a 2011 report by Robert D. Blackwill, a former deputy national security adviser, and Walter B. Slocombe, a former under secretary of defense, asserting that Israeli and American interests aligned "virtually identically" and enumerating contributions from intelligence sharing to joint missile defense development. The Council on Foreign Relations lent additional prestige. Congress translated the assumption into appropriations with remarkable consistency, approving a record $38 billion military aid package in 2016 and, since 1976, providing more than $140 billion in total assistance on terms available to no other country: early disbursement, 25 percent spent on Israel's own industry, no accounting of end use, and access to weapons systems denied to NATO allies. At the United Nations, the United States cast 43 vetoes of resolutions critical of Israel since 1972, more than all other permanent members combined. Bipartisan leaders described the relationship as "unbreakable," a word that functioned less as a description than as a signal that the relationship was beyond the normal calculus of costs and benefits.

The costs accumulated across decades. The 1973 American airlift to Israel triggered the OPEC oil embargo, which damaged Western economies far more than the aid delivered was worth. During the 1991 Gulf War, the United States had to expend significant diplomatic capital keeping Israel out of the conflict to avoid fracturing its Arab coalition, illustrating that the alliance was, in operational terms, a constraint rather than a force multiplier. Unconditional support provided recruitment material for Islamist extremist groups and generated sustained anti-Americanism across the Arab and Muslim world, complicating relationships Washington needed for other strategic purposes. The Jonathan Pollard espionage case revealed that the intelligence relationship ran in directions the strategic asset argument preferred not to discuss; Pollard disclosed the names of thousands of people who had cooperated with US intelligence agencies and sold the National Security Agency's ten-volume manual on American signals intelligence collection. Government Accountability Office and State Department reports documented Israeli technology transfers to China and a pattern of reneging on commitments. The 1967 attack on the USS Liberty killed 34 American sailors, wounded 174, and destroyed the ship, yet the United States accepted Israel's apology and a compensation payment of close to $13 million without further consequence. The Gaza campaign beginning in October 2023 brought the tensions into sharpest relief: roughly 32,000 dead, mostly women and children, vast infrastructure destruction, more than 100 approved US arms sales, vetoed ceasefire resolutions, and stated red lines ignored without consequence, all of which inflicted measurable damage on American credibility and standing, particularly in the Global South.

The assumption began to crack publicly with the 2006 essay and subsequent book by political scientists John Mearsheimer and Stephen Walt, who argued in the language of realist international relations theory that the Israel lobby had pushed American foreign policy farther from the national interest than any other organized group, and that unconditional support caused terrorism, anti-Americanism, and diplomatic handicaps. The response from the establishment was fierce, but the argument did not disappear; it gained new readers with each subsequent crisis. A substantial body of experts now rejects the proposition that the benefits of unconditional support consistently outweigh the costs. The Gaza war accelerated the reckoning, as the United States found itself simultaneously approving weapons for operations its own officials called unacceptable and vetoing ceasefire resolutions while publicly urging restraint. The Cato Institute's assessment was blunt: the war had exposed the gap between the assumption's claims and the observable reality of American influence, image, and maneuverability in the region. Whether the assumption was wrong from the start or simply outlived the Cold War conditions that gave it plausibility remains genuinely contested among serious analysts. What is no longer seriously contested is that the question deserves an honest answer.
---

## Education Builds Human Capital

*Education causally builds human capital and productivity through skills learned in school, driving economic growth and labor market returns.*

The assumption that education causally builds human capital, that schools impart skills which raise productivity and thereby drive both individual earnings and national economic growth, was for decades the most broadly accepted proposition in economics. It enjoyed bipartisan support so complete that questioning it meant arguing against the stated interests of virtually every organized constituency in education policy. Democrats cited it to justify public investment in schools; Republicans cited it to justify workforce development. The empirical foundation looked solid: cross-country regressions showed that greater schooling enrollment in 1960 was associated with 0.30-percent faster annual GDP growth over the following three decades. A bachelor's degree carried an earnings premium of roughly 73 percent over a high school diploma. The inference seemed obvious. Schools teach skills, skills raise productivity, productivity raises wages. Robert J. Barro's models of transitional human capital growth became foundational in development economics. Philippe Aghion, Caroline Hoxby, Leah Boustan, and Jerome Vandenbussche refined the argument with instrumental variable strategies presented at the Brookings Papers on Economic Activity conference, distinguishing between graduate education suited to innovation near the technological frontier and undergraduate education suited to imitation. The prestige of the venues matched the confidence of the claims.

Growing evidence suggests, however, that the human capital model overstates what schooling actually does. Bryan Caplan, an economist at George Mason University, spent years assembling the case that signaling, not skill-building, explains the majority of education's labor market payoff. His book "The Case Against Education" argued that employers reward degrees because graduation certifies pre-existing traits like intelligence, conscientiousness, and conformity, not because the curriculum imparts useful knowledge. The evidence he marshaled was varied and convergent. Sheepskin effects showed that the graduation year of high school adds 15.1 percent to earnings compared to 4.4 percent for a normal year; for college, the figures are 34.1 percent versus 5.1 percent. If schooling built skills uniformly, the payoff per year should be roughly uniform. It is not. Ability bias corrections, controlling for IQ and non-cognitive traits, reduced the estimated education premium by 20 to 45 percent, meaning a large share of apparent returns to schooling were returns to characteristics that existed before the student ever enrolled. Students forget most of what they learn after exams. Graduates typically cannot explain discounted present value. Schools rarely teach students to fill out a tax form. Foreign language requirements yield low returns in English-dominant America. Jason Brennan and Phillip W. Magness extended the critique to higher education's internal incentive structures, arguing that universities are organized around the interests of faculty and administrators rather than student learning.

The macroeconomic version of the assumption fared no better under scrutiny. Mark Bils and Peter J. Klenow calibrated a human capital accumulation model to Mincerian returns from 56 countries and found that schooling's impact on growth explains less than one-third of the empirical cross-country relationship. The remainder was attributable to omitted factors (property rights, economic openness) and reverse causality: anticipated growth reduces the effective discount rate, independently boosting schooling demand and producing a correlation that runs partly in the wrong direction. Education yields a personal return of roughly 10 percent per additional year across countries, but national returns are mixed, low, and in some studies negative, indicating that much of the private benefit is redistributive rather than productive. The assumption spread anyway, carried by the academic prestige of the growth literature and by the cultural authority that education commands in every modern society. Thomas Sowell praised immigrant cultures that valued schooling, treating their economic success as proof that education investment pays off, without distinguishing between pre-existing cognitive traits, cultural selection, and actual skill acquisition.

The policies built on this assumption were enormous in scale. The U.S. government spends approximately $1 trillion per year on education across all levels, justified almost entirely by the premise that schooling builds productive skills generating returns exceeding the public cost. Countries enacted curriculum reforms extending or compressing mandatory schooling by a year based on projected human capital benefits, then found no measurable earnings gains for affected cohorts. Head Start, the federal preschool program, received over $8 billion annually on the explicit premise that early educational intervention produces lasting academic gains for low-income children, at a per-pupil cost exceeding $14,000 over two years. A congressionally mandated evaluation completed in 2010 found no lasting benefits by first grade and some evidence of harm in math skills. HHS delayed releasing the third-grade follow-up past its September 2011 deadline; Senator Tom Coburn demanded its release; President Obama had pledged to fund only programs that work. Head Start's budget was not cut. In Pennsylvania, mandatory culturally responsive education guidelines were imposed on teacher certification in 2022, premised on the logic that the right kind of schooling produces the right kind of results, until a 2024 legal settlement made them optional.

The harm is both measurable and diffuse. Credential inflation raised the average education required for employment by 1.5 years between the early 1970s and mid-1990s, while higher-skilled occupations accounted for only 0.3 years of that increase. The remaining 1.2 years represented a pure positional arms race, with workers spending time and money acquiring credentials that signaled relative standing rather than absolute capability. Public subsidies underwriting this process transferred resources from taxpayers to a competition that left aggregate productivity unchanged. The credential race delays family formation, raises divorce risk, and has been linked to declining birth rates. An influential minority of economists now argues that signaling accounts for at least half, and possibly as much as 80 percent, of education's labor market payoff. The human capital model is not dead, but the confidence with which it was once applied to justify a trillion dollars in annual spending looks increasingly difficult to defend. Approximately 56 percent of education returns may reflect genuine human capital effects and 44 percent signaling, though the two theories remain, by the admission of researchers on both sides, extremely difficult to distinguish empirically. What can be said is that the assumption of pure human capital, the assumption on which the spending was justified, was never as solid as the people spending the money believed.
---

## There Are Five Stages of Grief

*Grief universally progresses through denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance in sequence.*

In 1969, the Swiss-born psychiatrist Elisabeth Kübler-Ross published "On Death and Dying," a book that would be cited over 15,000 times and reshape how the Western world thought about loss. Drawing on interviews with more than 200 terminally ill patients, she described five emotional responses she observed: denial, anger, bargaining, depression, and acceptance. The tidy acronym DABDA entered the lexicon almost immediately. Kübler-Ross was working in good faith; she was responding to a real problem, the tendency of hospital staff to ignore the psychological dimensions of dying. Her framework, however, was built on anecdotal observations of people facing their own deaths, not on systematic study of bereaved survivors. That distinction was lost almost as soon as the book hit the shelves. The model was swiftly generalized to grief after bereavement, then to divorce, job loss, amputation, chronic illness, and eventually to disappointments as trivial as a new iPhone. Earlier stage theories by Bowlby and Parkes, and a later synthesis by Jacobs, lent the idea of orderly emotional progression an air of scholarly consensus it never earned. No empirical study established that people actually move from stage one through stage five.

The model spread with extraordinary speed and penetration. By 1982, it was being taught in an estimated 125,000 courses across medical schools, seminaries, nursing programs, hospitals, and social work institutions. A 1997 survey found medical schools relied heavily on it. The National Cancer Institute maintained a website describing phases of grief drawn from the model. Textbooks reproduced it routinely, often without noting criticisms or the crucial fact that it was developed for the dying, not the bereaved. A study of 72 websites found that 61.1% addressed the model positively or uncritically, with no mention of its limitations. Pop culture absorbed it completely; movies, television shows, and self-help books referenced the five stages as settled fact. Kübler-Ross herself became one of Time Magazine's 100 Most Important Thinkers and was inducted into the Women's Hall of Fame. The model's appeal was simple: it promised to tell people what to expect, to make the chaos of grief feel orderly and navigable.

The harm was substantial and well documented. Bereaved individuals who did not experience the prescribed sequence, which is to say most of them, were made to feel they were grieving incorrectly. Health-care professionals, trained to expect orderly progression, made hasty assessments and offered ineffectual support. The Institute of Medicine warned that stage theory could produce inappropriate behavior toward the bereaved. Social psychologist Carol Tavris observed that stage theories create guilt and pressure on grievers who fail to experience the right emotions at the expected times. People who recovered quickly were met with suspicion rather than relief, because the model taught that prolonged suffering was normal and necessary. Meanwhile, the roughly one in ten bereaved individuals who develop prolonged grief, marked by years of unrelenting yearning, went unidentified because the model offered no tools for distinguishing normal from abnormal adjustment. The framework lacked any treatment utility whatsoever.

The warnings came early and were ignored for decades. In 1989, researchers Wortman and Silver published a critique highlighting the model's widespread adoption despite its total lack of evidence, and the harmful consequences for bereaved persons. Charles Corr warned against rigid application and exposed misrepresentations in textbooks. Robert Neimeyer of the University of Memphis called the theory "suspiciously simplistic" and confirmed that scientific studies failed to support any sequence of emotional phases. A 1981 study of 193 widows found no stages and persistent stress. George Bonanno's longitudinal research found that only 11 percent of bereaved individuals followed anything resembling the assumed trajectory; the most common pattern was resilience, not prolonged suffering through sequential phases. Studies across diverse traumas, including the September 11 attacks, SARS, divorce, and accidents, consistently revealed the same three patterns: resilience, recovery, and prolonged grief. No stages. Bisconti and colleagues showed in 2004 that emotional wellbeing oscillates rather than progressing. A survey by Sawyer and colleagues in 2021 found that professionals were far more skeptical of the model than the general public, with only 8 percent of professionals endorsing it as definitely true compared to 30 percent of the public. Even Kübler-Ross herself eventually conceded that the stages were not linear or sequential, that most patients experienced multiple stages simultaneously and not in order, a clarification that amounted to a quiet repudiation of the model's core claim.

The five stages of grief remain embedded in popular consciousness, still cited on wellness websites and in TED talks, still offered as comfort by well-meaning friends. The scientific verdict, however, is unambiguous. Stroebe, Schut, and Boerner called for its discard in professional practice. Russell Friedman of the Grief Recovery Institute stated plainly that no study has established stages of grief exist. The model was never validated, never useful for treatment, and never accurate as a description of how human beings actually grieve. What it did accomplish was to make millions of suffering people feel defective for failing to follow a script that no one follows, because it does not describe anything real.
---

## No Racial Differences in Athletic Ability

*Racial gaps in athletic performance such as West African dominance in sprinting and East African dominance in distance running stem from culture, stereotypes, or environment rather than genetics.*

The assumption was straightforward: the dramatic overrepresentation of West African-descended athletes in sprinting and East African-descended athletes in distance running was a product of culture, economics, stereotype, and opportunity, not of genetics. The belief had understandable origins. The association of racial biology with eugenics, colonial exploitation, and Nazi atrocity made any claim about innate group differences radioactive in postwar science. Richard Lewontin's influential 1970s finding that genetic variation within any racial group exceeds variation between groups gave the position a quantitative foundation that seemed decisive, even though it said nothing about whether small average differences in specific trait distributions could produce large effects at the extremes of athletic performance. Stephen Jay Gould's 1981 "The Mismeasure of Man" reinforced the intellectual framework, and by the turn of the century a generation of popular science writers, including Angela Saini ("Superior"), Gavin Evans ("Skin Deep"), and Adam Rutherford ("How to Argue with a Racist"), had made the denial of genetic explanations for racial athletic gaps a marker of scientific respectability. Joe Henrich at Harvard attributed Kenyan distance running success to cultural emphasis. Paul Campbell at the University of Leicester dismissed evolved body type differences as myth. Yannis Pitsiladis told the BBC that Asian athletes' failure to challenge West African sprint dominance was "a head thing." The position was not fringe; it was orthodoxy, enforced by the most powerful institutions in science publishing and media.

The assumption spread through mutually reinforcing channels. Nature published Saini's review of "Skin Deep," which praised Evans for demolishing genetic explanations and framed hereditarian inquiry as persistent pseudoscience. The BBC, particularly during Olympic cycles, quoted sociologists who attributed African running dominance to social construction and economic incentive. Academic papers deployed terms like "racialist paradigm" and "scientific racism" to discourage research. Tade Souaiaia and colleagues published "Revisiting Stereotypes: Race and Running," arguing that non-West Africans breaking sprint records disproved inherent advantage. The deliberate-practice framework of K. Anders Ericsson and colleagues, based on their 1993 study of violinists, provided a broader intellectual scaffold: the claim that expert performance in any domain was sufficiently explained by accumulated practice, not innate endowment. Popular science books and high citation counts carried the message far beyond academia. Meanwhile, the broader cultural environment made dissent costly. When James Watson, co-discoverer of DNA's structure, stated publicly in 2007 that Africans might have lower average intelligence than Europeans, the Federation of American Scientists, the U.S. government's supervisor of genetic research, and the New York Times condemned him within days. He was stripped of his honorary titles and forced into retirement. The lesson was not subtle.

The policies that followed from the assumption were varied but consistent in their logic. The Association of American Medical Colleges set numerical targets for underrepresented minorities in the physician workforce, premised on the idea that observed disparities reflected only remediable barriers. Mandatory diversity, equity, and inclusion programs were imposed across academic medicine. In collegiate athletics, recruiting cultures at major Division I-A football programs systematically deprioritized white high school athletes with competitive statistics, feeding a talent pipeline to the NFL built on assumptions about which populations produced which kinds of players. Broader education policy treated average cognitive differences between racial groups as entirely environmental and therefore fully correctable through equalized opportunity, generating decades of interventions whose results consistently fell short of predictions.

The harm was substantial and took multiple forms. Scientists withheld data on ethnic physiological differences, as David Epstein documented in "The Sports Gene," calculating that the professional risks outweighed the scientific value. Henry Harpending, a National Academy of Sciences member, had his fieldwork observations dismissed by Saini as "bizarre" in a major journal. Research agendas were distorted; funding flowed toward environmental interventions with smaller effects than predicted; dissenting careers were destroyed. Watson's fate ensured that few researchers would follow him into the open. In medicine, the pursuit of demographic proportionality through racial preferences produced ongoing tension between representation targets and qualification standards, with the targets proving consistently unachievable without reducing those standards. White high school athletes with strong performance records were passed over in recruiting, a pattern documented by critics but rarely examined in sports media, where the narrative of Black athletic superiority made the outcomes appear meritocratic.

A substantial body of evidence now challenges the assumption. Analysis of the top 100 all-time 100m performances shows 97 by men of West or South African ancestry. West African-descended athletes hold 99.7% of the top 350 times despite representing roughly 7.4% of the global population. East African dominance in distance running is equally stark: 3,343 of the top 5,049 marathon times belong to Kenyan and Ethiopian runners. The Swedish physiologist Bengt Saltin tested Kenyan boys against Swedish elites and found superior muscle capillaries and mitochondria. The ACTN3 R577X genotype shows measurable association with elite sprint performance; every male Olympian power athlete studied carried at least one copy of the functional R allele. The morphological specificity is telling: Eliud Kipchoge, the marathon world record holder, stands 5'5" and weighs 123 pounds; Usain Bolt, the sprint world record holder, stands 6'5" and weighs 207 pounds. John Hawks's 2007 study demonstrated that human genetic evolution accelerated more than a hundredfold with the advent of agriculture, and Ilan Libedinsky's 2023 study confirmed bursts of recent evolution particularly in mental function, vision, and nutrient processing. David Reich, the Harvard geneticist, warned that after 70,000 years of separation, average differences between European and African populations may extend beyond the superficial. Macnamara, Hambrick, and Oswald's 2014 meta-analysis demolished the deliberate-practice framework, showing practice explained only 18% of variance in sports performance and less than 1% in professions, leaving the vast majority of variance unexplained by effort alone. Evans himself, in "Skin Deep," grudgingly acknowledged some genetic role in Kenyan athletic success, including slow-twitch muscle fibers and long legs, a concession Saini's Nature review managed not to mention. The assumption has not been universally abandoned, but the data have made it increasingly difficult to maintain with a straight face.
---

## Schools Can Make All Students Equal

*Schools and equalized environments can eliminate or substantially narrow individual differences in student achievement and learning outcomes.*

The assumption that schools can make all students substantially equal in achievement is among the most expensive beliefs in the history of public policy. It rests on a simple chain of reasoning: children differ in academic performance; schools differ in resources; therefore, equalizing resources will equalize performance. The logic seemed self-evident to the architects of the Great Society, to the framers of No Child Left Behind, and to the state legislators who have collectively pushed American K-12 spending to nearly $1 trillion annually. It seemed credible in part because the alternative, that individual differences rooted substantially in genetics drive most of the variation, was and remains politically intolerable. Howard Gardner's theory of multiple intelligences gave educators a palatable framework for dismissing IQ as "too simplistic to measure intelligence with one score," and teacher training programs taught it widely despite its near-total lack of support among research psychologists. Arthur Jensen warned over fifty years ago that an equalization philosophy built on denying genetic differences in ability would fail. He was treated as a pariah. The Coleman Report of 1966, commissioned under the Civil Rights Act with the expectation that it would document school-resource disparities driving achievement gaps, instead found that a child's family background was the primary predictor of academic success and that school resources explained remarkably little once family factors were controlled. The Johnson administration drew as little attention to the findings as possible and pressed ahead with the Elementary and Secondary Education Act.

The assumption spread through every layer of the education system. Federal compensatory programs like Head Start, the Banneker Project, and Higher Horizons were enacted on the deprivation hypothesis, the idea that academic lag resulted mainly from social and economic deprivation that schools could remedy. The United States Commission on Civil Rights evaluated these programs and found no significant group achievement gains. No Child Left Behind mandated annual testing, adequate yearly progress reporting, and escalating sanctions for failure, with President Bush setting the explicit target of 100 percent proficiency by 2014. Secretary of Education Rodney Paige promoted the law as fulfilling civil rights promises and closing the achievement gap. States were allowed to define their own proficiency standards, which produced absurdities like Kentucky inflating its AYP rate from 59 to 94 percent through alternate statistical methods. Court-ordered desegregation, implemented most aggressively in the South from the early 1970s onward, rested on the same logic: shared schools would equalize environments and thereby equalize outcomes. New York City spent nearly $400,000 per student over a K-12 career for its 2013-17 cohort, and roughly $2.2 million per low-income student who eventually earned an associate's or bachelor's degree. Teacher training programs, meanwhile, actively suppressed accurate information about IQ and genetics to maintain egalitarian premises, with large majorities of teachers rejecting the relevance of heredity and endorsing Gardner's debunked framework.

The harm has been diffuse, cumulative, and enormous. Mixed-ability classrooms created by opposition to tracking forced teachers to address fifth-grade classes spanning second- to eighth-grade skill levels, an impossible task. Massive resources were diverted toward equalization while high-ability students underperformed and low-performing students saw no lasting gains. The 2024 NAEP results showed 12th-grade scores at record lows, with the lowest-performing students losing 12 scale points from 2013 to 2024. Racial gaps persisted almost unchanged: 54 percent of white fourth-graders were proficient in math versus 18 percent of Black students and 26 percent of Hispanic students, and income-based gaps widened. In St. Cloud, Minnesota, Talahi Elementary, 45 percent Somali, became a state priority school with bottom-five-percent test scores despite bilingual liaisons, prayer accommodations, and pork-free lunches. In Baton Rouge, Woodlawn High recorded 61 arrests in 2013 and repeated filmed brawls, leading to the successful St. George secession movement that siphoned $48 million in annual tax revenue from the parish. The Stand and Deliver mythology, which compressed Jaime Escalante's decade of patient program-building into a story of one inspirational year, misled teacher training programs into emphasizing quick fixes; after Escalante left Garfield High in 1991, the number of students passing the AP calculus exam collapsed from 85 to 11. Pennsylvania mandated Culturally Relevant and Sustaining Education competencies in teacher certification without evidence they improve outcomes, distorting training curricula toward bias-disruption frameworks built on contested claims about microaggressions.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption's core premise is wrong. Decades of twin, adoption, and genome-wide studies have converged on the finding that in wealthy industrialized countries, the heritability of intelligence approaches 0.80 in adults, meaning roughly 80 percent of individual differences are genetic. Twin studies show that shared environment, the parenting, books, and neighborhood factors that equalization policies target, has very small effects on intelligence by adulthood. Ninety percent of achievement differences stem from individual factors, leaving school-level variables to explain roughly ten percent. The Coleman Report's findings have been corroborated repeatedly; quasi-experimental studies like those by Chetty and Hendren confirmed that family structure, neighborhood, and parental education drive outcomes through channels schools cannot replicate. College Board data from 1996 to 2015 showed the Black-white SAT math gap unchanged at approximately 0.9 standard deviations despite fifteen years of interventions. A meta-analysis of deliberate practice in sports found it explained only 18 percent of performance variance overall and just 1 percent among elites, undermining the broader claim that environmental inputs largely determine individual differences. NAEP data from 2013 to 2024 documented regression after Common Core implementation, with peaks in 2013 followed by steady drops, establishing that the most ambitious curricular standardization effort in American history produced nothing.

An influential minority of researchers now argues that the equalization assumption has not merely failed but has actively prevented honest engagement with the sources of academic differences. Jensen's warning, Coleman's data, and the accumulating genetic evidence all point in the same direction: schools cannot do what they have been asked to do, and pretending otherwise wastes resources, demoralizes teachers, and harms the students the policies claim to help. The Soviet Union banned standardized testing in 1936 rather than confront the persistence of individual differences after equalization. American policy has been more subtle but not fundamentally different, lowering cut scores, inflating graduation rates, and redefining proficiency downward rather than revisiting premises. The question is not whether schools matter; they do, modestly. The question is whether a society can build effective education policy on a premise that the best available evidence increasingly suggests is false.
---

## Foreign Assistance Pulls Africa Out of Poverty

*Foreign aid and reparations will kickstart economic growth and lift developing countries out of poverty.*

For the better part of half a century, the governments of wealthy nations, the United Nations, the World Bank, and a substantial portion of the development economics profession operated on a shared premise: that Africa was poor because it lacked capital, and that if enough capital were transferred from rich countries to poor ones, growth would follow. The logic had a surface plausibility that made it durable. The Two-Gap Model held that developing economies were constrained by shortages of savings and foreign exchange. The Solow-Swan Growth Model suggested capital injections would raise output per worker. Jeffrey Sachs of Columbia University argued that Africa was caught in a poverty trap from which only a massive, coordinated push of external investment could extract it. The United Nations formalized this reasoning through the Millennium Development Goals, which calculated that Africa needed 7 percent annual growth to halve poverty by 2015 and warned that at 5 percent the task would take 150 years. The implication was that the gap could be closed by donor transfers. Between 1960 and 1997, more than $500 billion in aid flowed to Africa, a sum equivalent to four Marshall Plans. Between 1970 and 1998, when those flows were at their peak, poverty on the continent rose from 11 percent to 66 percent.

The assumption was propagated through an interlocking system of summits, pledges, and institutional commitments. Tony Blair established the Commission on Africa, called for $50 billion annually in aid, and made the cause the centerpiece of the 2005 G8 summit at Gleneagles. George W. Bush tripled U.S. aid to Africa to $4.3 billion and launched the Millennium Challenge Account. The United Nations organized a Special Session on Africa in 1985, a $25 billion Special Initiative in 1996, and called on rich countries to commit 0.7 percent of GDP to aid. The World Bank disbursed over $20 billion in structural adjustment loans to 29 African countries between 1981 and 1991. Western donors poured $10 billion into Tanzania's Ujamaa socialist experiment. USAID and international organizations funded irrigation, roads, storage, livestock treatment, and farmer training in Somalia. In South Africa, the ANC extended a pandemic-era unemployment grant to more than six million recipients despite the Finance Minister's objections, and Cyril Ramaphosa described the welfare system as "an investment in the future." Each initiative reinforced the next, creating a self-referencing architecture in which the answer to failed aid was always more aid.

The harm was concrete and measurable. Sub-Saharan Africa's GDP per capita fell at 0.59 percent annually between 1975 and 2000, from $1,770 to $1,479 in purchasing power parity terms. Tanzania's economy contracted at 0.5 percent annually from 1973 to 1988 under the aid-funded Ujamaa program; personal consumption dropped 43 percent and per capita income stood at $290. Chad diverted $4.5 million of its initial $25 million oil bonus to military spending, and military expenditure eventually exceeded health and education spending combined. Nigerian dictators are estimated to have stolen $500 billion in public funds. An estimated $700 to $800 billion in capital flight left the continent, with 40 percent of Africa's private wealth held overseas. Countries like Ghana and Uganda became so dependent on transfers that aid funded more than half their national budgets. In Somalia, food aid timed to arrive during planting seasons diverted labor from farming and suppressed crop output. In South Africa, more than 24 million people drew grants supported by a tax base of just 7.1 million, with unemployment at 32 percent and poverty at 60 percent.
