The raw number had become unfalsifiable in public discourse: any attempt to explain it was treated as an attempt to excuse it.

The policies that followed were extensive and expensive. The Obama administration pushed the Paycheck Fairness Act. Massachusetts banned salary-history inquiries. Minnesota enacted a comparable-worth plan for state employees in 1982, raising wages for female-dominated occupations at a cost of 3.7 percent of payroll. Similar proposals surfaced in New York. Birmingham City Council in the UK lost a landmark Supreme Court case in 2012 that held bonuses for physically demanding male-dominated jobs like bin collection violated equal pay law when not extended to female roles like cooking and cleaning. The council paid out 1.1 billion pounds in claims, faced another 760 million in potential liability, and in September 2023 issued a Section 114 bankruptcy notice. It then cut bin men's pay by up to 8,000 pounds to equalize with lower-paid female jobs, triggering a strike that left 17,000 tons of rubbish in the streets, attracting rats, foxes, cockroaches, maggots and the risk of Weil's disease. Glasgow City Council paid over 50 million pounds in compensation with more tribunals scheduled. Specialist no-win-no-fee lawyers took 10 to 25 percent cuts from women's settlements. Harvard expanded diversity initiatives and bias training. Over one hundred federal lawsuits targeted exclusive DEI programs built on the same logic. The harm was not abstract. Boys absorbed the message that their ambition was privilege; girls learned that systemic barriers would thwart them regardless of effort. Men who dominated dangerous jobs accounting for 92 percent of workplace fatalities, along with higher rates of suicide, incarceration and homelessness, found their disadvantages invisible in a discourse fixated on earnings ratios. Academic inquiry was chilled: Lawrence Summers resigned the Harvard presidency after suggesting at an NBER conference that innate variability might partly explain sex differences in high-end scientific achievement.

Growing evidence suggests the discrimination story was always incomplete and probably wrong as a primary explanation. The CONSAD Research Corporation, commissioned by the U.S. Department of Labor in 2009, found that controlling for occupation, hours, experience and career interruptions explained 65 to 76 percent of the raw gap, leaving only 4.8 to 7.1 percent unexplained. Blau and Kahn's 2016 analysis showed occupation and industry alone accounted for more than half the difference. Payscale's 2020 study found a 2 percent controlled gap. A Harvard study of Massachusetts Bay Transit Authority workers, where pay was set by union contract and managers had no discretion, attributed the residual entirely to women's choices about overtime and shifts. Claudia Goldin's research on "greedy jobs," which earned her recognition as a leading labor economist, demonstrated that nonlinear pay penalties for reduced or inflexible hours explained much of the remaining gap in business, law and finance. The federal government's own General Schedule pay system showed gaps driven by women choosing lower-paid occupations, not by managerial bias. Diana Furchtgott-Roth pointed out that Obama's own White House paid female staffers 84 percent of what males earned. McKinsey attributed 80 percent of the professional gender wage gap to experience differences and career pathways. Parenthood accounted for up to two-thirds of the gap, with mothers reducing hours and fathers pursuing higher pay. The gap shrank from 62.5 percent in 1979 to 82 percent by 2013 as women gained education and entered medicine and law. Jordan Peterson's widely viewed interview exposed the statistic's limitations to a mass audience. An influential minority of economists now argues that the raw ratio measures career and life choices, not employer prejudice.

The assumption has not vanished from political rhetoric, but its empirical foundation has crumbled under the weight of its own data. The 2023 bankruptcy of Birmingham, the bin-men strike, the hundred-plus federal DEI lawsuits, and the quiet revisions at law firms like Morrison and Foerster all mark the practical consequences of building policy on an unadjusted average. Rachel Greszler and John Early at Heritage and Cato warned for years that the gap reflected choices, not discrimination, and that corrective policies would backfire. They were largely ignored. The Department of Labor's own foreword to the CONSAD report noted that the raw gap "continues to be used in misleading ways to advance public policy agendas." That sentence, buried in a government document, may be the most precise epitaph for the assumption. The simple story that women earn less because employers discriminate has lost credibility among an expanding group of researchers, even as it continues to shape laws, corporate programs and public spending. Whether the political class will catch up to the evidence remains an open question.
---

## Trauma Causes Borderline Personality Disorder

*Borderline personality disorder is mostly caused by childhood trauma, especially sexual abuse.*

For the better part of three decades, the dominant explanation for borderline personality disorder was childhood trauma, especially childhood sexual abuse. The reasoning seemed straightforward: patients diagnosed with BPD reported terrible childhoods at rates far exceeding those seen in other psychiatric populations, with studies finding abuse and neglect histories in anywhere from 30 to 90 percent of cases. Psychodynamic theorists like Otto Kernberg described BPD as the product of early relational damage, while John Bowlby's attachment theory explained how disrupted bonds could produce the abandonment fears and emotional volatility that define the diagnosis. The 1989 study by Judith Lewis Herman, J. Christopher Perry, and Bessel van der Kolk, published in the American Journal of Psychiatry, gave the correlation its canonical empirical form, reporting strong associations between BPD and histories of physical abuse, sexual abuse, and witnessing domestic violence. That paper accumulated over 1,265 citations and became the foundation on which an entire clinical paradigm was constructed. Herman went further, introducing the concept of complex PTSD and arguing that BPD was best understood as a trauma-spectrum condition, its symptoms the predictable aftermath of chronic childhood abuse. Van der Kolk's 1994 paper and later bestselling book, "The Body Keeps the Score," added a neurobiological veneer, claiming trauma memories were stored somatically and required body-based therapies for release. The biopsychosocial model, which cast trauma as a necessary environmental trigger acting on biological vulnerability, became the most widely acknowledged account of BPD's origins in textbooks, review articles, and clinical training programs worldwide.

The assumption spread through every available channel. Van der Kolk's book became a cultural phenomenon, praised in terms suggesting it had solved a mystery rather than popularized a contested hypothesis, reaching millions of readers who had no way to evaluate its claims against the behavioral genetics literature. A Google search for "trauma and borderline personality disorder" now returns over nine million results. In academic settings, dozens of longitudinal and cross-sectional studies on adolescents used standardized scales to elaborate the trauma relationship rather than test it, each new paper citing the last as confirmation. Van der Kolk worked the professional training circuit directly, delivering workshops to mental health professionals, educators, policymakers, and law enforcement. The National Child Traumatic Stress Network, a Congressionally mandated initiative he helped create, funded 150 centers dedicated to trauma interventions, institutionalizing the framework at federal scale. Research literature in the 1990s shifted focus from parental loss to childhood sexual abuse specifically, proposing BPD as a trauma-spectrum disorder and generating consistent high-prevalence reports. Psychotherapy research journals published integrations of trauma and psychodynamic models, treating both as legitimate clinical frameworks. The result was a self-reinforcing system: clinical observation confirmed the theory, the theory shaped clinical training, and clinical training shaped what practitioners observed.

The policy consequences were substantial. Mental health services adopted trauma exploration therapies for BPD, prioritizing investigation of childhood history over symptom management. Psychotherapy practices integrated trauma processing with psychodynamic techniques, a combination that lacked the evidence base of symptom-targeted approaches like dialectical behavior therapy, which deliberately avoids historical focus. Clinical guidelines in some settings recommended screening BPD patients for childhood sexual abuse using symptom profiles, institutionalizing the assumption that abuse history was central to understanding the disorder. The movement to destigmatize BPD by reframing sufferers as trauma victims shaped clinical culture in ways that made it harder to discuss genetic and neurobiological contributions without appearing to blame patients. The recovered memory movement, drawing intellectual sustenance from the same trauma framework, produced a documented wave of false accusations of childhood sexual abuse, as therapists used suggestive techniques to recover allegedly repressed memories that turned out to be artifacts of the therapeutic process. For BPD patients who had not experienced significant childhood trauma, trauma-focused therapies generated distress and potentially false memories rather than relief. In adolescents, the trauma focus limited trials of non-serotonergic pharmacological interventions, delaying biologically targeted early intervention during a critical developmental window. BPD's clinical burden remained staggering throughout: approximately 75 percent of patients attempt suicide, 8 to 10 percent complete it, and comorbidity rates with mood, anxiety, and substance use disorders run between 83 and 95.7 percent.

Growing evidence now suggests the causal story was largely a statistical artifact. High-quality genetically informed studies, including twin research and Swedish register data covering more than 11,000 BPD cases, consistently found heritability above 46 percent, with some estimates exceeding 50 percent, and showed that the correlation between trauma history and BPD diagnosis was substantially explained by genetic confounding: the same genes that predispose a person to BPD also increase the likelihood of experiencing traumatic environments. The most direct challenge came in 2022, when Skaug and colleagues published a discordant twin study of 2,808 twin pairs that controlled simultaneously for shared genetics and shared environment and found no evidence that childhood trauma caused BPD traits. The specific parameters that researchers had identified as particularly associated with BPD, including CSA onset between ages 7 and 12 and close-relative perpetrators, represented correlations that had never been adequately controlled for family instability, genetic risk, or the base rates of trauma in clinical populations. Systematic reviews revealed that only about a third of BPD patients reported sexual abuse or severe abuse, a figure far lower than the trauma model's rhetoric implied. The DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for BPD do not require any trauma history, a fact the model's proponents tended to treat as an oversight rather than as evidence against their framework. Randomized controlled trials further exposed the limits of the assumptions undergirding treatment: no psychoactive medication has received regulatory approval for BPD, and psychotherapy consistently outperformed pharmacotherapy for core symptoms.

The debate is not fully resolved. Researchers working within the biopsychosocial framework continue to argue that gene-environment interactions, including specific polymorphisms like FKBP5 that moderate responses to abuse, preserve a meaningful causal role for trauma in at least some cases. An influential minority now contends that what the field identified as a causal relationship was largely the product of failing to account for genetic transmission, and that the confident assertion that BPD is "mostly caused" by childhood trauma is an overstatement the evidence does not support. The field is left with a disorder whose etiology remains genuinely uncertain, a generation of clinical infrastructure built on a causal story now under serious challenge, and the ongoing difficulty of revising institutional commitments that were made when the story seemed settled. The 150 federally funded trauma centers, the training programs, the treatment protocols, and the professional identities built around the trauma model do not dissolve because a twin study says they should.
---

## Blacks Receive Harsher Sentences

*Black and Latino defendants receive harsher sentences than Whites or Asians for most crimes.*

The belief that Black and Latino defendants receive harsher sentences than white or Asian defendants for most crimes became one of the most widely held assumptions in American public life. It rested on statistics that were stark and, at first glance, difficult to argue with. African American adults were reported to be 5.9 times as likely to be incarcerated as whites. Black men comprised 35 percent of the prison population while representing 13 percent of the general public. The United States Sentencing Commission published successive reports in 2010, 2012, and 2017 documenting persistent demographic differences in federal sentence lengths, with its 2017 update finding that Black male offenders received sentences 19.1 percent longer than similarly situated white males. The Harvard Law School Criminal Justice Policy Program submitted a report directly to Massachusetts Chief Justice Ralph D. Gants showing Black and Latino defendants receiving sentences roughly 31 and 25 days longer than similarly situated whites after extensive controls. Michelle Alexander's 2010 book "The New Jim Crow," which argued that mass incarceration had created a racial caste system comparable to Jim Crow segregation, became a surprise bestseller, won the NAACP's best nonfiction award, and was adopted as required reading in law schools and divinity schools across the country. The ACLU carried the argument to the Inter-American Commission on Human Rights. The Sentencing Project brought it to the United Nations. By the mid-2010s, the assumption that the American criminal justice system systematically punished Black defendants more harshly for equivalent conduct was treated not as a contested empirical claim but as established fact.

The statistical foundation, however, was weaker than it appeared. Studies cited to support the bias claim typically reported effect sizes around β=0.06, a figure below the β=0.10 threshold generally considered the minimum for meaningful effect and, in practice, indistinguishable from noise. These effects were flagged as statistically significant, but that significance was an artifact of large sample sizes rather than evidence of a real and consistent pattern. Lower-quality studies and those exhibiting citation bias, selectively citing prior work that supported the hypothesis, produced higher effect sizes, suggesting that researcher expectancy effects were inflating the apparent magnitude of disparities across the literature. The criminological models that generated the bias narrative routinely omitted critical controls. Self-report data from the National Longitudinal Study of Adolescent Health showed that African American males reported committing serious crimes at higher rates than white males, and when this factor was controlled for alongside cognitive ability scores, the racial disparities in arrests and incarcerations attributed to bias disappeared entirely. The raw disparities in sentencing were real; the explanation offered for them was not. Meanwhile, the empirical literature concentrated on arrests, charging decisions, and final sentences while largely overlooking plea bargaining, the mechanism through which over 95 percent of convictions are determined. Carlos Berdejó's analysis of Wisconsin cases found that white defendants were 25 percent more likely to receive charge reductions, a genuine racial asymmetry located at the prosecutorial stage rather than the judicial one. The field had been looking at the wrong part of the process.

The assumption spread through mutually reinforcing institutional channels. Progressive leanings in academic criminology made critical evaluation of the bias narrative professionally hazardous, and negativity bias led researchers to overinterpret trivial effect sizes. The Sentencing Commission's serial reports were cited in federal courts, congressional testimony, and academic papers as authoritative evidence. The Department of Justice's investigation into Ferguson, Missouri, which found revenue-driven policing producing disproportionate Black stops and arrests, was cited as validation of the broader sentencing bias narrative even though its findings were specific to one municipality's policing practices. Social media amplified the narrative during and after the 2014 Ferguson protests, and mainstream news organizations adopted the framing of a racially biased system as background assumption rather than contested claim. Educator advocacy organizations adopted the framework for school policy, with the Center for Black Educator Development distributing toolkits premised on research that later replication efforts found overstated. The assumption became infrastructure: built into sentencing guidelines, reform proposals, diversity training materials, and jury deliberations alike.

The policies produced were sometimes well-targeted and sometimes catastrophically misdirected. The 100-to-1 crack-powder cocaine sentencing disparity established by the 1986 Anti-Drug Abuse Act was a genuine policy anomaly that fell almost entirely on Black defendants, and later pharmacological research confirmed that crack and powder cocaine were the same drug in different forms. Habitual offender laws in Georgia and California were applied through prosecutorial discretion that produced extreme racial skews; 98.4 percent of life sentences under Georgia's two-strikes law went to Black defendants. Cash bail requirements detained poor Black defendants at higher rates, increasing their conviction rates and sentence lengths. But sentencing reform efforts, built on the assumption that judges were the primary drivers of racial gaps, targeted judicial discretion while leaving prosecutorial discretion largely untouched, meaning the stage where racial disparities were most clearly documented received the least policy attention. The Trump administration's First Step Act reduced sentences and enabled early releases based on claims of racist overincarceration, reforms celebrated by advocates including Van Jones at CPAC, but which also released dangerous offenders whose subsequent conduct raised serious public safety concerns. In a Florida murder trial, jurors who believed convicting a young Black defendant of first-degree murder would perpetuate racial injustice negotiated a manslaughter compromise despite evidence of premeditation; the forewoman rejected the verdict in open court, the judge declared a mistrial, and the victim's family was denied closure.

Growing evidence now suggests the general claim was wrong, even if specific disparities were real. A 2023 meta-analysis by Christopher Ferguson and Sven Smith, reviewing 51 studies with 120 effect sizes from 2005 to 2022, found no compelling evidence of race or class bias in sentencing for violent or property crimes. The correlation values for Black-White and Latino-White sentencing differences were r=0.054 and r=0.057, neither clearing any reasonable evidentiary threshold. Only drug crimes showed weak effects, and higher-quality studies showed even less bias. The Add Health data, when properly controlled, fully explained racial disparities in criminal justice processing through self-reported behavior and cognitive ability, eliminating the residual attributed to discrimination. The Fair Sentencing Act of 2010 and the First Step Act of 2018 reduced the crack-powder disparity from 100-to-1 to 18-to-1, implicitly conceding the original ratio had not been pharmacologically justified. But the broader assumption these reforms were taken to validate, that Black defendants received harsher sentences for most crimes, was not supported by the accumulating meta-analytic evidence. The specific was conflated with the general across two decades of advocacy and scholarship, and the harm from that conflation, eroded trust in law enforcement, reduced cooperation with criminal investigations, misallocated reform resources, multigenerational incarceration cycles driven by policies aimed at the wrong mechanisms, fell most heavily on the communities the narrative was constructed to protect.
---

## Flynn Effect Shows Real IQ Gains

*Mean IQ scores rose substantially during the 20th century due to a genuine increase in the capacity for intelligence.*

The assumption was simple and, on its face, well supported by data: mean IQ scores rose substantially during the 20th century, and this rise reflected a genuine increase in the capacity for intelligence. James R. Flynn, a political scientist at the University of Otago, documented the phenomenon in his landmark 1984 paper, showing that American children scored 13.8 IQ points higher on older tests than on newer ones across the period from 1932 to 1978, a rate of roughly three points per decade. Data from 14 nations, including comprehensive Dutch and Belgian military conscription records, confirmed gains of 5 to 25 points per generation. A 2014 meta-analysis of 285 studies estimated the rate at 2.31 points per decade. Ulric Neisser calculated that the average American in 1932 would score around 80 on 1997 norms, placing the median citizen of that era in the borderline range. Richard Herrnstein and Charles Murray coined the term "Flynn effect" in their 1994 book "The Bell Curve," ensuring it reached a mass audience. Richard Nisbett cited it in 2009 as the strongest evidence that the Black-White IQ gap was environmental in origin. The gains were real in the narrow sense that people scored higher on the same instruments. The question was whether this meant they were smarter, and a great many people, including researchers, clinicians, judges, and policymakers, assumed it did.

The assumption spread through academic journals, test publishers' manuals, textbooks, military records, and eventually Flynn's own 2013 TED Talk. Psychology and IQ testing textbooks adopted the term widely after "The Bell Curve" popularized it. Test publishers propagated the underlying premise through their norming practices: technical manuals recorded correlations between old and new versions without prominently flagging that contemporary samples had scored substantially higher on the old instrument. The Flynn Effect was rarely taught in behavioral science training programs, which meant the clinicians most directly affected by norm obsolescence were often the least aware of it. European military conscription data, covering virtually entire male birth cohorts tested on unaltered instruments, gave the effect an empirical solidity that laboratory studies could not match. Scholars in 35 countries independently measured IQ trends, often unaware of parallel work elsewhere, and Flynn assembled their findings into a picture that no single national dataset could have provided. The effect became the most-cited idea in the environmentalist arsenal against hereditarian views on race and IQ, and it reshaped developmental psychology by suggesting that broad environmental changes could powerfully influence cognitive development.

The policies that followed treated rising scores as evidence of rising intelligence, with consequences that ranged from bureaucratic inconvenience to life and death. The Supreme Court's 2002 decision in "Atkins v. Virginia" barred the execution of intellectually disabled defendants, defined in part by an IQ at or below 70. Because older tests systematically overestimated scores at a rate of roughly 0.3 points per year of norm obsolescence, defendants tested on outdated instruments appeared to score above the threshold when their true cognitive ability fell below it. By 2008, more than 80 death sentences had been converted to life imprisonment in Atkins proceedings, with hundreds of additional cases active; the implication was that an unknown number of executions had already been carried out on people who would have qualified as intellectually disabled under current norms. In special education, switching a student from an older to a newer version of the same IQ test produced an average score drop of 5.6 points, enough to push many across the disability threshold. Learning disability determinations, which depended on the gap between IQ and achievement, were similarly destabilized: children gained or lost eligibility for support based on which version of a test happened to be current at the time of their evaluation. Eligibility for Social Security Disability Insurance, ADA accommodations, and military enlistment all depended on IQ cutoffs that the Flynn Effect rendered unreliable.

The harm extended beyond individual misclassification. The assumption that rising scores reflected real intelligence gains fostered the belief that subpopulation IQ gaps would close through environmental improvement alone, misleading policy expectations. It enabled genetic arguments for racial IQ gaps by making the counterargument seem stronger than the evidence warranted: if the Flynn Effect was not a genuine g increase, then citing it to dismiss hereditarian claims was building on sand. It distorted research agendas for decades, directing funding and attention toward explaining nutritional, educational, and abstract-thinking causes of a cognitive revolution that may not have occurred in the sense researchers assumed. Premature causal theories proliferated before anyone had established whether the effect was a real increase in general intelligence, a within-family phenomenon, or largely artifactual.

A substantial body of experts now reject the strong interpretation. The evidence against it accumulated from multiple independent directions. Polygenic scores for educational attainment declined across 20th-century cohorts in European American, British, and Icelandic populations, indicating that the genetic potential for high cognitive performance was falling even as test scores rose. Reaction time studies, which correlate with IQ and reflect basic neural processing speed, showed lengthening times since at least the 1980s, implying cognitive decline of 1.3 to 1.7 IQ points per decade. Brain size data that had seemed supportive proved contaminated by sampling bias: early autopsy studies overrepresented criminals and charity cases, while later MRI studies drew volunteers skewed toward younger, college-educated subjects. Austrian data from Schuhfried GmbH showed score gains coexisting with a weakening positive manifold, the inter-test correlation structure that defines g, a combination flatly inconsistent with genuine general intelligence increases. A sibling fixed-effects reanalysis of the National Longitudinal Survey of Youth data eliminated the apparent Flynn Effect in children's mathematics scores entirely, revealing it as a compositional artifact of smarter, later-reproducing mothers. Flynn himself was among the earliest to doubt the strong interpretation, arguing in 1987 that if people in 1900 had truly scored 30 points below their modern counterparts on abstract reasoning, they would have been functionally incapable of the complex activities they demonstrably performed. He concluded that IQ tests measured a correlate of intelligence useful within a generation, not a ratio-scale instrument suitable for comparing cognitive capacity across historical periods. The scores rose. Whether intelligence did remains, as Flynn recognized early, genuinely unsettled.
---

## Post-Apartheid South Africa Safe for Whites

*White residents and farmers in post-apartheid South Africa would live safely under black majority rule through liberal coexistence.*

The assumption was simple and, in 1994, almost universally held: white South Africans would live safely under black majority rule. The negotiated end of apartheid, the Truth and Reconciliation Commission, and the personal moral authority of Nelson Mandela combined to produce a conviction so powerful that questioning it felt indecent. The willing buyer, willing seller model of land reform promised peaceful redistribution. Broad-Based Black Economic Empowerment legislation promised economic inclusion. The rainbow nation promised coexistence. Alan Paton, the anti-apartheid novelist whose work had helped build international sympathy for black South Africans, had campaigned for exactly this outcome. His widow Anne would later flee the country after watching nine friends murdered in four years. The assumption rested on the belief that the correction of a historic crime would naturally produce peace, and for a brief period the euphoria of 1994 served as its own evidence.

The evidence against the assumption accumulated steadily from the late 1990s onward, though the social cost of acknowledging it remained prohibitively high. Between January 1997 and December 1999, 356 people on farms or smallholdings were killed by intruders. J.M. Coetzee published "Disgrace" in 1999, depicting with clinical precision the pattern of violence against white farmers; the ANC denounced the novel as racist, and Coetzee left the country in 2002. President Mbeki characterized white crime fears as rooted in racism. Julius Malema sang "Shoot the Boer" at public events, a song the Equality Court declared hate speech in 2011; President Zuma sang it again at ANC centenary celebrations in 2012. Academic whiteness studies provided intellectual cover for dismissing white anxiety as colonial nostalgia rather than a response to documented danger. The South African Police Service collected farm attack statistics but applied no specialized investigative framework, took statements without follow-up, maintained minimal rural presence, and classified the attacks as ordinary robberies. The government refused to designate farm murders as a priority crime category, even as it allocated targeted resources to copper theft and rhino poaching. An estimated 1.5 million South Africans, predominantly white, emigrated. Reuters reported a one-third decline in European-descent farmers since 1997.

A substantial body of experts now reject the genocide framing that figures like Donald Trump and Tucker Carlson promoted internationally, but a significant and growing number also reject the official South African position that nothing exceptional was happening. The Institute for Security Studies found that farm murders represented roughly 0.2 percent of all national murders and that patterns were consistent with robbery. Two independent inquiries found no evidence of orchestrated campaigns. These findings were methodologically serious. But they addressed a question that critics argued was the wrong one. The brutality of many attacks, involving torture, livestock killed without apparent motive, and methods including boiling victims alive, went far beyond what robbery required. AfriForum documented 49 farm murders and 296 attacks in 2023 alone. The conviction rate for farm murders was 18 percent from 2016 to 2021, higher than the national 13 percent, which undermined narratives of targeted impunity but did nothing to address the violence itself. The SAPS Crime Information Analysis Centre's own data showed farm attacks rising sharply from 1997, with murder rates within attacks climbing from seven to twelve per month, exceeding aggravated robbery trends. Whether these patterns constituted racial targeting or simply reflected the extreme violence of South African crime generally remained contested. The suffering of the victims was not.

The harm extended well beyond the dead and the emigrated. South Africa's economy contracted under the weight of unemployment so severe that 24 million people depended on welfare funded by 7.1 million taxpayers. Eskom's blackouts, beginning in 2007 and projected to continue for years, imposed enormous costs. Johannesburg experienced building seizures by criminal networks, cholera outbreaks, broken rail systems, and six mayors in four years. The disbanding of commando units in 2003, justified as removing apartheid-era structures, created a security vacuum in rural areas. The Expropriation Act, signed in January 2025, enabled land seizure with nil compensation where deemed just and equitable. Trump's February 2025 executive order cut approximately R8 billion in annual U.S. aid, mostly for HIV and AIDS programs, fast-tracked Afrikaner refugee applications while freezing nearly all other admissions, and stranded approved refugees from Afghanistan and elsewhere. The Episcopal Church ended its 40-year resettlement partnership with the U.S. government rather than participate. The first 59 Afrikaners arrived on a government flight on May 13, 2025. Meanwhile, 101 of the 225 people killed on farms between 2020 and 2024 were black workers, a fact that complicated every narrative on offer.

The assumption has not been definitively refuted so much as it has been overtaken by a reality too complex for any of its partisans to describe honestly. South African officials and organizations like the ISS maintain that farm violence fits general crime patterns and that whites remain statistically less at risk than other racial groups nationally. AfriForum, the Trump administration, and Genocide Watch counter that the isolation, brutality, and political context of farm attacks constitute something the robbery framework cannot explain. Max du Preez, an Afrikaans journalist, and Johan Kotze, head of South Africa's largest agricultural organization, both told U.S. officials they had never witnessed white genocide. Dr. Gregory Stanton of Genocide Watch classified the situation as stage six. A Canadian Federal Court found in 2014 that the Refugee Board had failed to take such evidence seriously. What is no longer seriously contested by anyone paying attention is that the optimism of 1994, the belief that reconciliation would produce safety, that the correction of apartheid would bring peace, that white farmers could live without fear on their land, was at minimum premature and at maximum a fantasy that cost lives on all sides while the people responsible for governing the country argued about what to call it.
---

## Income Inequality Drives Crime

*Higher income inequality leads to more crime by increasing criminal payoffs and reducing opportunity costs.*

The idea that income inequality drives crime rested on a chain of reasoning that seemed, for decades, almost too elegant to question. Gary Becker's rational-choice framework held that wider gaps between rich and poor raised the payoff from theft while lowering its opportunity cost for those at the bottom. Merton's strain theory, dating to 1938, supplied a complementary sociological mechanism: relative deprivation bred anomie, and anomie bred deviance. Isaac Ehrlich formalized the economics in 1973, and by the time Pablo Fajnzylber, Daniel Lederman, and Norman Loayza published their cross-national panel studies in 1998 and 2002, the proposition had acquired the weight of empirical confirmation. Their papers, carried in the Journal of Law and Economics and backed by World Bank affiliations, reported that inequality predicted higher homicide and robbery rates across dozens of countries even after instrumental-variable corrections. The studies accumulated hundreds of citations and became standard references in development economics and criminology alike.

The assumption spread through a familiar academic feedback loop. Cross-sectional studies from the United States, China, and Mexico reported larger effects and attracted more citations, while null or negative findings struggled to reach print. Earlier meta-analyses reinforced the impression of a settled question: Pratt and Cullen reported a coefficient of 0.212, and Kim et al. in 2020 found one as high as 0.436, neither adequately correcting for publication bias. Gallup publicized a global survey of 148,000 people in 142 countries showing that national Gini coefficients correlated with fear of crime, with Venezuela and Norway offered as vivid bookends. The Economist ran a prominent piece declaring a "stark relationship" between inequality and crime, citing Becker and the Gallup data. Daniel Hicks and Joan Hamory Hicks added state-level evidence from the United States, arguing that inequality in visible expenditure (clothing, jewelry, cars, dining out) predicted violent crime over a twenty-year period. The theoretical consensus in economics journals created an expectation of strong positive results, and the publication pipeline obliged. Of 1,341 estimates later catalogued, 43.1 percent were positive and statistically significant, a distribution consistent with selective reporting rather than a robust underlying effect.

Policy followed theory. Criminology and sociology research used the assumption to justify treating inequality reduction as a direct crime-prevention tool, influencing how governments allocated resources between redistribution programs and policing. Educational curricula trained future analysts to treat Gini coefficients as primary drivers of offending. Research practices adopted race-specific arrest rates as proxies for offending, following Michael Hindelang's work, which later evidence showed to be biased. J.R. Blau and P.M. Blau's relative deprivation thesis, arguing that economic inequality between racial groups engenders resentment and crime, shaped both theory and policy discussions for years. When studies failed to find links between interracial inequality and Black crime rates, researchers pivoted to intraracial measures rather than questioning the underlying framework, a move that delayed understanding of how interracial factors actually operated.

The harm was cumulative and largely invisible. Research agendas were distorted for years, with funding and attention directed toward inequality while stronger predictors such as deterrence, labor-market conditions, and institutional culture were sidelined. An estimated 47.7 percent of models in the literature omitted deterrence variables entirely, producing systematically inflated coefficients. Resources flowed to inequality-focused interventions at the expense of policing strategies with stronger evidence bases. Racial crime disparities persisted in part because the fixation on intraracial deprivation obscured the role of interracial comparisons. The European meta-analytic literature eventually found that income inequality accounted for only about 3 percent of variance in crime outcomes, a figure that should have prompted far more caution than it did.

The unraveling began quietly. Eric Neumayer's 2005 reanalysis at the London School of Economics showed that the inequality coefficient in the cross-national robbery and violent-theft data turned insignificant once proper fixed effects and full samples were used; the earlier results had been artifacts of omitted cultural variables. Deutsch, Spiegel, and Templeman at Bar-Ilan University published a model demonstrating that inequality's net effect on crime is theoretically indeterminate, because higher gains from theft are offset by greater punishment risks and lost legitimate opportunities. Jesse Brush showed that the positive association in cross-sectional US county data reversed in time-series analysis, a red flag for any causal claim. A 2006 study using National Incident-Based Reporting System data found that interracial inequality, not intraracial, better predicted certain crime patterns, upending the dominant framework. The most comprehensive blow came from Matteo Pazzona's 2024 meta-analysis, which applied Bayesian model averaging and bias-corrected methods to 43 prior studies and found the true partial correlation between inequality and crime to range from 0.007 to 0.123, a figure that is, in practical terms, close to zero. Growing evidence suggests the relationship was never strong enough to justify the policies, curricula, and research programs built on top of it.
---

## 3-Cueing System Helps Kids Learn to Read

*Children learn to read the same way they acquire spoken language, by guessing words from contextual, semantic, and syntactic cues rather than systematic phonics.*

For more than a generation, schools across the English-speaking world taught children to read by teaching them to guess. The method was called the three-cueing system, sometimes dressed up as "balanced literacy" or "whole language," and it rested on a single foundational error: that learning to read works like learning to talk. Kenneth Goodman, a psychologist, published the idea in 1967. He called reading a "psycholinguistic guessing game" and claimed that proficient readers rely on print as little as possible, anticipating words from meaning, sentence structure, and context rather than decoding letters. Frank Smith went further, arguing that fluent readers treat words as ideograms, unanalyzed wholes, without bothering to process individual letters. The theory felt intuitive. Children do acquire spoken language without formal instruction; it is an evolved capacity wired into the human brain. But writing is not. It is a cultural invention roughly five thousand years old, and no child's brain comes equipped to decipher it automatically. The distinction was obvious to linguists. Forty of them warned in 1996 that Goodman's framework contradicted more than a century of research in their field. Steve Pinker had already laid out the case in The Language Instinct, showing precisely why the analogy between speech and reading fails. The warnings went unheeded.

The assumption spread through the infrastructure of American education with remarkable efficiency. Teacher training programs embedded three-cueing in their coursework. Classroom posters instructed children to look at the pictures, skip unfamiliar words, or try a word that might fit. Commercial curricula built by Fountas and Pinnell, Lucy Calkins, and Reading Recovery carried the model into thousands of schools, generating billions in revenue. An EdWeek survey found that 75 percent of K-2 teachers and 65 percent of education professors used or taught the approach. School districts issued parent booklets directing mothers and fathers to discourage sounding out in favor of guessing from context. In Australia, similar booklets went home with the same advice. The United Kingdom adopted a "searchlights" multi-cueing model as national policy in 1998. Researchers who knew better often kept quiet; grant pressures, journal politics, and professional norms discouraged them from spelling out the classroom implications of their findings. The gulf between cognitive science and teacher preparation widened for decades.

The harm was enormous and measurable. By the 2010s, NAEP reading scores had reached record lows. Two-thirds of American fourth graders failed to reach proficiency. Oregon, after heavy investment in cueing-based programs, posted the worst reading results among all fifty states when adjusted for demographics. Children who had been taught to guess rather than decode became stranded once pictures and predictable sentence patterns disappeared from their books, typically around fourth grade. Recovery after that point proved unlikely for many. Students with poor phonemic awareness, limited vocabularies, learning difficulties, or English as a second language suffered most severely, because guessing reinforced exactly the habits that kept them from reading. Teachers, lacking training in phonemes, graphemes, and morphemes, misdiagnosed the problems and doubled down on the same failed strategies. Billions of dollars went to curricula, interventions, and remediation that produced no measurable improvement. Louisa Moats, the reading researcher who founded the LETRS professional development program, warned for years that balanced literacy meant little to no systematic phonics and simply taught children to behave like poor readers. David Kilpatrick pointed out that three-cueing drew attention away from the very letters children needed to recognize and remember words. Mark Seidenberg documented how teacher training programs ignored the science entirely.

The National Reading Panel report in 2000 should have ended the debate. It identified systematic phonics as one of five essential elements of effective reading instruction and found it superior to context-based guessing for both word reading and comprehension. Eye-movement studies proved that skilled readers process every letter automatically; brain imaging showed that phonics instruction activates left-hemisphere language networks while cueing strategies do not. Castles, Rastle, and Nation confirmed in 2018 that word spelling and pronunciation are recognized before meaning, making grapheme-phoneme mapping far more efficient than prediction from context. Stanovich and colleagues had demonstrated as early as 1984 that better readers rely more on decoding, not less. Yet the commercial and institutional momentum behind cueing kept it alive in classrooms for another two decades.

The reckoning came through journalism rather than journals. Emily Hanford's 2022 podcast Sold a Story traced the history of the malpractice in plain language, documenting how publishers and education schools had sold teachers on methods that cognitive scientists had disproved decades earlier. The series proved enormously influential. Within two years, at least twenty-six states passed laws addressing reading instruction. Arkansas banned three-cueing in 2021. Louisiana's Act 517 followed in 2022, prohibiting the model in all textbooks and materials. Florida, Texas, Ohio, and others enacted similar statutes in 2023. Nebraska revised its education code in 2024 to define evidence-based reading instruction as explicitly excluding the cueing model. The United Kingdom had already reversed course in 2006 with its Primary National Strategy mandating synthetic phonics. The International Dyslexia Association began accrediting teacher preparation programs aligned with reading science, approving twenty within three years. The assumption that children learn to read the way they learn to talk, by guessing, is now recognized as false by the weight of scientific evidence and, belatedly, by the policymakers who allowed it to shape a generation of instruction.
---

## Food Aid Would End Somalia Famine

*Delivering food shipments protected by international peacekeepers will feed Somalia's starving children and resolve the famine.*

The belief that delivering food shipments under armed international protection would feed Somalia's starving children and resolve the famine rested on a premise so morally intuitive that questioning it seemed indecent. Children were dying on television. The United States had the world's most powerful military. The logic appeared to require no further elaboration: send the troops, guard the convoys, feed the hungry. President George H.W. Bush announced the deployment on December 4, 1992, calling it "God's work" at which Americans "cannot fail." The UN Security Council had already authorized the use of "all necessary means" under Resolution 794, the first time Chapter VII authority had been invoked for a purely internal humanitarian crisis without a functioning state's consent. Secretary-General Boutros Boutros-Ghali saw the intervention as a test case for muscular post-Cold War multilateralism, a proof of concept for rebuilding failed states. The Joint Chiefs planned it as a quick in-and-out: protect the convoys, hand off to the UN, come home. None of these plans accounted for the fact that the men with the guns had already decided that controlling food was more profitable than distributing it.

The assumption spread through television footage, UN resolutions, and the public endorsements of the very warlords whose militias were looting the convoys. Mohammed Farah Aidid and his rival Ali Mahdi Mohamed both endorsed the American troop proposal on consecutive days in November 1992, and their cooperation was cited by officials including National Security Adviser Brent Scowcroft as evidence that the mission would succeed. The UN's claim that 80 percent of aid shipments were being looted served as the statistical foundation for escalation, repeated in Security Council chambers and press briefings as established fact. The Pakistani commander on the ground contested the figure. Aid agencies questioned it. Its origins were untraceable. Mohammed Sahnoun, the Algerian diplomat heading UNOSOM I, had negotiated a limited troop agreement with Aidid through patient consultation and warned that bypassing Somali leaders would be read as colonial imposition. He was overruled and replaced by Ismat T. Kittani, who adopted a confrontational posture and pushed the 80 percent claim. Some senior UNOSOM I officials privately believed the famine's scope had been exaggerated to justify Somalia as a laboratory for a new model of conflict resolution. The military brass tipped off reporters to film Navy SEALs wading ashore on a Mogadishu beach, and the footage of armed Americans arriving to feed the hungry ran on every network before the operation had a workable plan.

The policies that followed escalated in a single direction. Operation Provide Relief attempted airlifts in April 1992 and failed when militias hijacked convoys. UNOSOM I deployed 500 Pakistani troops who ended up controlling little beyond the airport. Operation Restore Hope landed approximately 25,000 American troops in December 1992 to protect aid delivery sites. UNITAF transitioned to the UN-commanded UNOSOM II in March 1993, which expanded the mandate to include disarmament and national reconciliation, objectives the force was not equipped to achieve. The famine had already killed an estimated 300,000 to 350,000 Somalis by early 1992, with 4.5 million at risk. Subsequent analysis found the famine had nearly run its course before UNITAF landed; the intervention may have shortened it by approximately one month. Meanwhile, donated food was sold openly in Mogadishu markets, thousands of tons were plundered by warlord militias to retain troop loyalty, and the journalist P.J. O'Rourke observed from the ground that qat arrived in the city without military escort and was distributed efficiently by market mechanisms, while wheat required Marines and still got stolen. The longer-term aid programs, premised on development economics models like the Two-Gap Model and Jeffrey Sachs's Big Push Theory, assumed capital inputs would boost agricultural production. An analysis of time-series data from 1985 to 2022 found the opposite: development food aid negatively affected long-run crop production, partly because it arrived during planting seasons, diverted labor, and suppressed local market incentives.

The harm was comprehensive. The Battle of Mogadishu on October 3 and 4, 1993, killed 18 American soldiers and wounded 84, with Somali dead numbering in the hundreds. Bodies of American soldiers were dragged through the streets and broadcast internationally. Clinton ordered withdrawal within days. The UN pulled out entirely in 1995, leaving Somalia in the same clan warfare it had entered. Canadian peacekeepers tortured and killed a Somali teenager in what became the Somalia Affair; Italian forces faced their own commission of inquiry into civilian abuse. Decades of food aid fostered dependency among Somali farmers, reduced their incentives to invest in production, and suppressed crop output. The World Food Programme paid interclan cartels hundreds of millions annually for transport and tolerated the skimming of 30 to 50 percent of cargo. By 2010, the UN Monitoring Group documented systematic diversion by contractors linked to al-Shabaab. When the Obama administration loosened delivery guidelines and expanded relief to $580 million in 2011, up to half of deliveries were stolen, with American taxpayer dollars effectively financing the insurgency that American troops had been sent to suppress. The same famine emergency recurred thirty years later, a fact documented by reporters who had covered the original crisis. Alex de Waal had warned two decades earlier that food aid in conflict zones creates dependency, paralyzes agriculture, and builds war economies structured around capturing supplies. His warnings were noted in academic journals and ignored in policy offices, which is the customary sequence.

The assumption did not fall apart in a single moment, though the Black Hawk Down footage came close to providing one. It eroded across two decades of accumulating evidence that no one with budget authority wanted to read. The UN Monitoring Group's 2010 report documented the contractor complicity. Associated Press reporting in 2011 confirmed the scale of theft. Research published using data spanning nearly four decades demonstrated that development food aid had a statistically significant negative effect on Somali crop production, the precise opposite of what every theoretical model had predicted. When USAID paused aid to Ethiopia after discovering that the World Food Programme had colluded in the military theft of entire flour mills of donated wheat, diversion dropped sharply, the Ethiopian regime accepted electronic tracking, and nutritional indicators stayed flat, proving that much of the aid had never reached vulnerable populations in the first place. Aid stoppages in Ethiopia and Yemen did not cause the famine spikes that defenders of unconditional funding had predicted, indicating that the aid had been enriching diverters rather than feeding the hungry. The assumption that food aid protected by peacekeepers would end Somalia's famine was not merely wrong. It helped create the conditions, dependency, warlord financing, agricultural suppression, donor fatigue, under which famine became a permanent feature of Somali life rather than an emergency that could be resolved.
---

## Airport Profiling is Racial Discrimination

*Airport security must avoid profiling Arab or Middle Eastern-looking travelers to prevent racial discrimination and disparate impact.*

The belief that airport security must avoid any practice producing disparate impact on Arab or Middle Eastern travelers took root well before September 11, 2001. During an October 2000 presidential debate, George W. Bush denounced the profiling of Arab-Americans as racial discrimination, a remark calculated to win votes in Michigan, home to a large Arab-American population. The response was immediate: Osama Siblani, publisher of an Arab-American newspaper, received thirty-one calls within minutes; Palestinian-born Washington lawyer George Salem helped Bush raise more than a million dollars from Arab-American donors; Khalil Jahshan of the American-Arab Anti-Discrimination Committee called it the first time a presidential candidate had mentioned their community by name. Aides to GOP Senator Spencer Abraham, the Senate's only Arab-American member, had urged Bush to raise the issue. The assumption rested on a simple equation: any statistical disparity in screening proved bias. Civil rights organizations reinforced the logic by equating "Flying While Arab" with "Driving While Black," and the ACLU ran a national Campaign Against Racial Profiling that turned isolated complaints into a narrative of systemic discrimination. Academic research on implicit bias from psychologists Mahzarin Banaji, Brian Nosek, and Anthony Greenwald lent scholarly weight, suggesting unconscious prejudice drove outcomes even when no one intended harm. The framework seemed both morally and scientifically sound.

Once Bush took office, the assumption became federal policy. Transportation Secretary Norman Mineta insisted that screening must remain uniform and could not consider race, ethnicity, appearance, or dress. He joined Energy Secretary Spencer Abraham in producing public service announcements, broadcast to seventy-one million homes, warning against stereotyping Arab and Muslim Americans. The Bush Administration commissioned a study at Detroit's airport to measure disparate impact on Arab passengers. After September 11, the Aviation and Transportation Security Act codified the prohibition, assigning screening to the federal government and banning the use of subjective factors. The Department of Transportation contacted airlines to remind them that profiling violated anti-discrimination laws. James Zogby, president of the Arab American Institute, praised the department for largely eliminating airport profiling. The same disparate-impact logic spread far beyond airports. More than a dozen state legislatures passed laws requiring police to record the race of every traffic stop. The EEOC pursued employers who used criminal background checks or aptitude tests that produced racial imbalances. The 1981 Luevano Consent Decree had already banned federal hiring exams that yielded lower pass rates for Blacks and Hispanics, replacing them with self-rating questionnaires. Schools received guidance warning that racial disparities in discipline could trigger civil-rights investigations. By the time the Biden Administration issued its Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights, the principle that disparity equals discrimination had colonized employment, education, policing, medicine, and technology regulation.

The harm was concrete and, in one case, catastrophic. On the morning of September 11, U.S. Airways ticket agent Michael Tuohey checked in Mohammed Atta despite noting his one-way first-class ticket and his own gut suspicion. He later said he had slapped himself for the politically correct thought that he should not single out the man. Atta boarded the plane and led the deadliest terrorist attack in American history. In January 2002, eighty-six-year-old Joseph J. Foss, a Medal of Honor recipient, former Marine general, and former governor, was detained for forty-five minutes at Phoenix airport security while screeners fixated on his medal. The GAO later found that the TSA's Behavioral Detection Program produced zero terrorists among 150,000 secondary referrals; TSA employees in Boston estimated that eighty percent of those pulled from security lines were minorities. Meanwhile, misinformation about Israeli security practices discouraged adoption of effective behavioral profiling. ABC News anchor Diane Sawyer and correspondent Pierre Thomas claimed Israel openly profiled by race, a claim contradicted by Rafi Ron, former head of security at Ben Gurion Airport, who explained that Israel used behavioral indicators and that prominent Arabs passed through routinely. Beyond aviation, federal agencies hired using self-assessments that the Merit Systems Protection Board found far less predictive of performance than the tests they replaced. Companies built sprawling HR departments to preempt EEOC complaints. De-policing linked to disparity concerns correlated with homicide spikes after 2014, producing thousands of additional deaths disproportionately concentrated in minority neighborhoods.

The assumption began to crack on September 11 itself and has continued to erode since. Tuohey's admission and the Foss incident made the policy look simultaneously dangerous and absurd. The U.S. Customs Service, which had targeted Black women for drug searches based on ethnicity, found that shifting to behavioral indicators raised hit rates substantially, a lesson the aviation sector was slow to absorb. The FBI's expanded homicide data tables showed that interracial crime patterns tracked offending rates rather than policing bias. The transition from the Uniform Crime Reports to the National Incident-Based Reporting System revealed that many Hispanic offenders had been misclassified as White, distorting the disparity statistics on which the entire framework depended. Psychologists Hal Arkes and Philip Tetlock argued that implicit attitudes measured by the IAT were mere associations, not genuine prejudice, undermining one of the assumption's academic pillars. The Supreme Court's ruling in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard cast doubt on race-based classifications more broadly. Edward Blum's American Alliance for Equal Rights filed over a hundred federal lawsuits against exclusive DEI programs, forcing firms like Morrison and Foerster, Gibson Dunn, and Perkins Coie to revise or drop racial criteria. The Trump Administration moved to dissolve the Luevano Consent Decree, arguing it conflicted with current law. The 2014 Dear Colleague letter on school discipline was rescinded in 2018.

Growing evidence suggests the core equation, that statistical disparity proves discrimination, was always too crude for high-stakes security decisions. The base rate of terrorism among Arab travelers was vanishingly low, making ethnic profiling no more effective than random screening, yet the prohibition extended to behavioral methods that happened to flag certain groups more often. The assumption treated every disparity as a civil rights violation and every screening decision as a potential act of bigotry. The cost was measured not only in bureaucratic absurdity and institutional decay but in nearly three thousand lives lost on a single morning when an airline agent chose political correctness over his own judgment. An influential minority of security experts, legal scholars, and statisticians now argues that the framework must be replaced with one that distinguishes between invidious discrimination and rational threat assessment. The broader debate over disparate-impact theory continues, but in aviation security, the assumption's credibility has not recovered from the morning it was tested and failed.
---

## Bank Deregulation Modernizes Finance

*Deregulating banking through laws encouraging mergers and failures would consolidate and strengthen the sector for a modern economy.*

The belief that deregulating American banking would consolidate and strengthen the sector for a modern economy rested on a set of interlocking propositions that, taken individually, each had genuine empirical support. Economists like Charles Calomiris had demonstrated that Depression-era geographic restrictions produced small, undiversified banks vulnerable to local shocks. Randall Kroszner and Raghuram Rajan argued in 1994 that pre-Glass-Steagall banks had avoided underwriting questionable securities because market participants already priced the conflicts. George Benston concluded the same year that all-in-one banking offered substantial benefits and few costs. The Federal Reserve pointed to its own Section 20 affiliates, which had engaged in securities activities through the 1990s without apparent harm to parent banks. Alan Greenspan, the assumption's most consequential champion, synthesized these findings into a simple public argument during his nineteen years as Fed Chairman: the separation of commercial and investment banking was an artifact of a misdiagnosed crisis, modern risk management had rendered the old fears obsolete, and banks would become "dinosaurs" without reform. Beneath all of it lay a deeper conviction that private self-interest generates its own regulation, making government oversight not merely unnecessary but actively harmful.

The assumption spread through reinforcing channels of political power, institutional authority, and academic respectability. The banking lobby aligned with Reagan-Bush anti-government politics, and the financial services sector spent $2.7 billion lobbying Congress in the decade before the 2008 crisis. Regulatory agencies absorbed the deregulatory view as professional doctrine; the Federal Reserve declined to use its authority under the Home Ownership and Equity Protection Act of 1994 to curb predatory mortgage lending, and its regional publications endorsed the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act as creating a competitive banking universe comparable to European universal systems. Greenspan testified before Congress in favor of H.R. 10 in 1997, and the Board of Governors formally endorsed the legislation as a means to improve efficiency while containing risks. Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac used political influence to resist tighter regulation. Brooksley Born, chair of the Commodity Futures Trading Commission, raised formal alarms about unregulated derivatives and was met with a coordinated effort by Greenspan, Robert Rubin, and Lawrence Summers to strip her agency of jurisdiction. The legislative results came in stages: the Riegle-Neal Act of 1994 removed interstate banking barriers, the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act of 1999 repealed Glass-Steagall's separations, and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act of 2000 placed over-the-counter derivatives beyond regulatory reach. Citigroup, formed in 1998 from Citibank and Travelers Group before the law even formally permitted such combinations, became the era's emblematic creation.

The harm was structural and, in its acute phase, catastrophic. The number of U.S. banks declined from roughly fourteen thousand in the post-war era to fewer than four thousand today, with small and regional banks' share of industry assets falling from half to seventeen percent. Control of credit shifted from local institutions with knowledge of local borrowers to distant mega-banks; JPMorgan earned $96 billion in net interest margin in 2025 from government-guaranteed spreads, a figure critics argue reflects rent extraction rather than productive lending. The 2007-2008 financial crisis, which Ben Bernanke described as the worst in global history, was inflated by mortgage origination practices the Fed had declined to regulate, packaged into securities by institutions whose affiliations Gramm-Leach-Bliley had blessed, and distributed through derivatives the Commodity Futures Modernization Act had placed beyond oversight. Millions of foreclosures followed. The costs fell hardest on small businesses and households that depended on community lenders, many of which had already been absorbed or had failed in the consolidation wave.

A substantial body of experts now reject the assumption in its strongest form, though the debate over its precise mechanisms remains contested. The Financial Crisis Inquiry Commission concluded in 2011 that deregulation was among the crisis's causes. The Dodd-Frank Act of 2010 reimposed significant regulatory requirements and created new oversight mechanisms. By 2016, both major party platforms called for reinstating Glass-Steagall, a position that would have been considered eccentric twenty years earlier. Yet a parallel strand of scholarship, including work in the Virginia Law Review, has argued that Gramm-Leach-Bliley and the Commodity Futures Modernization Act largely codified permissions that already existed under regulatory interpretations, and that the crisis's roots lay in supervisory failures and macroeconomic conditions rather than in the specific statutes. Saule Omarova, a Cornell scholar who argued that banks are government franchises carrying public obligations, was nominated as Comptroller of the Currency in 2021 and blocked by the banking lobby, illustrating that the institutional forces behind the deregulatory consensus retained power even after the consensus itself had fractured. What is no longer seriously disputed is that the assumption's proponents were wrong about the risks, and that the institutions whose growth they facilitated proved, in the event, incapable of policing themselves.
---

## America Faces STEM Shortage

*The US faces a massive shortage of STEM workers that justifies expanding the H-1B visa program.*

For more than four decades, American industry, government agencies, and universities promoted the belief that the United States faced a critical shortage of workers in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics, a deficit so severe that it justified large-scale importation of foreign labor through the H-1B visa program. The claim had institutional pedigree. The National Science Foundation published a study in the 1980s predicting a demographic decline in the domestic supply of scientists and engineers and proposing foreign recruitment as the remedy. The National Academies of Sciences released "Rising Above the Gathering Storm" in 2005, a 307-page report whose chair, Norm Augustine, compared the situation to a Category 5 hurricane. The President's Council of Advisors on Science and Technology projected a need for one million additional STEM professionals. Microsoft, the U.S. Chamber of Commerce, the Information Technology Industry Council, and the Partnership for a New American Economy produced reports citing low unemployment rates and unfilled positions as proof that American universities were failing to produce enough talent. Steve Jobs told President Obama in 2011 that Apple could not find 30,000 engineers domestically. Bill Gates and Mark Zuckerberg spent millions lobbying Congress to increase the number of foreign STEM workers. The claim seemed plausible on its face: technology was growing fast, and employers said they could not hire.

The narrative spread through channels that were structurally resistant to correction. The ITAA orchestrated a public relations campaign in the late 1990s that generated a wave of newspaper articles about desperate employers, giving Congress the political cover to raise H-1B quotas in legislation signed by President Clinton in October 1998. Vivek Wadhwa testified before the House Judiciary Committee that visa restrictions were chasing away the world's best engineers and scientists, citing his team's finding that immigrants had founded 52 percent of Silicon Valley startups. Brad Smith of Microsoft cited a 3.4 percent unemployment rate for computer occupations as evidence of shortage, benchmarking it against the national 4 percent full-employment standard. Bipartisan bills proposed automatic green cards for foreign STEM graduates. Senate bill S.744 passed in 2013 with large increases in temporary and permanent STEM workers. Mitt Romney's 2012 campaign proposed raising visa caps. Businesses, universities, and government research agencies, the three groups with the most to gain from expanding the labor supply, dominated every policy discussion. The organizations producing contrary evidence were academic researchers and think tanks without comparable lobbying budgets.

The policies that followed were substantial. The H-1B program imported 71,000 new foreign guest workers for computer occupations in 2020 alone, exceeding the Bureau of Labor Statistics' projected annual demand. Foreign workers on H-1B visas reached 29 percent of the STEM workforce by 2022, roughly double the 2010 figure. Education policy pushed more STEM training at every level, from K-12 reforms to expanded university enrollment, producing 97,047 computer science bachelor's degrees in 2020 against a projected annual need of 56,900 jobs, and 148,120 engineering degrees against a ten-year total demand of 127,700 positions. The Biden administration applied the same shortage logic to trucking, launching a Trucking Action Plan that issued over 876,000 commercial driver's licenses and funded apprenticeship programs, despite evidence that the industry's real problem was retention driven by poor working conditions, not an insufficient supply of credentialed drivers.

The harm fell most directly on the American workers the assumption claimed were in short supply. Research found the H-1B program suppressed wages for American STEM workers by up to 5.1 percent and reduced their employment by up to 10.8 percent. A National Bureau of Economic Research study of 340,000 H-1B hires found employers paid these workers roughly 16 percent less than comparable Americans, saving approximately $100,000 per six-year visa. The visa's structure, which tied workers to their sponsoring employer, created conditions critics described as a form of indenture. Census Bureau data showed 12.1 million STEM degree holders against 5.3 million STEM jobs; 74 percent of STEM bachelor's holders were not employed in STEM occupations. American programmers faced rampant age discrimination beginning around 35. Hewlett-Packard laid off 120,000 STEM workers over a decade. IBM cut its American workforce by 30 percent while quadrupling offshore staff. Real wages for computer and math occupations fell 0.4 percent from 2016 to 2021, and BLS data showed inflation-adjusted STEM hourly compensation dropped 7.1 percent from 2019 to 2023. These are not the wage patterns of a labor market in shortage.

Growing evidence suggests the shortage was, at minimum, seriously overstated, and an influential minority of researchers now argues it was fabricated from the start. The wage data was always the simplest test: in a genuine shortage, employers compete for scarce workers by raising pay. That never happened. Economists Kenneth J. Arrow and William M. Capron noted the "remarkable lack of direct evidence" for shortage claims as early as 1959. Michael Teitelbaum of the Sloan Foundation stated in 2014 that alarms about widespread shortfalls were "inconsistent with nearly all available evidence." Norman Matloff's decades of documentation, Hal Salzman's finding that the nation graduates more than twice as many STEM students as find STEM jobs, Daniel Costa's dismantling of Microsoft's flawed benchmarks, and Kirk Doran's "particularly clean" natural experiment showing H-1B lottery winners hired fewer workers and produced no innovation gains have collectively undermined the intellectual foundation of the claim. Republican-led states including Florida have begun restricting public university H-1B hiring. The BLS itself has warned repeatedly that its employment projections assume market equilibrium and should not be read as shortage forecasts, a warning that was systematically ignored for two decades. Employer groups continue to press for expanded visa access, and the political debate is not settled. But the data has not cooperated with the narrative for a very long time, and the question increasingly is not whether the shortage was real but how a claim so weakly supported managed to drive policy for so many years.
---

## Saturated Fat Causes Heart Disease

*Dietary fat — particularly saturated fat and cholesterol — is the primary dietary driver of cardiovascular disease.*

For the better part of half a century, the governments of the United States and the leading medical organizations told Americans that dietary fat, particularly saturated fat and cholesterol, was the primary dietary cause of heart disease. The belief rested on observational data, most prominently from Ancel Keys' Seven Countries Study, which found that populations eating more saturated fat had higher rates of coronary heart disease. Keys' work produced an equation that predicted blood cholesterol changes from specific fatty acids, and controlled feeding studies appeared to confirm that saturated fat raised LDL cholesterol, a recognized risk factor. Expert panels at the American Heart Association, which had been issuing dietary recommendations since 1961, treated the chain of reasoning as sufficient: saturated fat raises cholesterol, cholesterol causes atherosclerosis, therefore saturated fat causes heart disease. The logic seemed tight. It was not.

The assumption was institutionalized with remarkable speed. In 1977, Senator George McGovern's Select Committee on Nutrition and Human Needs released the Dietary Goals for the United States, the first comprehensive federal statement linking dietary fat to the nation's leading killers. The press conference was deliberately modeled on the Surgeon General's report on smoking. D. M. Hegsted of Harvard served as the committee's chief scientific consultant and framed the American diet rich in saturated fat as clearly associated with ischemic heart disease. The report set quantitative targets: total fat to 30 percent of calories, saturated fat to 10 percent, cholesterol to 300 milligrams per day. Three Republican senators filed a dissent noting inadequate data and a lack of scientific consensus, but their cautions were buried under the committee's confident tone. The USDA and the Department of Health and Human Services adopted the framework in the 1980 Dietary Guidelines for Americans and gave it visual form in the 1992 Food Guide Pyramid, which placed grains at the base and warned against fats with symbolic icons. The AHA reinforced the message through decades of white papers and clinical guidance, tightening its saturated fat limit to below 7 percent of calories by 2006. The assumption shaped school lunches, food labeling, physician counseling, and the diets of millions of people who trusted their government's advice.

The harm proved difficult to quantify but impossible to ignore. Policies promoting polyunsaturated fats, particularly linoleic acid, as replacements for saturated fat sent Americans toward vegetable oils and margarine. The Sydney Diet Heart Study, a trial initially interpreted as supporting linoleic acid for heart patients, had its full data recovered and reanalyzed years later; the results showed higher coronary heart disease mortality and higher all-cause mortality in the group assigned to increase linoleic acid. An updated meta-analysis confirmed that replacing saturated fats with linoleic acid was associated with increased death risk. Meanwhile, the low-fat message encouraged the food industry to produce products high in sugar and refined carbohydrates as substitutes for fat, contributing to a dietary environment that many researchers now believe worsened the very chronic diseases the guidelines were meant to prevent. Americans were consuming 125 pounds of fat and 100 pounds of sugar per person annually even as the guidelines were being issued, and the decades that followed saw obesity and diabetes rates climb rather than fall.

The assumption began to crack from multiple directions. Canadian and British health authorities questioned cholesterol restrictions for the general population. A meta-analysis of prospective cohort studies found no significant evidence that dietary saturated fat was associated with increased risk of coronary heart disease. The Seven Countries Study's limitations became more visible: it was observational, its country selection was debatable, and the neat correlations it reported did not survive more rigorous causal analysis. Controlled feeding studies that had been cited as gold-standard evidence were critiqued for short durations and reliance on surrogate endpoints like LDL rather than actual heart attacks or deaths. Growing evidence suggests that overall dietary patterns matter far more than any single macronutrient, and an influential minority of researchers now argues that the original causal framework was fundamentally flawed. The dietary cholesterol limit of 300 milligrams per day was quietly dropped from the 2015 Dietary Guidelines, an acknowledgment that for most people dietary cholesterol has minimal impact on blood levels.
