Fairfax County replaced Thomas Jefferson's standardized admissions test with a system reserving proportional seats from each middle school, a design intended to raise Black and Hispanic enrollment through socioeconomic proxies. At the federal level, the Luevano Consent Decree, settled by the Carter administration in 1981, banned the Professional Administrative Career Examination and any replacement test producing adverse impact on Black and Hispanic applicants, substituting self-rating questionnaires that the Merit Systems Protection Board later found far less predictive of job performance. The decree bound federal agencies for four decades.

The harm ran in multiple directions. The traditional referral system had genuinely missed high-ability children from disadvantaged backgrounds; that finding is not seriously disputed. But the remedy adopted in most jurisdictions was not to fix the referral process. It was to eliminate or restructure the programs themselves. Critics warned that New York City's phase-out would deny specialized acceleration to students who needed it and drive families toward charter schools. Up to half of gifted students nationally were estimated to underachieve significantly under standard classroom conditions. Profoundly gifted children, roughly 0.13 percent of the population, faced boredom, behavioral mislabeling, inappropriate medication recommendations, and social isolation when their needs went unaddressed. In Chicago, 25 percent of high school dropouts had ranked in the top academic quarter. The Fairfax County policy reduced Asian American enrollment at Thomas Jefferson, prompting a successful Equal Protection challenge. Federal agencies spent decades hiring through self-ratings that failed to predict performance. Culturally and linguistically diverse gifted students faced elevated risks of dropping out and incarceration; 15 percent of incarcerated youth tested in the top 3 percent on IQ scales. The interventions designed to help underrepresented students produced, by the equity movement's own metrics, declining Black participation in gifted programs relative to white participation between 2011 and 2018.

A substantial body of evidence now challenges the assumption's core claims. Owen Thompson's analysis of Civil Rights Data Collection records across nearly all U.S. elementary schools found that gifted programs caused little measurable change in school-level segregation and produced no significant enrollment shifts when programs were initiated or discontinued. The Florida universal screening experiment, the strongest empirical case for the fixable-system argument, contained its own refutation: when the district suspended the program in 2010 due to budget cuts, gifted identification rates returned to their pre-intervention baseline within a year. The Fourth Circuit ruled that Fairfax County's new Thomas Jefferson admissions policy likely violated the Equal Protection Clause by discriminating against Asian Americans. The Supreme Court's 2023 decision in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard ended race-based admissions at universities, and the Trump administration cited it in a 2025 motion to dissolve the Luevano Consent Decree. Anti-DEI legislation passed in nine states. The backlash to de Blasio's phase-out came from within his own coalition: Democratic state senator John Liu called it the elimination of an integral option without proper engagement, and the National Association for Gifted Children's president warned it would not meet gifted learners' needs. Between 50 and 100 percent of gifted identification disparities can be explained by student-level achievement differences, a finding that does not settle the debate but reframes it considerably. The assumption that disparities in gifted enrollment were primarily a product of systemic flaws that districts could fix, rather than a reflection of preparation and ability gaps that preceded school entry, remains contested, but the policies built on it have produced results that even their architects struggle to defend.
---

## Grooming Gangs are a Moral Panic

*The Muslim grooming gang panic is a moral panic alleging that Asian men are sexually abusing young White girls in the United Kingdom.*

For the better part of two decades, a significant body of opinion in British public life held that concerns about organized child sexual exploitation by gangs of Pakistani-heritage men were, at bottom, a moral panic. The term carried a specific implication: that the pattern was manufactured or exaggerated by racist agitators and credulous media, that perpetrators of group-based abuse came from all ethnic backgrounds in rough proportion to the population, and that singling out any one community amounted to dangerous stereotyping. This view had institutional weight behind it. Sue Berelowitz, Deputy Children's Commissioner for England, authored reports asserting that perpetrators came from all ethnic groups and that the model of Pakistani men targeting white girls was merely one among many. Keith Vaz, chairman of the Home Affairs Select Committee, rejected Jack Straw's 2011 warning about a "specific problem" with some Pakistani-heritage men as dangerous stereotyping. Martin Narey, chief executive of Barnardo's, insisted the abuse targeted vulnerable girls regardless of race. A Nottingham Crown Court judge ruled that the race of victims and abusers in a major grooming case was coincidental. Academics Aisha K. Gill and Karen Harrison published peer-reviewed work arguing that British media had constructed South Asian men as "folk devils." Wikipedia maintained a page titled "Grooming gang moral panic in the United Kingdom" into late 2024, asserting no ethnic link to the abuse.

The assumption rested on data that was not merely incomplete but actively prevented from becoming complete. The Home Office's 2020 report cited two prior studies by Berelowitz claiming a white majority among group-based abusers, yet the report's own data showed 75 percent of identified grooming gang perpetrators were Asian. Newspapers selectively quoted the unreliable prior studies and ignored the 75 percent figure; Wikipedia incorporated these misreadings. Ethnicity data was recorded for only 37 percent of suspects nationally. Police forces in Greater Manchester, South Yorkshire, and West Yorkshire possessed local data showing disproportionate Asian involvement but did not compile it into any national picture, and in some cases were instructed not to confirm suspects' ethnicity publicly. The absence of data was then cited as proof that no pattern existed. Meanwhile, those who raised the alarm were punished or ignored. Ann Cryer, MP for Keighley, was approached by mothers of girls aged 12 and 13 being exploited by gangs of Asian men and could not get police or social services to act. Nick Griffin of the BNP warned about the problem in 2001 and was prosecuted for inciting racial hatred. Andrew Norfolk of The Times spent four years investigating before publishing his first piece in 2011. Adele Gladman, a youth worker, produced an unpublished Home Office report in 2002 documenting the exploitation; it was suppressed because senior officers did not believe it. Dominic Cummings later revealed that Whitehall officials had tried to block The Times' reporting at Rotherham Council's request. Simon Danczuk, Labour MP for Rochdale, was warned by senior Labour politicians against discussing perpetrators' ethnicity to avoid losing votes.

The consequences of this institutional consensus were measured in destroyed childhoods. Professor Alexis Jay's 2014 independent inquiry found that at least 1,400 children in Rotherham alone had been sexually exploited between 1997 and 2013, suffering rape by multiple perpetrators, trafficking, beatings, and death threats, with victims as young as 11. The Telford inquiry documented approximately 1,000 girls abused over a similar period. Estimates for England as a whole reach as high as 10,000 victims across dozens of cities. Girls were drugged, gang-raped, passed between men, made pregnant, given abortions, addicted to heroin and crack cocaine, doused in petrol, and threatened with guns. In Telford, 14-year-old Lucy Lowe was killed by her abuser after giving birth to his child; her death was used to threaten other victims into silence. In Rochdale, Victoria Agoglia died after her abuser injected her with heroin; her rape reports had been ignored. Children in care homes were collected by men with the knowledge of staff who checked only their return times. Police dismissed victims as lacking credibility or making "lifestyle choices." Social workers closed cases. Fathers who tried to rescue their daughters were told the town would "erupt" if the abuse became public. The cover-up was not a failure of one institution but a coordinated, if largely unspoken, agreement across police, social services, councils, and central government that the political costs of acknowledging the ethnic pattern outweighed the duty to protect children.

The assumption began to crack under the weight of evidence that could no longer be suppressed. Jay's 2014 Rotherham report, which confirmed the majority of known perpetrators were of Pakistani heritage, gave journalists a document they could safely cite. The council leader resigned. The chief executive departed. Successive inquiries in Rochdale, Telford, and Oldham confirmed the same patterns and the same institutional paralysis driven by fears of racism. Post-2010 operations secured convictions: Operation Clover in Rotherham produced 21 convictions including for rape; Operation Sanctuary in Newcastle convicted 18 defendants with sentences ranging from 4 to 20 years. In 2025, Baroness Casey's rapid national audit laid out the full picture: disproportionate representation of Asian and Pakistani-heritage men among suspects in every area with usable data, 15 years of ignored reports, and a culture in which organizations "avoided the ethnicity topic altogether for fear of appearing racist." Home Secretary Yvette Cooper admitted the failures to Parliament and apologized to victims. Keir Starmer, who as Prime Minister had initially dismissed calls for a national inquiry as "amplifying what the far right is saying," reversed course. Wikipedia deleted its moral panic page. The government accepted all 12 of Casey's recommendations, including mandatory ethnicity data collection and a statutory inquiry with a £65 million budget.

Growing evidence suggests that the moral panic framing was not merely wrong but actively harmful, functioning as an intellectual apparatus that enabled the abuse to continue. The label served to discredit anyone who noticed the pattern, from far-right figures whose motives were genuinely suspect to mainstream politicians, journalists, and social workers whose motives were not. It conflated the identification of a statistical reality with bigotry, and it treated the protection of a community's reputation as more important than the protection of children. The cost was borne almost entirely by vulnerable girls, predominantly white and working-class, whom the system had already failed in every other respect. Whether the full scale of the scandal will ever be established remains uncertain; with ethnicity unrecorded in two-thirds of cases, the national data that would settle the question does not exist, because for decades the people responsible for collecting it decided it was better not to know.
---

## Epigenetics Transmits Ancestral Trauma

*Environmental stresses and traumas are transmitted across multiple human generations via heritable epigenetic modifications.*

The idea that environmental stresses and traumas are transmitted across multiple human generations via heritable epigenetic modifications drew its initial plausibility from genuine science. Epigenetics, a term coined by the British embryologist Conrad Waddington in 1942, describes how a single genome produces the diversity of cell types in a developing organism. DNA methylation and related chemical modifications act as molecular switches, telling liver cells to behave like liver cells and neurons to behave like neurons. The science was real and reproducible. The trouble began when researchers and commentators started treating these intra-organismal switches as messages capable of surviving intact from one generation to the next, carrying the imprint of a grandmother's famine or a great-grandfather's wartime terror into the biology of descendants who had experienced neither. The conceptual leap was enormous. The evidence for it was, from the start, far thinner than the excitement surrounding it.

The studies that seemed to anchor the assumption were, on inspection, considerably less solid than advertised. The Dutch Hunger Winter of 1944-1945 became a canonical reference point: researchers observed elevated metabolic disorders in children born to women pregnant during the famine and inferred heritable epigenetic reprogramming. A 2008 study by Painter et al. claimed effects extending to the grandchildren, but the F2 sample numbered only 1,496, the significant differences appeared only in a catch-all "Other causes" category, and no birthweight effect was detected. A 2013 follow-up on the same cohort reversed course, finding effects only through the paternal line, generating an inconsistent sub-belief while relying on self-reported adult weights. The Overkalix studies, which claimed sex-specific effects linking grandparents' food supply to grandchildren's mortality, rested on 303 probands, with sex-specific combinations chosen after the data were examined rather than before. In rodent research, the MSUS mouse model developed by Isabelle M. Mansuy at the University of Zurich produced striking claims of behavioral and metabolic transmission to the F3 and F4 generations, but Kevin Mitchell of Trinity College Dublin identified the specific statistical practices that generated these appearances: uncorrected multiple comparisons, post-hoc slicing, high within-group variability, and selective reporting. A 2014 Nature Neuroscience paper claiming sperm microRNAs mediate transgenerational trauma inheritance presented no raw behavioral data for its key injection experiments, only summary statistics. The probability of the ten behavioral experiments in the Dias and Ressler paper succeeding as reported was estimated at 0.023. What looked like mechanisms were, on closer examination, noise that had been selectively reported.

The assumption spread with remarkable speed because it combined the authority of molecular biology with the emotional resonance of historical suffering. Discover magazine, TIME, the New York Review of Books, and ABC Science all presented the claims as established or near-established science without noting the methodological weaknesses or the existence of serious dissent. Pop psychotherapists adopted the framework enthusiastically, locating the source of present suffering in a grandparent's unresolved grief. Headlines proclaimed that Darwin was wrong. The idea dovetailed with politically charged discussions about the lasting damage of slavery, colonialism, and racism, giving it a cultural momentum that outpaced its evidentiary basis. Within academia, each publication in a prestigious journal added another layer of apparent legitimacy and generated another round of press coverage. Scientific Reports published a study claiming epigenetic signatures of violence exposure across three generations of Syrian refugees. Environmental Epigenetics validated the MSUS model as "solid." The science hype industry, in which researchers promote exploratory findings as established conclusions, did the rest.

The harm was distributed across several domains. In criminology and public health, researchers linked social adversity to methylation patterns in ways confounded by genetics, poor blood-brain correlations, tiny effect sizes, and inadequately controlled covariates like smoking. Laboratories spent years pursuing epigenetic biomarkers for psychiatric conditions that proved unreplicable. Resources were diverted from approaches with stronger mechanistic foundations. The public was told, repeatedly and by credible-sounding sources, that ancestral trauma leaves molecular marks on descendants' genomes, a claim that shaped how individuals understood their own psychology, how therapists framed their clients' difficulties, and how historical injustices were discussed. Pseudoscientific interpretations latched onto the framework, promoting ideas that environmental toxins cause autism via epigenetics or that genetic destiny can be overridden by lifestyle choices. When claims of this kind are later found to rest on weak evidence, the damage to public trust in science is not easily repaired.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption is falling apart, though the process is gradual rather than sudden. The most fundamental challenge comes from molecular biology itself: mammalian reproduction involves two wholesale erasures of epigenetic marks, one during gamete formation and another during early embryonic development. These resets are not incidental; they are the mechanism by which each new organism begins its developmental program without the accumulated epigenetic history of its parents' somatic cells. A century of Mendelian genetics and the practical success of animal breeding programs provide additional evidence: if environmentally induced epigenetic changes were commonly transmitted across generations, the traits of domesticated animals would fluctuate unpredictably in response to ancestral stresses, which they do not. A review by leading researchers in the field has acknowledged that epigenetic inheritance in mammals is rare, largely limited to transposons, and not demonstrably adaptive. Deeper analyses of the most compelling associations have revealed that many are better explained by shared DNA sequence variants, which are genuinely heritable, than by epigenetic marks, which are not. An influential minority of geneticists and neuroscientists, including Kevin Mitchell, Razib Khan, and Jerry Coyne, have argued for years that the human evidence remains speculative. The assumption has not been formally abandoned; it persists in the literature, in therapeutic practice, and in popular culture. But the intellectual foundation on which it rested has been significantly eroded, and the burden of proof has shifted back to those who claim that what a person's grandmother endured is written, in any meaningful molecular sense, into that person's genome.
---

## High-profile Hate Crime Allegations are Likely True

*High-profile hate crime allegations are presumptively true because systemic racism makes them statistically expected.*

The assumption that high-profile hate crime allegations deserve presumptive belief because systemic racism makes them statistically expected has produced a remarkable string of institutional failures over the past two decades. The logic runs as follows: because racism, homophobia, and related hatreds are real and documented, any specific allegation that fits the pattern should be treated as credible until proven otherwise. This inversion of ordinary evidentiary standards found eager adopters in newsrooms, universities, prosecutors' offices, and the United States Senate. The results have been, with striking regularity, catastrophic for the accused, corrosive to public trust, and ultimately damaging to the very communities the presumption was meant to protect.

The 2006 Duke lacrosse case established the template. A Black woman accused three white lacrosse players of rape at a party, and the allegation landed in a city where town-gown tensions, racial demographics, and class resentment made it feel inevitable. Duke President Richard Brodhead canceled the lacrosse season, accepted the coach's resignation, and suspended a player before any charges were filed. Professors like Mark Anthony Neal framed the case as racialized sexual violence; the Duke Progressive Alliance posted players' mug shots around campus. District Attorney Mike Nifong, facing an election in a 44% Black city, told audiences of 400 at North Carolina Central University that he was convinced a crime occurred and described the alleged offenses as worse than second-degree murder. He said this while state crime lab DNA tests on the accuser's body, fingernails, clothes, and possessions had already failed to match any of the 46 players tested. Players were threatened on the streets near campus. Lawyer Joe Cheshire, representing nearly all of them, predicted the DNA would prove no rape occurred. He was right. Nifong was eventually disbarred. The players' reputations, seasons, and sense of safety were not so easily restored.

The pattern repeated with Jussie Smollett in January 2019. The actor reported that two men shouting racial and homophobic slurs, including "This is MAGA country," beat him, poured bleach on him, and placed a noose around his neck on a Chicago street. The account fit neatly into a moment when FBI data showed hate crimes rising for three consecutive years. Senators Kamala Harris and Cory Booker called it a "modern-day lynching" and invoked it on the Senate floor while introducing the Justice for Victims of Lynching Act, which passed unanimously. Speaker Nancy Pelosi called it "an affront to humanity." President Trump called it "horrible." Actress Ellen Page went on The Late Show to connect it to Trump-era rhetoric. Cook County State's Attorney Kim Foxx, after contact from Tina Tchen, a former Michelle Obama aide relaying the family's concerns, attempted to transfer the case to the FBI and later had her office drop 16 felony counts in exchange for bond forfeiture and community service, despite First Assistant Joe Magats privately believing Smollett was guilty. Chicago's police chief, Eddie Johnson, called the dismissal a disgrace. Mayor Rahm Emanuel called it a whitewash. A special prosecutor, Dan Webb, was appointed; a grand jury re-indicted; and a jury convicted Smollett on five of six counts of disorderly conduct for staging the attack with two brothers who had worked as extras on his show, Empire. The hoax diverted police resources, made it harder for real victims of racist and homophobic violence to be believed, and handed right-wing media a weapon against mainstream journalism that they have not stopped using.

The Covington Catholic High School incident of January 2019 demonstrated how a short video clip could substitute for evidence. A snippet showing student Nick Sandmann standing close to Native American activist Nathan Phillips, who was drumming, went viral. Phillips told reporters the students were "beasts" preying on Black Hebrew Israelites and that he heard chants of "build the wall." The Diocese of Covington issued an immediate condemnation and apology to Phillips. School administrators considered expelling students. NBCUniversal ran 15 newscasts and six articles portraying the boys as a racist mob. CNN pundit Reza Aslan tweeted that Sandmann had "a punchable face"; his colleague Bakari Sellers agreed. Celebrities called for doxxing. Students and their families received death threats. Within 48 hours, nearly two hours of additional footage emerged showing Black Hebrew Israelites hurling slurs like "white crackers" and "incest kids" at the students for an extended period before Phillips walked into the student group uninvited. Greater Cincinnati Investigation Inc., an independent firm retained by the diocese, spent 240 hours interviewing 43 students, 13 chaperones, and other witnesses, and reviewed 50 hours of video. The report found no evidence of racist statements, no "build the wall" chant, and no instigation by students. Bishop Roger Foys wrote to parents that the students were exonerated and their behavior was "laudatory given the bizarre and threatening situation." Sandmann filed defamation suits against CNN and The Washington Post, among others, seeking hundreds of millions in damages.

Growing evidence suggests that the presumption of guilt in high-profile hate crime cases does not protect vulnerable communities but instead undermines them. Each exposed hoax or collapsed narrative, from Duke to Smollett to Covington to the 2022 Duke volleyball allegation against BYU fans (where no evidence of racial slurs was found after a week and a half of investigation), deposits another layer of public skepticism that real victims must then overcome. The pattern is consistent: a claim that fits prevailing assumptions about systemic hatred; immediate amplification by media, politicians, and institutions; punitive action against the accused before investigation; and belated correction that never fully reaches the audience that absorbed the original story. Police departments have historically been reluctant to classify crimes as hate crimes, and FBI data captures fewer than 10,000 incidents annually while estimates suggest 200,000 to 300,000 occur. The gap between real hate violence and the performative certainty surrounding high-profile allegations is where the damage accumulates. An influential minority of scholars and commentators now argues that the presumption of belief, however well-intentioned, has become an obstacle to the credibility it was designed to secure.
---

## Grit is More Important than IQ

*Grit is more important than IQ when you're trying to become successful.*

The assumption that grit matters more than intelligence for success entered popular consciousness through the work of Angela Duckworth, a psychologist at the University of Pennsylvania who defined grit as "trait-level perseverance and passion for long-term goals." Her 2007 study on West Point cadets appeared to show that grit predicted who survived basic training better than cognitive ability. Her 2013 TED talk, viewed over 17 million times, carried the message to a global audience. Her 2016 bestselling book cemented it. The idea had obvious appeal: intelligence feels fixed and unfair, while grit sounds democratic and trainable. Daniel Gilbert endorsed the book on its cover, calling grit the secret of success. The U.S. Department of Education recommended teaching grit in schools. The UK Department for Education announced plans to make England a global leader in character education. Charter school networks like KIPP adopted grit curricula and assessments. The Institute for Educational Sciences called for interventions to bolster students' grit. Academic publications on the topic exploded from 76 in 2008 to 1,650 in 2018. A parallel line of research, led by Nobel laureate economist James Heckman and co-authors in a 2016 PNAS paper, argued more broadly that personality traits predict life outcomes better than IQ, lending further institutional weight to the idea that cognitive ability had been overrated.

A substantial body of experts now reject the core claim. Marcus Credé, a psychologist at Iowa State University, conducted a meta-analysis of 88 studies encompassing 66,808 participants and found that grit correlated only .17 with GPA, compared to .30 to .41 for conscientiousness. More damaging, grit scores correlated between 80 and 98 percent with conscientiousness, a well-established Big Five personality trait studied since the 1930s. Grit was not a new discovery; it was an old personality dimension with a new name. The "perseverance" component did most of the predictive work, while "consistency of interests" predicted almost nothing. Credé stated plainly that there was no empirical support for grit adding to our understanding of success. Duckworth's West Point finding, often cited as proof that high grit made completion 99 percent more likely, turned out to describe a shift from a 95 percent completion rate to 98 percent, a 3 percentage point difference dressed in dramatic odds-ratio language. The study's apparent strength came partly from range restriction: West Point cadets were already selected for high intelligence, so there was little cognitive variance left for IQ to predict. When Zissman and Ganzach tested the comparison on a nationally representative sample from the NLSY97, they found intelligence was 48 to 90 times more predictive of educational outcomes and 13 times more predictive of occupational outcomes than grit.

The Heckman line of research fared little better under scrutiny. Zisman and Ganzach re-analyzed the shared datasets and additional ones, concluding that intelligence was a superior predictor of educational and occupational success. Golsteyn and colleagues identified methodological problems driving the discrepant findings, including differences in measures, populations, test conditions, and outcome availability. Lazar Stankov critiqued both sides for inadequate measurement of intelligence and personality. In several of the datasets, the supposed IQ measure was not a proper intelligence test at all; only one dataset used composites from validated instruments like the Stanford-Binet and Wechsler scales. Achievement tests like the AFQT, used as intelligence proxies in over 50 published papers, were treated by Borghans and colleagues as capturing personality rather than cognition, despite their strong correlations with general cognitive ability. The Big Five personality traits, measured through standard questionnaires, showed weak average correlations with life outcomes (around .23 for education, .20 for pay). Heckman told Bloomberg View that only 1 to 2 percent of income differences tie to IQ, a claim that contradicted decades of meta-analytic evidence from researchers like Gottfredson and Schmidt and Hunter showing IQ as the dominant predictor of job performance and wages.

The harm was diffuse but real. Schools and districts enacted grit curricula and accountability measures, diverting resources from evidence-based approaches like direct instruction and cognitive skill-building. Because grit is largely heritable (a 2016 twin study of 4,642 UK teenagers found perseverance heritable at 37 percent with no shared environment influence and a genetic correlation of .86 with conscientiousness), programs designed to teach it were spending money on something that could not be taught in any meaningful sense. Allan Wigfield of the University of Maryland warned that grit interventions were premature, given the inconsistent factor structure across age groups and populations. A Yale study found that conscientiousness and emotional regulation, not grit per se, predicted school outcomes. The emphasis on grit also functioned, as several critics noted, as a mechanism for blaming individuals for structural failures: if you did not succeed, you lacked grit, not opportunity. Meanwhile, the broader claim that willpower and self-control drive success was undermined by experience-sampling research showing that people with high trait self-control actually use willpower less often, relying instead on habits, routinization, and proactive avoidance of temptation. State self-control interventions produced modest short-term gains but failed over the long term, with participants reverting to baseline.

The grit hypothesis is not entirely dead, but its strongest claims have been substantially discredited. Duckworth herself made partial concessions in response to Credé's meta-analysis, though she continued to defend grit's value. The episode illustrates a recurring pattern: a psychologist identifies a real but modest effect in a restricted sample, packages it with a catchy name, delivers a TED talk, writes a bestseller, and watches as policymakers build programs on findings that cannot survive contact with representative data. The construct was not fraudulent. It was oversold. The difference between a correlation of .17 and a correlation of .50 is the difference between a footnote and a foundation, and grit was always the footnote.
---

## Marshmallow Test Predicts Life Success

*The length of time children delay eating a marshmallow predicts their long-term success in life.*

In the late 1960s, Walter Mischel, a psychologist at Stanford University, placed marshmallows in front of preschoolers at the campus Bing Nursery School and timed how long they could wait for a second treat. The test was conceived as a straightforward probe of self-control strategies. When Mischel and his collaborators, Yuichi Shoda and Philip Peake, followed up with those children years later, they reported that the ones who had waited longer scored higher on the SAT and showed better social adjustment. By 1990, Mischel had published longitudinal results linking short wait times to frustration, poor academic and social skills, drug use, and obesity. The popular press treated these correlations as settled science. What went largely unmentioned was that the original sample consisted of fewer than ninety children drawn almost entirely from Stanford faculty families, a cohort so homogeneous that nearly all of them went on to attend college regardless of how long they had sat with the marshmallow.

The finding spread through every available channel. YouTube videos of children squirming in front of treats accumulated millions of views. Sesame Street produced a segment with Cookie Monster practicing delayed gratification. TED Talks cited the test as proof that character could be cultivated. Introduction to Psychology courses placed it alongside Pavlov's dogs and Milgram's obedience experiments as a foundational demonstration. The Association for Psychological Science honored the research with a 2015 Golden Goose Award. Angela Duckworth of the University of Pennsylvania extended the same intellectual tradition into her concept of grit, promoting self-control as a determinant of success comparable to intelligence. Schools incorporated marshmallow tests and self-control training into curricula. West Point used grit measures to predict cadet retention. Chicago public schools applied similar frameworks to student and teacher selection. Intervention developers created programs targeting disadvantaged children under the explicit premise that building delay of gratification early would alter long-term life trajectories.

The harm was both material and conceptual. Resources were directed toward interventions that, growing evidence suggests, produced only short-lived gains: a meta-analysis of 85 randomized trials found that 40 to 50 percent of cognitive and social-emotional skill interventions showed fadeout, with children returning to baseline after programs ended. The assumption also distorted how disadvantage was understood. Children from lower-income households consistently waited less time, and the standard interpretation was that they lacked self-control. Sociologist Jessica McCrory Calarco and others pointed out a simpler explanation: children from unstable environments, where adults regularly fail to deliver on promises, had rational reasons to eat the marshmallow now. By framing the gap as a character deficit rather than a response to environmental unreliability, the assumption encouraged policymakers to focus on changing children rather than their circumstances. Meanwhile, the overemphasis on willpower as the mechanism of self-regulation sidelined more effective strategies like habit formation and temptation avoidance, distorting research agendas across psychology, economics, and neuroscience for years.

The assumption began to crack in 2018, when Tyler W. Watts of NYU, along with Greg Duncan and Haonan Quan of UC Irvine, published a conceptual replication using 918 children from the National Institute of Child Health and Human Development's nationally representative dataset. Once they controlled for family income, mother's education, and home environment, the predictive link between wait times and later outcomes shrank to statistical insignificance. No link was found to behavioral or personality measures. A 2021 study in the Journal of Economic Behavior and Organization followed Mischel's own Bing Nursery School subjects into their forties, using pre-registered hypotheses and modern statistical methods. Mischel himself was a co-author before his death in 2018, having helped design a study whose hypotheses contradicted the conclusions his earlier work had been used to support. The study found no meaningful associations between childhood marshmallow wait times and adult net worth, educational attainment, health, or debt. A broader self-regulatory competence index did predict outcomes moderately, but adding marshmallow wait times to the model changed nothing.

The deeper unraveling came from experience-sampling research led by Michael Inzlicht and others, which found that people with high trait self-control did not exercise willpower more often; they simply faced fewer temptations, formed stronger habits, and arranged their environments to reduce the need for effortful resistance. This broke the causal logic the marshmallow test had always implied: that the child who waits is practicing a skill that compounds over a lifetime. Longitudinal analyses further showed that dramatic short-term improvements in trait self-control reverted to baseline over seven years, exposing the futility of intervention strategies premised on training momentary willpower into lasting character change. Growing evidence now suggests that the marshmallow test measured not some durable engine of success, but a brief behavioral snapshot shaped overwhelmingly by the socioeconomic conditions the child had already been born into.
---

## COVID-19 Has Natural Origin

*SARS-CoV-2 emerged naturally from wildlife and the lab-leak hypothesis is a baseless conspiracy theory.*

The assumption that SARS-CoV-2 emerged naturally from wildlife, and that any suggestion of a laboratory origin was a baseless conspiracy theory, became the dominant position in mainstream science and media within weeks of the pandemic's onset. It rested on several pillars: early genomic analyses showing the virus's receptor-binding domain differed from computationally predicted optimal sequences, the identification of related bat coronaviruses like RaTG13 with 96.2% genome similarity, the detection of wildlife DNA including raccoon dogs and civets in virus-positive samples at the Huanan Seafood Wholesale Market, and phylodynamic models dating the first spillover to around November 2019 with evidence of two separate zoonotic introductions. Papers in Nature Medicine, Science, and The Lancet framed these findings as overwhelming evidence for natural spillover, and the weight of institutional authority behind them, from the NIH to the WHO to the US National Academies, made dissent professionally hazardous. The Lancet letter of February 2020, signed by 27 scientists, stated flatly that they "strongly condemn conspiracy theories suggesting that COVID-19 does not have a natural origin." It was treated not as an argument but as a verdict.

The verdict was delivered by people with undisclosed conflicts. Anthony Fauci, director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, had overseen millions in funding to EcoHealth Alliance for bat coronavirus research at the Wuhan Institute of Virology, and had written in 2012 that gain-of-function experiments might spark pandemics but were worth the risk. Peter Daszak, who organized and signed the Lancet letter, ran EcoHealth Alliance, which had collaborated with WIV and the University of North Carolina on a 2018 DARPA proposal to engineer furin cleavage sites into bat coronaviruses in BSL-2 laboratories. DARPA rejected the proposal as too dangerous. NIH funded related work anyway. Christian Drosten, Jeremy Farrar of the Wellcome Trust, and other signatories of the Lancet letter lent their considerable reputations to the natural origin consensus. Chinese researchers including Jianguo Xu and Zhenjun Li published reviews emphasizing bat and pangolin coronaviruses as precursors, reinforcing the narrative through state-affiliated institutions. Michael Worobey and colleagues produced geospatial analyses clustering early cases around the Huanan market's live animal section. The scientific literature presented a unified front. Dissenters like Richard Ebright of Rutgers and Alina Chan of the Broad Institute, who pointed to the absence of an intermediate host, the unusual furin cleavage site, and the proximity of the outbreak to the world's foremost bat coronavirus laboratory, were marginalized as conspiracy theorists.

The assumption spread through the most authoritative channels available to modern science: peer-reviewed publication in top journals, institutional endorsements from the WHO and National Academies, and media repetition by journalists who treated the Lancet letter and the "Proximal Origin" paper in Nature Medicine as settled science. Fauci promoted the wet market origin story in congressional testimony and public appearances while denying that NIH had funded gain-of-function research at Wuhan, a denial that Ebright and others directly contradicted. Daszak appeared on the WHO's origins investigation team before recusing himself under pressure. Media outlets, journalists, and pundits wrote off the lab-leak theory as debunked, and social media platforms suppressed discussion of it. The resulting policy framework directed pandemic preparedness resources toward zoonotic surveillance rather than biosafety reform, and the NIH continued funding EcoHealth's Wuhan research even after DARPA's rejection. When the Trump administration moved to terminate the grant in 2020, Fauci questioned the legality of doing so. David Morens, a Fauci adviser, was later found to have used personal email to help Daszak navigate funding issues and evade public records requests.

The harm was compounded by the delay. Several WIV researchers reportedly fell ill with COVID-consistent symptoms in November 2019, before the outbreak was publicly acknowledged. That information, surfaced through US intelligence, was not investigated by the WHO team and was not part of the scientific literature's treatment of origins. By the time congressional investigations began in earnest, the relevant databases at WIV had been taken offline, biological samples were inaccessible, and China had declined to provide the data needed for a definitive inquiry. The window closed. Meanwhile, the pandemic killed more than five million people in its first two years and inflicted trillions in economic damage. The conspiracy label applied to the lab-leak hypothesis created fear and prejudice that jeopardized the international collaboration needed to address the crisis, and the suppression of legitimate scientific inquiry foreclosed policy conversations about gain-of-function oversight that might have reduced the risk of a future incident.

A substantial body of experts now reject the certainty with which natural origin was proclaimed. The US Department of Energy assessed in March 2023, with low confidence, that a laboratory incident was the most likely origin. The FBI reached the same conclusion with moderate confidence. A House panel investigation concluded that COVID-19 most likely came from a lab leak and probed Fauci and other officials on the suppression of the hypothesis. The Lancet quietly added an addendum acknowledging competing interests among its letter's signatories. Ralph Baric, the University of North Carolina virologist who had collaborated with Shi Zhengli on inserting bat virus spike proteins into SARS backbones, admitted he could not rule out a lab escape. No intermediate animal host has ever been identified. No infected animals were found at the Huanan market. The furin cleavage site that distinguishes SARS-CoV-2 from its closest known relatives remains unexplained by any confirmed natural mechanism, despite citations of the RmYN02 bat virus with a different insertion at the same junction. The early genomic intermediates once cited as evidence of separate zoonotic introductions turned out, on closer examination, to be contamination artifacts or low-coverage sequencing errors. What remains is a question that should have been asked openly from the beginning, and a record of institutional behavior that ensured it was not.
---

## Diverse Essential Workers Should be Vaccinated Before Seniors

*Vaccinating essential workers before seniors would best promote equity and save more lives despite data showing higher deaths.*

In late 2020, as the first COVID-19 vaccines reached American hospitals, the CDC's Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices faced a question that should have been straightforward: after healthcare workers and nursing home residents, who gets the shots next? The committee's answer, delivered in November, was that roughly 100 million essential workers should come before most Americans aged 65 and older. The reasoning was that essential workers were younger, more likely to be Black, Hispanic, Native American, or Pacific Islander, and more likely to hold the high-contact, low-wage jobs where the virus spread fastest. Seniors, by contrast, were disproportionately Non-Hispanic White. Prioritizing them, the argument went, would deepen existing inequities. The word "equity" came up repeatedly in committee discussions, functioning less as one consideration among many and more as a trump card. The committee made this choice despite its own internal modeling, which projected that the plan would increase overall deaths by as much as seven percent compared with straightforward age-based prioritization.

The assumption drew support from several directions at once. A compartmental model published in PNAS, developed by Jack Buckner, Michael Springborn, and Gerardo Chowell at UC Davis and Georgia State, divided the population into eight demographic groups by age and essential-worker status, assigned higher contact rates to workers who could not distance, and concluded that vaccinating older essential workers first was optimal for minimizing deaths under certain transmission scenarios. A separate modeling effort from Simon Fraser University by Mulberry, Tupper, Kirwin, McCabe, and Colijn claimed that essential-worker prioritization in a British Columbia-sized population would prevent over 200,000 infections, 600 deaths, and half a billion dollars in costs compared with age-first rollout. The National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine, working at the request of the NIH and CDC, built a framework around the Social Vulnerability Index that incorporated race, poverty, and housing into allocation decisions, proposing that ten percent of doses be reserved for the worst-off quartiles. WHO's Strategic Advisory Group of Experts issued parallel guidance urging countries to reduce "unjust disparities." Harald Schmidt, Parag Pathak, Tayfun Sönmez, and M. Utku Ünver, researchers at the University of Pennsylvania, MIT, and Boston College, argued that prioritizing worse-off groups was urgent, justified, and feasible. The convergence of peer-reviewed models, international guidance, and institutional frameworks created an impression of expert consensus that was, in fact, thinner than it looked.

The assumption spread rapidly through official channels and into real policy. ACIP presented slides at its December 1 and December 11 meetings showing essential workers slotted ahead of the 65-and-older population, sparking immediate debate on Twitter and in policy outlets. Los Angeles County structured its early 2021 rollout accordingly, moving food, agriculture, education, and janitorial workers ahead of many seniors from March 1 through 15, explicitly invoking equity. States across the country drew on the NASEM framework and its Social Vulnerability Index integration, embedding equity metrics into the operational software that governed appointment queues. A widely shared tweet mischaracterized the CDC's rationale as declaring it "unethical" to prioritize the elderly because they were not racially diverse enough; the distortion was inaccurate in its specifics but captured something real about the committee's reasoning. Gregg Gonsalves, a Yale epidemiologist and AIDS activist, defended the initial plan and accused critics of lacking understanding, illustrating the genuine moral conviction behind the position.

The harm was substantial and, in retrospect, predictable. COVID-19 deaths among those 65 and older represented more than 80 percent of total deaths in 2020 and 2021. Infection fatality rates rose exponentially with age; Saad Omer, director of the Yale Institute for Global Health, noted that the death risk for the 65-to-74 cohort was roughly 90 times that of younger groups. The equity rationale required assigning essential workers more than six times the moral weight of the elderly to justify the tradeoff when vaccines did not fully curb transmission. A Los Angeles County surveillance study of over 5,500 adults later found that the marginal increase in vaccination uptake from prioritizing essential workers was statistically similar across racial and ethnic groups, meaning the strategy delivered negligible reductions in disparity while consuming early doses that could have gone to higher-mortality seniors. The plan did not merely fail to achieve its equity goals; it actively produced more deaths among the very minority populations it claimed to protect, since older members of every racial and ethnic group would have benefited more from age-based priority.

The assumption began to crack almost immediately. Marc Lipsitch, a Harvard epidemiologist, co-authored a December 2020 analysis showing that prioritizing the 65-to-74 age group over front-line workers would save more lives and more years of life nationally, in nearly every state, and within each racial and ethnic group. Nate Silver, Matt Yglesias, and Zeynep Tufekci mounted pointed public criticism, arguing that equity language did not justify accepting more deaths. Phase 3 trial data from Pfizer-BioNTech, Moderna, and AstraZeneca revealed stronger-than-expected effects on infection and transmission, undermining the transmission-control rationale that had propped up essential-worker priority and reinforcing the case for protecting those at highest risk of dying. Under pressure, ACIP revised its guidance on December 20, 2020, creating a 1b tier that placed persons 75 and older alongside frontline essential workers and a 1c tier covering ages 65 to 74, quietly elevating age while still gesturing toward occupational and equity factors. Growing evidence now suggests that the original framework was a costly error, one in which a legitimate concern for fairness was allowed to override the data on who was actually dying, with consequences measured not in abstractions but in lives.
---

## Implicit Bias Test Predicts Discrimination

*The Implicit Association Test measures unconscious racial bias that causes discriminatory behavior.*

The Implicit Association Test arrived in 1998 with a seductive promise: by measuring how quickly people sorted Black and white faces paired with positive and negative words, it could detect unconscious racial bias lurking beneath professed egalitarianism. Anthony Greenwald and Mahzarin Banaji, psychologists at the University of Washington and Harvard respectively, developed the instrument and promoted it as a window into prejudice that self-reports could never reach. The test seemed to work. When Michael Inzlicht, then a PhD student at Brown, sat down with the IAT in his advisor's office and found himself slower to pair Black faces with positive words, the experience felt like a revelation. Early claims that 80 to 90 percent of test-takers harbored implicit bias gave the tool an air of universality. Project Implicit, the nonprofit that administered the test online, collected over four million responses. The critical leap, the one that mattered for policy, was the assertion that these reaction-time differences predicted real discriminatory behavior.

A substantial body of experts now reject that claim. The IAT's test-retest reliability averages around r = .50, meaning the same person can score quite differently on consecutive sittings. Its predictive validity for actual microbehaviors is a feeble r = .07. A 2013 meta-analysis by Oswald and colleagues found the IAT provided little insight into who would discriminate. A 2019 meta-analysis by Forscher and colleagues showed that interventions changing IAT scores produced no corresponding changes in behavior. Elizabeth Paluck and colleagues, writing in the 2023 Annual Review of Psychology, concluded the evidence for implicit prejudice reduction techniques was thin and could not justify widespread adoption. Even Anthony Greenwald, the test's co-creator, eventually conceded there was no evidence that implicit bias training worked. Lee Jussim, a psychologist at Rutgers, had been saying for years that the IAT conflated memory associations with unconscious bias and failed to predict anything useful. Hal Arkes and Philip Tetlock argued that implicit attitudes were mere associations, not genuine prejudice, since people never endorsed them. The philosopher Edouard Machery described the entire research program as quicksand requiring a rescue mission. None of this mattered much to the institutions already committed.

The assumption spread with remarkable speed from psychology labs into the bloodstream of American public life. Hillary Clinton discussed implicit bias during a 2016 presidential debate and pledged first-budget funding to address it in policing. Philip Dunwoody, a psychology professor at Juniata College, taught students that implicit bias was a universal feature of cognitive architecture explaining police disparities. Psychology Today articles, peer-reviewed publications in Psychological Science, TED Talks, and books carried the message to millions. HR departments built a multi-million dollar training industry around it, with sessions costing $1,000 to $5,000 each. Universities mandated unconscious bias workshops. The U.S. Air Force ordered an inspector general review of its military justice system for racial inequities after its chief of staff issued a memo denouncing racism. Keir Starmer pledged unconscious bias training for Labour Party staff. The Biden administration's Blueprint for an AI Bill of Rights cited algorithmic discrimination as a threat requiring proactive equity assessments. Schools faced federal investigations under Title VI guidance that treated racial disparities in discipline as presumptive evidence of discrimination. Over 80 percent of four-year colleges went test-optional by 2023, partly on the theory that standardized tests were instruments of bias, despite evidence that the SAT outperforms other predictors of college performance and gives poor students their best chance to demonstrate potential.

The harm was extensive and varied. Anti-bias trainings changed scores on a test that did not predict behavior, diverting institutional resources from measures that might have addressed actual structural problems. Bilal Khan, a diversity facilitator, warned that labeling discrimination as unconscious removed accountability and ignored systemic issues, but his objection was drowned out by the convenience of a twenty-minute online video. Universities enforced ideological conformity under the banner of DEI: MIT rescinded an invitation to geophysicist Dorian Abbot for a major lecture after mobs denounced his criticism of DEI as harmful; the University of Utah denied Nicholas Wolfinger tenure on specious grounds. A 2024 FIRE survey found 14 percent of faculty reported discipline or threats for speech, implying tens of thousands of investigations over a decade. Targets of controversies published 20 percent fewer papers. The de-policing that followed bias-focused consent decrees in cities like Ferguson correlated with homicide spikes, resulting in thousands of additional deaths disproportionately in minority communities. Meanwhile, the FBI's own 2013 homicide data showed that disparities in policing aligned substantially with offending rates rather than pure bias, a fact that disparity-only analyses systematically ignored. A study claiming Black babies were more likely to die under white doctors was debunked when reanalysis showed white doctors simply treated higher-risk low-birth-weight infants. Audit studies purporting to show persistent anti-female hiring bias were undercut by a 2023 meta-analysis finding biases had faded or reversed by 2009. The callback gap in name studies, long cited as proof of pervasive racism, turned out to reflect class perceptions tightly linked to names rather than racial animus alone.

The assumption is not dead, but it is badly wounded. Proponents like Bertram Gawronski have tried to salvage implicit bias by distinguishing the concept from its failed measurement tools, a move that concedes the central problem while trying to preserve the franchise. The replication crisis delivered a broader blow: social psychology replicated at only 25 percent in Many Labs studies, and Brian Nosek, himself a replication expert and IAT proponent, confirmed there was no known replicable social priming finding. Prediction markets run through DARPA's SCORE program estimated average replicability of social science papers at 54 percent, with no improvement from 2009 to 2018 despite widespread awareness of the crisis. The 2014 Title VI guidance on school discipline was rescinded in 2018. Subsequent analyses of Air Force justice data showed disparities stemmed from higher offense rates among minorities, not bias. A counter-petition supporting Dorian Abbot gathered over 13,000 signatures; the University of Chicago's president reaffirmed free speech principles; Abbot was promoted to full professor in 2024. Scott Lilienfeld's 2017 critique identified five unproven core assumptions of microaggression research and called for a moratorium on related trainings. The multi-study finding that pro-female bias was stronger than racial bias on the IAT, and that racial biases varied inconsistently by task and group, did not fit the narrative at all. What remains is a concept searching for a valid instrument, attached to a training industry searching for evidence of its own effectiveness, embedded in institutions that spent years and billions acting on the assumption that reaction times in a computer task could tell you who was a racist.
---

## DEI Drives Business Success

*DEI initiatives improve corporate performance, attract talent, and pose no significant legal or reputational risks.*

The belief that diversity, equity, and inclusion initiatives reliably improve corporate performance, attract superior talent, and carry no meaningful legal or reputational risk became, in the years following George Floyd's death in May 2020, something close to an operating assumption of American corporate governance. The evidence base was thin but widely cited: a Boston Consulting Group survey of 27,000 employees claiming DEI increases profits and employee commitment, Human Rights Campaign rankings that correlated LGBTQ+ inclusion scores with corporate prestige, and a frequently repeated statistic that companies with above-average diversity enjoyed 19 percent higher innovation revenues and 9 percent higher EBIT margins. Kroger's internal allyship guide told employees directly that "diverse and inclusive companies perform better because diversity attracts the right mix of people and inclusion makes them feel comfortable bringing their best selves to work." The evidence was largely anecdotal and survey-based, but in the post-2020 moment, demanding rigorous proof struck many executives as beside the point. A second and more consequential belief underpinned the whole structure: that these programs were legally safe. Law firms like Morrison Foerster and Perkins Coie restricted fellowship eligibility by race and gender. Pfizer ran a fellowship with race-conscious criteria. Salesforce tied executive compensation to numerical hiring quotas. Uber and Citi embedded "anti-racist" language directly into SEC filings. None of these practices had been stress-tested against the possibility that they constituted the very discrimination federal law was designed to prevent.

The assumption spread through competitive conformity. After 2020, the question facing most large companies was not whether to make a DEI commitment but how large and how public. Ken Frazier of Merck and Ginni Rometty of IBM co-founded OneTen, a coalition of 37 companies pledging to hire one million Black Americans without four-year degrees, and the initiative received prominent coverage in NBC News, CBS News, and the New York Times. Salesforce published annual equality updates, proxy statements, and 10-K reports tracking progress toward targets like 40 percent women or non-binary employees and 50 percent increases in Black, Indigenous, Latinx, and multiracial representation, treating the metrics as evidence of sound management. Brands posted black squares on Instagram during Blackout Tuesday, operating on the shared expectation that visible moral alignment would generate consumer goodwill. Law firms multiplied race-restricted fellowship programs without serious legal review. More than three dozen universities, including Columbia, Harvard, Ohio State, and the University of Texas at Austin, implemented identity-segregated graduation ceremonies. UW Medicine created a Workforce Anti-Racism Group for white staff, running sessions on white fragility and intersectionality. Texas A&M sponsored student attendance at a conference restricted to Black, Latinx, and Native American applicants. Each institution's participation made the next institution's abstention harder to justify, and the accumulation of commitments made the entire practice appear both normal and safe.

The resulting policies were extensive and concrete. Salesforce committed more than $100 million to Black-owned businesses and created race- and sex-exclusive affinity groups and training programs. Major corporations including Activision Blizzard, Kellogg, Morgan Stanley, PricewaterhouseCoopers, and McDonald's adopted DEI hiring policies and internal metrics assumed to comply with anti-discrimination law. Kroger required allyship behaviors from employees, promoted pronoun use, supported nonprofits like GLSEN and the Trevor Project, and advocated for the federal Equality Act. Boston City Hall hosted a race-exclusive holiday party for "Electeds of Color," excluding white councilmembers. Gibson Dunn awarded a $50,000 diversity scholarship based on identification with an underrepresented group. The harm was correspondingly specific. White and Asian law students were excluded from fellowship programs that determined career-shaping summer placements. Brands that endorsed BLM experienced measurable declines in follower growth, reduced likes, and increased negative commentary, with backlash amplified when solidarity posts appeared alongside promotions, as a peer-reviewed Marketing Science study of 435 brands and nearly 400,000 posts demonstrated. Companies accumulated legal exposure their boards had not assessed; America First Legal cited jury verdicts in comparable discrimination cases ranging from $25 million to $70 million. Taxpayer funds supported a racially exclusive conference trip at Texas A&M until the governor threatened to fire the university president.

The assumption began to fracture after the Supreme Court's June 2023 ruling in Students for Fair Admissions, which curtailed race-conscious university admissions and established a judicial climate hostile to race-conscious programs of any kind. Edward Blum's American Alliance for Equal Rights filed suits against Morrison Foerster and Perkins Coie; both firms opened their programs to all applicants, and the suits were dismissed as moot. Gibson Dunn revised its scholarship criteria before being sued. Pfizer amended its fellowship to include all racial backgrounds. Uber and Citi quietly deleted anti-racist language from their filings without explanation. Stephen Miller's America First Legal Foundation filed EEOC complaints against more than a dozen major corporations, converting a political argument into an administrative one. Governor Greg Abbott publicly threatened to fire the Texas A&M president over the conference trip, producing a cancellation within days and an admission that the trip violated the spirit of state law despite having passed through normal approval processes. By 2024, Walmart, Lowe's, Ford, and Toyota had dialed back their DEI programs, and among companies reducing commitments, leaders cited the political climate and lack of measurable ROI as the top factors. Florida, Texas, Utah, Alabama, and Iowa banned DEI offices in public universities.

Growing evidence suggests the business case for DEI, as it was actually implemented, was weaker than its proponents claimed and the legal risks were larger than its practitioners acknowledged. The BCG survey, the HRC rankings, and the anecdotal accounts of talent attraction that sustained the assumption were not replicated in controlled studies, and the Marketing Science research on brand endorsements pointed in the opposite direction. Yang Wang and Xueming Luo, the study's authors, had warned that mixing social justice messaging with commercial promotion triggered perceptions of inauthenticity and slacktivism, a finding that brands had never bothered to test before committing. Ibram X. Kendi's Center for Antiracist Research, which had provided much of the intellectual framework for institutional antiracism efforts, itself laid off a disproportionate number of minority staff as it contracted in 2023, an outcome that attracted commentary given its stated mission. An influential minority of legal scholars and organizational researchers now argues that the assumption was flawed in its core performance claims, legally naive in its implementation, and politically miscalculated in its public expression. A substantial number of DEI practitioners maintain that well-designed programs produce genuine benefits. What is no longer seriously contested is that the assumption of zero legal and reputational risk was wrong, and that the programs built on that assumption were constructed without the evidentiary foundation or legal scrutiny that their scale and consequences demanded.
---

## Test-Blind Admissions Promote Equity

*Eliminating standardized testing in college admissions would promote racial equity without harming academic preparation.*

For decades, standardized tests like the SAT and ACT served as the primary gatekeepers of American college admissions. They were imperfect instruments, but they measured something real: a student's readiness for college-level work. Critics had long argued the tests were culturally biased, that they measured privilege rather than potential, and that eliminating them would open doors for underrepresented minorities. The argument gained force after George Floyd's murder in 2020 and the racial reckoning that followed, supercharged by the practical difficulty of administering tests during COVID lockdowns. By 2023, over 80 percent of four-year colleges had gone test-optional or test-blind. The University of California regents went furthest, banning SAT and ACT submission across all UC campuses in November 2021, overruling their own faculty senate's advice. Harvard adopted test-optional admissions through the class of 2030. The logic seemed straightforward: if tests disadvantage minorities, remove the tests and equity follows.

Growing evidence suggests the logic was backwards. A 2023 paper coauthored by Harvard economist Raj Chetty demonstrated that standardized tests actually identify promising students from under-resourced high schools better than GPAs do, which had become unreliable predictors thanks to rampant grade inflation during COVID and the broader erosion of standards after No Child Left Behind. The SAT, for all its flaws, remained the single best predictor of college performance, outperforming high school GPA even for nonwhite students. Removing it did not level the playing field; it tilted the field toward wealthy applicants who could afford private essay coaches and curated extracurriculars. Firms like Empowerly, staffed by former admissions officers from Stanford and the University of Chicago, charged families for polished personal statements, turning the essay into a paywall. First-generation students reported that only 16 percent faced no barriers to standardized testing, compared to 49 percent of their peers, yet the test at least gave poor kids a measurable way to prove their potential. Without it, admissions committees relied on signals that correlated even more tightly with family income.

The consequences materialized with startling speed. At UC San Diego, which had shifted admissions to favor applicants from heavily Hispanic, lower-resourced high schools, acceptances from those schools rose 215 percent while dropping 24 percent from higher-performing ones. By 2025, the share of entering freshmen with math skills below middle-school level had increased nearly thirtyfold, reaching one in eight students. A sample remedial math assessment showed students struggling with problems like 7 + 2 = ? + 6. The UC San Diego faculty senate confirmed in a November 2025 report that the combination of test elimination, grade inflation, and expanded admissions from under-resourced schools had caused measurable academic decline. At Harvard, the math department was forced to create Math MA5, a five-day-a-week remedial course covering basic algebra and geometry for freshmen who lacked foundational quantitative skills. Students in the standard math sequence faced unreasonable challenges because their classmates could not keep up. Writing and language skills declined in parallel. The policy designed to help disadvantaged students was instead setting them up to fail.

The assumption also dovetailed with a broader pattern visible across federal hiring, where the 1981 Luevano consent decree had abolished the superbly validated PACE civil service exam because blacks and Hispanics scored lower on it. For over four decades, the Office of Personnel Management tried and failed to develop a replacement test that was both predictively valid and free of racial disparities, because, as psychometric research consistently showed, valid cognitive measures inherently produce group differences. The parallel to college admissions was precise: the belief that removing a valid measurement tool would produce equity without cost. The Trump Justice Department moved to vacate the Luevano decree, and Harvard reversed course by reinstituting mandatory test scores for the class of 2029, quietly acknowledging that tests predict success better than the alternatives. Congressional hearings exposed how test-blind policies functioned as a mechanism to obscure what amounted to racial preferences in violation of the Supreme Court's 2023 ruling in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard. An influential minority of researchers and policymakers now argues that the entire test-optional movement, far from advancing equity, made admissions more opaque, more coachable, and more favorable to the affluent, while admitting students to institutions where they were least prepared to succeed.
---

## System 1 Thinking Causes Irrational Violence

*Human errors, biases, and violence stem from fast System 1 thinking that can be corrected by deliberate System 2 reflection.*

The assumption that human violence stems primarily from fast, reflexive System 1 thinking, and that it can be corrected by training people to engage slower, more deliberate System 2 reflection, drew its authority from Daniel Kahneman's 2011 bestseller Thinking, Fast and Slow. Kahneman's dual-systems model sorted cognition into two modes: System 1, automatic and intuitive, and System 2, deliberate and analytical. The framework was elegant, backed by a Nobel Prize, and absorbed eagerly by policymakers, academics, and public intellectuals. Jens Ludwig, the University of Chicago economist who directs its Crime Lab, became the most consequential figure in applying this model to gun violence. In his book Unforgiving Places, Ludwig argued that most urban homicides were not instrumental acts committed for money or territory but expressive eruptions of System 1 automaticity: a scowl misread, an insult that lands wrong, a moment when the reflexive brain fires before the reflective one can intervene. He cited FBI data showing only 23 percent of murders were instrumental and Chicago Police Department estimates that arguments drove 70 to 80 percent of homicides. Richard Thaler's presentation of the Gap experiment at a seminar helped set Ludwig on this path, and Malcolm Gladwell's New Yorker review carried the thesis to a mass audience. The intellectual foundation seemed solid. The question was whether it could bear the weight of the policies built on top of it.

The most direct policy expression was the Becoming a Man program, implemented in Chicago Public Schools using cognitive behavioral therapy to train young men to pause before reacting to perceived threats. Early randomized controlled trials, conducted by researchers including Sara Heller and Monica Bhatt, reported violent crime arrest reductions of up to 50 percent. Those results drove Mayor Rahm Emanuel to commit $36 million to mentoring programs, prompted Obama's My Brother's Keeper initiative to fund similar behavioral interventions across 35 states, and helped secure over $100 million in total public investment. The Crime Lab published its findings in the Quarterly Journal of Economics, lending the work the imprimatur of top-tier academic economics. Katie Hill, the Crime Lab's executive director, co-authored a policy piece with Ludwig framing the staggering mortality gap among young men (homicide rates nearly 700 percent higher than young women aged 15 to 20) as a decision-making problem with a behavioral solution. Meanwhile, the older instrumental-violence assumption, that criminals were rational actors weighing costs and benefits, had already generated its own policy architecture: mass incarceration, mandatory minimums, stop-and-frisk, and concealed carry laws. Both frameworks, the liberal cognitive-error model and the conservative rational-deterrence model, shared a common deficiency. Neither could adequately explain why violence concentrated so sharply in specific places, why it fluctuated so rapidly over time, or why neighborhoods with identical poverty levels could differ by a factor of two in murder rates across a single street.

The harm was substantial and compounding. Over $100 million in public funds went to BAM expansions after a second round of randomized controlled trials between 2013 and 2015 found no statistically significant effects as the program scaled to new populations and settings. The money was spent; the violence reduction at scale did not materialize. Mass incarceration, justified by the rational-actor premise, imprisoned millions at enormous public expense while research found that incarceration increased criminal inclination among youth rather than deterring it, and that only certainty of punishment, not severity, showed even a modest effect on crime rates. Concealed carry laws, passed on the theory that armed citizens would deter rational criminals, were associated with a 20 percent rise in violent crime and a 50 percent increase in gun thefts as legally purchased weapons entered the black market. Gun violence, while representing less than 1 percent of crimes, accounted for 70 percent of total crime-related social harm, and each gun murder drove a net population loss of approximately 70 people from a city. In Baltimore, population fell to a century low as residents fled unsafe conditions while city policy under Mayor Brandon Scott remained focused on historical redlining as the explanatory frame, delaying direct responses to the crime and education failures that demographers identified as the actual drivers of departure. The System 1 framework helped identify the problem's scale but generated interventions that, at population level, had not demonstrably reduced it.

The assumption began cracking under the weight of evidence that had been accumulating for decades. As early as 1969, a Houston study found barely one-tenth of homicides occurred during predatory crimes. James Q. Wilson's Thinking About Crime in 1976, Tom Wolfe's Bonfire of the Vanities in 1987, and Jill Leovy's Ghettoside in 2015 all portrayed urban homicide as chaotic, argument-driven, and committed by people who were not running cost-benefit analyses. Ludwig's own block-level analysis showed that identical demographics and poverty levels produced dramatically different violence rates, demolishing the poverty-causes-violence thesis at the granular level where it needed to hold. The 1990s crime drops, occurring across cities with different policing strategies and without significant changes in underlying inequality, further undermined structural explanations. Gerd Gigerenzer, the evolutionary psychologist, had long argued that the System 1/2 binary was a vague dichotomy lumping diverse cognitive processes into two black boxes. Josh Zlatkus pointed out that participants in behavioral economics experiments were not failing to think but reading social cues, mistaking social intelligence for irrationality. Kahneman himself acknowledged that the behavioral priming effects central to his framework "cannot be as large and as robust as my chapter suggested," and independent analysis found that Chapter 4 of Thinking, Fast and Slow, on priming, had a replicability index of 14, indicating essentially no reliability. Meta-analyses like Pratt and Cullen's 2005 review exposed the weak predictive power of rational choice theory, while studies on robbers, sexual arousal, and anger demonstrated that emotions drove criminal decisions in ways neither the rational-actor model nor the System 1 correction model could accommodate.

A substantial body of experts now rejects the clean System 1/2 binary as a sufficient explanation for violence. The framework was not entirely wrong; it correctly identified that most homicides are expressive rather than instrumental, a fact the criminal justice system had been slow to absorb. But it misdiagnosed the mechanism. Labeling a street argument that escalates to murder as a "System 1 error" imports a cognitive vocabulary developed in psychology labs and applies it to situations shaped by neighborhood ecology, social networks, weapon availability, and individual cognitive capacity, factors the dual-systems model was never designed to address. The policy consequences of this mislabeling were real: billions spent on deterrence that did not deter, millions spent on cognitive interventions that did not replicate, and a generation of criminological debate that cycled between poverty, policing, and individual pathology without resolving the core question of why violence concentrates so intensely in specific places and moments. The debate over what should replace the assumption, and what policies should follow, remains genuinely open. What is no longer open is the pretense that a two-box model of cognition, however Nobel-certified, can serve as the operating system for crime policy in a country where a dozen men can be killed in a single Chicago weekend.
---

## BLM Not Responsible for Homicide Spike

*The spike in homicides in 2020 occurred started before George Floyd's death on May 25.*

The belief that America's 2020 homicide surge began before George Floyd's death on May 25 rested on an artifact of data presentation that proved remarkably durable. Annual crime statistics, by their nature, compress twelve months of violence into a single number.
