When the FBI's Uniform Crime Report documented a roughly 30 percent national homicide increase for 2020, the figure appeared as a block, undifferentiated by month or week. This made it possible, at a glance, to argue that the killing had been accelerating since January, driven by pandemic stress, job losses, home isolation, and increased firearm purchases. The New York Times, which reached approximately 11 million subscribers, consistently emphasized these explanations while treating the role of protests and de-policing as secondary or incidental. On May 24, 2025, the fifth anniversary of Floyd's death, the paper published an article by Steven Rich, Tim Arango, and Nicholas Bogel-Burroughs that included a line graph, designed by Daniel Wood, plotting annual homicide totals in a way that placed the vertical marker for May 25, 2020, between the 2020 and 2021 data points. The visual effect was to suggest that the homicide surge had already crested before the racial reckoning began. Early 2020 figures did show rates running slightly above 2019 levels, with some widening in March and April, and this modest pre-Floyd uptick became the evidentiary anchor for the entire assumption. What it obscured was the scale of what followed.

The assumption spread through the ordinary mechanisms of elite consensus. Mainstream outlets covering the FBI report listed pandemic causes and mentioned police distrust as an afterthought. Academic work added apparent rigor: Travis Campbell, an economics professor at Southern Oregon University, published a study claiming Black Lives Matter protests from 2014 to 2019 had reduced police killings by approximately 200, a finding that circulated widely through opinion columns and news coverage. The same study's finding that the protest activity was associated with more than 3,000 additional civilian homicides received considerably less attention. Thomas B. Edsall, a careful and experienced New York Times columnist, wrote about the Campbell study in a way that highlighted the police killing reductions and gave less emphasis to the civilian death toll. The asymmetry was not the result of a coordinated suppression campaign. It was the result of a media environment in which certain findings fit the prevailing story and others did not. Researchers who associated with dissenters faced professional consequences. Steve Sailer, who had been writing about the Ferguson Effect and post-2014 homicide trends for years in Taki's Magazine and on his Substack, published his analysis in venues that elite readers had already decided not to read.

The policies that followed were substantial. Police departments in cities including St. Louis, Baltimore, Chicago, and Milwaukee reduced aggressive enforcement after the 2020 protests, a retreat followed in each case by measurable homicide increases. The "defund the police" movement translated the assumption into budget cuts in Minneapolis and elsewhere. Prison and jail populations fell 14 percent from 2019 to mid-2020. In Baltimore, the response was especially comprehensive: a federal consent decree, repeal of the law enforcement bill of rights, a new Office of Neighborhood Safety and Engagement, a public health violence reduction framework, and State's Attorney Marilyn Mosby's decision to stop prosecuting low-level offenses. The city recorded over 300 homicides in 2020. In Britain, the Sentencing Council proposed a two-tier sentencing system that would account for defendants' ethnicity, on the theory that existing disparities reflected bias rather than differential offending rates; Prime Minister Keir Starmer publicly rejected the proposal. Across the Atlantic, the Lammy review, commissioned by Theresa May, recommended action on perceived ethnic disparities in criminal justice from arrest to sentencing, treating statistical overrepresentation as presumptive evidence of discrimination.

The harm was measured in bodies. The national homicide rate rose approximately 30 percent in 2020, translating to more than 5,000 additional deaths. Black Americans absorbed a disproportionate share: a 34 percent proportional increase yielding roughly 8 additional deaths per 100,000. The Ferguson Effect had already established the pattern between 2014 and 2019, contributing to more than 11,000 excess homicides over the period. Baltimore's murder rate reached levels worse than the crack epidemic. Black traffic fatality rates rose 45 percent from 2014 to 2024; black motor vehicle fatalities rose 36 percent in the June through December 2020 period compared to 9 percent for other groups. Young black male suicide rates rose significantly. Insured property losses from arson, vandalism, and looting across 20 states reached between $1 billion and $2 billion, surpassing every prior civil disorder event in American insurance history, including the 1992 Los Angeles riots. The losses fell disproportionately on small business owners in minority neighborhoods. Peter Moskos, a criminologist, observed that the pandemic explanation collapsed city by city: Baltimore and Newark, both devastated by COVID-19, did not see the same murder spikes as cities where anti-police protests were most intense. Thomas Hargrove of the Murder Accountability Project argued directly that protests and de-policing caused the 30 percent surge. They were largely ignored.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption was an artifact of annual data aggregation rather than a genuine feature of the underlying reality. The CDC's WONDER database, which tracks mortality weekly, showed the surge in black homicide victimizations beginning precisely in the week of May 25, 2020, and not before. On May 26, 2025, Sailer emailed the Times with these weekly mortality graphs. The paper corrected its online chart without issuing a correction notice, updating the article text, or explaining to readers what had changed. A Denver study by David Pyrooz, Justin Nix, and Scott Wolfe found homicides spiked 54 percent after Floyd's death and attributed the increase to de-policing and eroded trust. International comparisons were equally damaging: countries with comparable COVID severity but no large-scale anti-police protests saw no equivalent homicide increases. Counties with higher Republican vote share had lower homicide growth from 2019 to 2020, suggesting that pro-police political cultures curbed the de-policing effect. The practical test came when conditions changed. As Black Lives Matter's organizational influence declined after 2021, police departments gradually resumed active enforcement, and homicide rates fell sharply in 2023. By mid-2025, CDC data showed weekly homicide deaths continuing to decline, with some cities recording lows not seen in decades. The assumption had predicted that policing levels were irrelevant to homicide rates in black communities. A decade of data, examined at the right resolution, suggested the opposite.
---

## Disinformation Research is Apolitical

*Anti-disinformation efforts are an unbiased, politically neutral enterprise concerned with detecting and combatting lies.*

The assumption that anti-disinformation efforts constitute a neutral, apolitical enterprise took hold after 2016 with remarkable speed. Oxford named "post-truth" its word of the year. Collins followed with "fake news." Time ran a cover asking "Is Truth Dead?" The World Economic Forum listed misinformation as a top global threat, ahead of nuclear war. A Pew survey found half of Americans considered fabricated news a problem on par with violent crime. The World Health Organisation declared an "infodemic" during COVID. Governments, foundations, and tech companies poured money into organizations promising to identify and neutralize false information. The premise seemed straightforward: disinformation is a measurable pathology, experts can diagnose it objectively, and institutions can treat it without ideological favoritism. The term itself, borrowed from the Soviet "dezinformatsiya," originally described a political weapon. Its modern inheritors claimed to have scrubbed it clean of politics. A substantial body of evidence now challenges that claim.

The infrastructure that emerged was vast and interlocking. The Global Disinformation Index, founded in 2018 by Clare Melford and Daniel Rogers, built a ratings system that scored websites for "disinformation risk" and sold those scores to ad-tech firms like Microsoft Xandr and Oracle, steering advertising revenue away from flagged outlets. It received funding from the U.S. State Department, the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office, the German Foreign Office, the EU, and the Knight Foundation. The Center for Countering Digital Hate, founded by Morgan McSweeney and led by Imran Ahmed, both former Labour Party operatives, pressured platforms to deplatform figures and demonetize publications, meeting with over sixty congressional offices and lobbying Senator Amy Klobuchar on misinformation regulation. The Aspen Institute convened a Commission on Information Disorder, co-chaired by journalist Katie Couric and featuring Jigsaw director Yasmin Green. The Department of Homeland Security, under Secretary Alejandro Mayorkas, created a Disinformation Governance Board in 2022, appointing Nina Jankowicz as executive director. PolitiFact partnered with Meta to label and demote content. CISA distributed infographics urging citizens to consult fact-checkers before sharing. NBC News employed Brandy Zadrozny as a disinformation reporter; she authored a guide in the Verification Handbook on using paid data tools to identify anonymous social media users, a practice she applied to Trump supporters and QAnon accounts, celebrating on Twitter when Facebook censored groups she had targeted. NPR, under new CEO Katherine Maher, whose 29,400 tweets revealed fluency in phrases like "structural privilege" and "toxic masculinity," suspended senior editor Uri Berliner for five days without pay after he published a critique of the network's ideological drift. Berliner resigned. The pattern was consistent: institutions presented themselves as referees while behaving as participants.

The political asymmetry of these efforts became difficult to ignore. When the Washington Examiner's Gabe Kaminsky published an investigative series in February 2023 examining GDI's ratings, he found that all ten sites rated highest risk were right-leaning, while the lowest-risk sites skewed left. GDI placed UnHerd, the British publication edited by Freddie Sayers, on a dynamic exclusion list, citing "anti-LGBTQI+ narratives" for publishing gender-critical feminist Kathleen Stock, whose views polls showed were held by a majority of the British public. UnHerd's advertising revenue dropped significantly. The Daily Wire and The Federalist sued, alleging State Department-funded censorship. CCDH pressured Google into demonetizing Zero Hedge and The Federalist over their coverage critical of Black Lives Matter, pushed Twitter to ban the "Disinformation Dozen" including Robert F. Kennedy Jr. over COVID content, and advocated deplatforming Katie Hopkins and David Icke. Leaked documents later revealed an internal CCDH strategy titled "Kill Musk's Twitter." The organization's own lawyers questioned whether its congressional lobbying complied with its 501(c)(3) tax-exempt status. George Soros's Open Society Foundations provided $250,000 in funding, a fact CCDH's non-disclosure of donors had obscured. Nate Silver, hardly a conservative firebrand, criticized what he called the "Misinformation Industrial Complex" for systematically ignoring left-leaning misinformation. A large-scale LLM analysis of 600,000 social science abstracts found 90 percent exhibited left-leaning framing from 1960 to 2024, with leftward intensification after 1990, undermining the premise that the academic disciplines feeding these efforts operated from neutral ground.

The harms ran in multiple directions. Conservative media outlets lost advertising revenue. Researchers who published on taboo topics faced professional destruction: Bryan Pesta, a tenured intelligence researcher at Cleveland State University, was fired after publishing a peer-reviewed paper on ancestry and cognitive ability using standard methods and legitimately obtained NIH data, with the complaint against him targeting his topic rather than his methodology. Nathan Cofnas, a philosopher of biology, endured five petition campaigns, student protests, and demands for his arrest in Belgium for publishing on hereditarian research. Doxing by journalists like Zadrozny led to harassment and threats against anonymous social media users. The Disinformation Governance Board lasted three weeks before collapsing under criticism, but not before Jankowicz, eight months pregnant, received death threats and was subjected to a campaign by Fox News hosts Tucker Carlson, Sean Hannity, and Laura Ingraham that included demonstrable falsehoods (Fox later paid $787 million to settle a related defamation case involving Dominion Voting Systems, and Carlson was fired). Canada's Online Harms Act, introduced by Justice Minister Arif Virani with support from Meta, amended the Criminal Code to increase hate speech sentences and created new regulatory bodies, prompting Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre to warn it would chill free expression. PolitiFact's inaccurate coverage of the Kenosha events involving Kyle Rittenhouse, rating Trump's factual description of video evidence as false, illustrated how fact-checking under political pressure could itself become a vector of misinformation. The cumulative effect was a system that punished dissent from progressive orthodoxy while claiming to defend truth itself.

The edifice began cracking from several points at once. Elon Musk's release of the Twitter Files in 2022 revealed internal communications showing coordination between intelligence agencies and platform content moderation teams. The U.S. Congress passed a 2023 law banning the Pentagon from funding GDI. The UK's FCDO ceased its funding the same year after MPs including Kemi Badenoch raised bias concerns. The National Endowment for Democracy dropped GDI. In December 2025, the State Department sanctioned Clare Melford's visa, citing her role in coercing platforms to censor opposing viewpoints. Musk's 2023 lawsuit against CCDH, alleging falsified reports and unauthorized data scraping, combined with the 2024 leak of the "Kill Musk's Twitter" documents and an America First Legal FARA complaint, exposed the organization's partisan architecture. Meta's 2025 decision to end its U.S. fact-checking partnerships, including with PolitiFact, in favor of a Community Notes model, signaled that even the companies that had built the moderation apparatus recognized its unsustainability. Mike Benz, executive director of the Foundation for Freedom Online, documented the pipeline from DARPA-funded tech origins through post-2016 federal pressure on platforms, reframing what had been sold as corporate responsibility as government-directed censorship laundered through NGO intermediaries. A systematic 2018-2021 analysis of all PolitiFact checks on Congress members, controlling for partisanship, prominence, media coverage, and content quality, found no evidence that Republicans were checked more often than Democrats, but this careful work arrived too late to undo the damage of a system that had already been weaponized far beyond fact-checking. The assumption of neutrality did not merely prove wrong; it provided cover for a political project that could not have survived honest description.
---

## Anti-Police Activism Cuts Homicides

*Reducing police presence through anti-police activism will lower violence and homicide rates in Black communities.*

The assumption that reducing police presence through anti-police activism would lower violence and homicide rates in Black communities rested on a syllogism that seemed intuitive to its proponents: police interactions with Black Americans were disproportionately violent, therefore fewer interactions would mean less violence. The belief drew its emotional force from high-profile incidents, above all the deaths of Michael Brown in Ferguson in 2014 and George Floyd in Minneapolis in 2020, and its institutional force from Black Lives Matter, which sent 127 million emails and drove 1.2 million actions in the weeks after Floyd's death. The movement's central demand, to defund or abolish police departments, was adopted with remarkable speed by city councils in Seattle, Minneapolis, and elsewhere, and endorsed in softer form by mainstream media outlets that declared a "racial reckoning" and framed reduced policing as a corrective to systemic racism. What the assumption ignored, and what growing evidence now suggests was always the more important variable, was the role of police presence in interrupting the cycle of retaliatory violence that drives most homicides in high-crime neighborhoods. Urban anthropologist Elijah Anderson had warned for years that street bravado culture stems precisely from the lack of faith in police protection, making communities vulnerable to self-help violence. The assumption inverted his diagnosis: it treated the disease as the cure.

The policies that followed were swift and consequential. Police departments in Chicago, Baltimore, St. Louis, and Milwaukee reduced proactive enforcement after BLM protests, pulling back from traffic stops, street stops, and arrests. The pullback was rarely announced as formal policy; it emerged from the rational calculations of individual officers who understood that aggressive enforcement now carried career-ending risk. In Seattle, the city council cut $3.5 million from the police budget in 2020, eliminated specialized units, then slashed an additional $10 million and 100 officer positions in 2021; a COVID vaccine mandate cost another 100 officers, bringing total losses to 350. In Minneapolis, Mayor Jacob Frey ordered the abandonment of the Third Precinct during protests, and a council majority publicly supported dismantling the department. In Baltimore, State's Attorney Marilyn Mosby stopped prosecuting low-level offenses, the city repealed the law enforcement bill of rights, and a public health model replaced traditional enforcement as the framework for violence reduction. Prison and jail populations nationally dropped 14 percent from 2019 to mid-2020. School districts from Alexandria, Virginia, to cities across the country removed school resource officers. The George Floyd Justice in Policing Act passed the House, encoding the assumption into proposed federal law, though it stalled in the Senate.

The harm was measured in bodies. US homicides rose 41.9 percent from 2014 to 2023 per FBI data, against population growth of 5 percent. The 2020 spike alone added an estimated 5,000 extra murders, with African Americans comprising 53 percent of victims. CDC weekly data showed the surge beginning precisely in the last week of May 2020, the week of Floyd's death, not during the pandemic's onset months earlier. Baltimore's homicide rate rose 63 percent in the year after the Freddie Gray riots, worse than the crack era, with nonfatal shootings up 72 percent. More than 1,500 people were killed in Baltimore in the five years after 2015, more than half those cases unsolved. The clearance rate for Black homicide victims in Baltimore in 2016 was 11 percent, compared to 35.7 percent for white victims. Black traffic fatality rates rose 45 percent from 2014 to 2024. Black motor vehicle fatalities rose 36 percent from June through December 2020, compared to 9 percent for other groups, tracking directly with reduced traffic enforcement. The national murder clearance rate fell from over 80 percent in 1965 to 54 percent by 2021. Travis Campbell, an economics professor whose study was cited by proponents, found that BLM protests from 2014 to 2019 reduced police homicides by roughly 200 but increased civilian homicides by over 3,000. Thomas Edsall of the New York Times highlighted the first number in a column; the second received considerably less attention.

The assumption began to collapse under the weight of its own data. CDC WONDER weekly mortality figures showed homicide deaths declining through mid-2025 as anti-police activism lost political salience and normal policing resumed. Counties with higher Republican vote share, proxies for pro-police political cultures, had lower homicide growth in 2019 to 2020. A Denver study by David Pyrooz, Justin Nix, and Scott Wolfe documented a 54 percent homicide spike post-Floyd amid a 25 percent violent crime rise, naming depolicing, eroded trust, and delegitimacy as causes. The pandemic explanation, promoted by outlets including the Atlantic and the New York Times, was undercut by the fact that murders did not rise in cities hardest hit by COVID, such as Baltimore and Newark, and that the timing of the spike matched protests rather than lockdowns or mobility changes. Steve Sailer had warned as early as 2019 about Baltimore's Ferguson Effect murder spike; Peter Moskos noted the pandemic theory's inconsistencies; Thomas Hargrove of the Murder Accountability Project traced the clearance rate collapse to post-Floyd mistrust. A majority of Black respondents, 52 percent, opposed defund efforts, with only 18 percent strongly supporting them. Carmen Best, Seattle's first Black female police chief, resigned hours after the council vote, calling the position destined to fail. She later observed that what people actually want is for someone to answer when they call 911.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption was not merely wrong but inverted: the communities that most needed effective policing were the ones from which it was most aggressively withdrawn. The historical pattern is older than BLM. Raymond Fosdick documented elite indifference to Black-on-Black violence in 1920. James Baldwin wrote of the alienation produced by a state that neither protected nor respected Black citizens. Martin Luther King Jr., in 1957 sermons now largely memory-holed by contemporary academe, denounced criminality, violence, and poor habits within African-American communities alongside his demands for justice. The post-2014 experiment in reduced policing did not create a new problem; it intensified a very old one. The code of the streets, as Anderson described it, arises from the absence of reliable state order, not from its presence. When police pulled back, the vacuum was filled not by community healing but by the ancient logic of self-help violence: if no one else will protect you, you must protect yourself, and the surest way to do that is to establish a reputation for being dangerous. The result was thousands of additional dead, overwhelmingly Black, in the name of a movement that claimed to value Black lives.
---

## Microaggressions Cause Mental Health Harm

*Microaggressions cause harm and have negative impacts on health and mental health, as inferred from correlations in nonexperimental studies.*

The assumption was straightforward: subtle, everyday slights directed at minorities, termed "microaggressions," cause measurable harm to mental and physical health. The concept originated with Chester Pierce in 1970 and was revived and popularized by Derald Wing Sue of Columbia University, whose influential 2007 paper defined microaggressions as "brief and commonplace daily verbal, behavioral, or environmental indignities" that communicate hostility toward marginalized groups. The idea seemed credible on its face. Minorities reported unpleasant interactions; researchers correlated those reports with depression, anxiety, and other outcomes; and a large meta-analysis of 293 studies published in PLoS One reported associations between perceived racism and poorer mental health (r = -.23) and physical health (r = -.09). The trouble was that virtually all of this evidence came from nonexperimental, cross-sectional studies. Inferring causation from such correlations is a mistake covered in undergraduate statistics textbooks, yet peer reviewers and journal editors let it pass, paper after paper, for decades.

The research infrastructure that grew around the concept was impressive in scale and flimsy in substance. Kevin Nadal developed a widely used microaggression questionnaire in 2011 that measured only subjective perceptions of experiences, never verifying whether any microaggressive behavior actually occurred or whether any perpetrator harbored racist intent. Susan Torres-Harding and colleagues at Roosevelt University created the Racial Microaggressions Scale (RMAS), whose 32 items included statements like "I attend a school where most of the students and faculty are of a different race," which measures minority status, not exposure to hostility. Kanter and colleagues in 2017 attempted to link microaggression scales to racist beliefs and found a correlation of .4, but the study relied on hypothetical scenarios, a small unrepresentative sample, and non-significant correlations on most individual items. Monnica Williams, a clinical psychologist and senior author on key papers, published reviews claiming microaggressions cause harm. When Scott Lilienfeld published a rigorous 2017 critique in Perspectives on Psychological Science identifying five unproven core assumptions of the research program, Williams responded by calling his critique racist. This was not an aberration. The field had developed a circular defense mechanism: skepticism about whether a given interaction constituted a microaggression was itself classified as a microaggression ("invalidation" or "gaslighting"), rendering the framework unfalsifiable.

The assumption spread rapidly through academia, professional organizations, and media. Google Trends data show microaggression headlines rising steeply from 2012, concentrated on university campuses. The American Psychological Association adopted resolutions, established committees, sponsored conferences, and dedicated convention programming to combating racism as a psychological harm, all built on the correlational evidence base. Campuses created microaggression reporting systems and blogs; Oberlin College's version turned a minor scheduling email using the word "futbol" during Latino Heritage Month into mutual public accusations of racism and tokenism. Colleges distributed advisories listing phrases to avoid. Diversity trainings proliferated in clinical, counseling, and corporate settings, teaching participants to detect and remediate microaggressions as established science. The New York Times published multiple articles amplifying microaggression complaints from elite professionals, including a concertmaster in the San Francisco Symphony earning over $170,000 who described feeling "like cattle," and a young composer with commissions from top orchestras and a Princeton PhD who felt her music was wrongly characterized as Indian-influenced. The concept's reach extended well beyond race: it became embedded in policy debates over gender-affirming care, abortion regulation, and the racial wealth gap, wherever correlational social science was used to justify causal claims of harm from discrimination or stigma.

The harm produced by the assumption ran in several directions. Fifty years of microaggression scholarship produced, as Lee Jussim of Rutgers and Richard McNally of Harvard documented, no substantiation for its main claims, wasting enormous academic resources and distorting psychotherapy with clinical recommendations lacking empirical basis. Scientists who might have challenged the work were cowed into silence by the threat of being labeled racist, undermining the organized skepticism on which science depends. On campuses, the framework fostered what sociologists Bradley Campbell and Jason Manning identified as a "victimhood culture," in which minor verbal slights were escalated into public spectacles requiring authority intervention, supplanting the tolerance and direct negotiation characteristic of dignity cultures. Faculty speech was chilled. Institutional resources were diverted from material problems facing minority communities, such as unsafe housing, hunger, and violence, toward policing the language of the privileged. Flawed scales like the RMAS were used in studies linking perceived microaggressions to suicide risk, potentially worsening mental health among minority students by training them to interpret ambiguous interactions as threats.

A substantial body of experts now reject the causal claims at the heart of microaggression research. Lilienfeld's 2017 paper marked a turning point, finding negligible support for all five core premises and recommending a moratorium on microaggression training programs until the concept could be properly operationalized. His recommendation was largely ignored, but the critique proved durable. Subsequent analyses showed that effect sizes in the meta-analytic literature were stronger in cross-sectional studies than in longitudinal ones, precisely the pattern one would expect if the associations were not causal. Physical health links were small and inconsistent, especially weak for outcomes like hypertension. A 2021 analysis detailed how the RMAS's subjective items captured negative emotionality rather than objective exposure to hostility. Rob Sica, a librarian at Colorado State, used AI tools to evaluate a recent microaggression review and found its claims unsupported. Surveys of minorities themselves showed that many of the phrases catalogued as microaggressions were not considered offensive by their supposed victims. Lee Jussim continued to document how the field's epistemic insulation, its habit of treating criticism as proof of the problem, had rendered it resistant to correction but not to scrutiny. The research program persists in journals and trainings, but its scientific foundations have been exposed as correlations dressed in causal language, a body of work that told institutions what they wanted to hear and charged them handsomely for the privilege.
---

## There is no housing bubble (2008)

*Rising housing prices before 2008 reflected economic fundamentals with no bubble present.*

For most of the early 2000s, a cohort of well-credentialed economists assured the public that American housing prices, though rising at extraordinary rates, reflected nothing more than sound economic fundamentals. The assumption rested on a simple proposition: low interest rates, local supply constraints, and expected growth justified what the market was doing. Conventional warning signs, such as soaring price-to-income and price-to-rent ratios, were dismissed as crude and misleading. In their place, researchers offered refined models built on user cost theory, which adjusted for tax treatment, interest rates, and anticipated appreciation. Charles Himmelberg of the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, Christopher Mayer of Columbia Business School, and Todd Sinai of the Wharton School published a 2005 paper, issued as both an NBER working paper and a Fed staff report, concluding that house prices as of late 2004 showed "little evidence" of a bubble. Margaret Hwang Smith and Gary Smith of Pomona College used matched rent and sale data in ten metro areas to argue that buying remained attractive. In a 2005 Wall Street Journal op-ed, Mayer and Sinai went further, calling bubble skeptics economically illiterate Chicken Littles. In October 2005, Fed Chair nominee Ben Bernanke told Congress that house price increases "largely reflect strong economic fundamentals." The consensus was broad, credentialed, and wrong.

The assumption spread through every channel that mattered. The NBER distributed the Himmelberg, Mayer, and Sinai paper to researchers and policymakers. The American Economic Association published a version in its Journal of Economic Perspectives, lending it the authority of a flagship journal. The Brookings Institution ran the Smiths' analysis in its Papers on Economic Activity. The Wall Street Journal carried op-eds to investors and officials. Non-technical summaries with titles like "Is There a Housing Bubble?" reached audiences beyond academia. Citations accumulated. Each paper reinforced the next. The models varied in detail but converged on the same conclusion: prices were reasonable, and those who said otherwise simply did not understand the economics. Dissenters existed. Robert Shiller at Yale warned for years of an unprecedented bubble and predicted real prices might fall 50 percent. Paul Krugman wrote in August 2005 that a coastal bubble was already leaking air. Dean Baker identified the bubble as early as summer 2002. These voices were noted, then overruled by the weight of institutional consensus.

The no-bubble view did not merely comfort anxious homebuyers. It shaped policy. The Federal Reserve kept interest rates low into 2006, and the academic literature treating low rates as a fundamental justification for high prices aligned neatly with that stance. Policymakers who might have tightened lending standards or questioned the proliferation of subprime mortgages found intellectual cover in papers from the Fed's own research staff and from Ivy League real estate centers. Regulatory inaction was not an accident; it was the logical product of a framework that defined away the problem. If prices reflected fundamentals, there was nothing to regulate. The models that propped up this view shared a critical blind spot: they accounted for interest rates and rents but ignored the leverage, speculation, and deteriorating loan quality that were actually driving demand. Nationwide price surges of 50 percent in five years, and over 100 percent in hot markets, were treated as confirmation of fundamentals rather than as a warning.

Housing prices peaked in 2006 and then collapsed. By 2009, values had fallen far below the rental fundamentals that were supposed to anchor them. The crash vaporized billions in household wealth, triggered millions of foreclosures, destroyed the financial security of families who had been told by credentialed experts that buying was a sound investment, and plunged the country into its worst recession since the 1930s. The social consequences extended beyond economics: rage, despair, and a lasting distrust of institutions fed a populist backlash that reshaped politics for a generation. The very models that had been deployed to prove prices were reasonable had, in the late 1980s, flagged overvaluation in cities like Boston, Los Angeles, and San Francisco, where prices subsequently fell. The authors knew their framework could identify mispricing. They simply concluded, in 2004 and 2005, that it was not happening.

The collapse did not merely discredit individual papers. A 2009 study by David Colander and colleagues argued that the financial crisis exposed a systematic failure of academic economics, not just a missed forecast but a flawed set of assumptions about how markets work. The rent-based models, the user cost adjustments, the dismissals of simple ratios: all had served to rationalize a mania rather than diagnose it. The economists involved were not, for the most part, dishonest. They were careful, credentialed, and published in the best journals. They were also spectacularly wrong, and the cost of their error was borne not by them but by millions of ordinary people who lost their homes.
---

## Gain-of-Function Benefits Outweigh Risks

*The benefits of gain-of-function experiments on viruses outweigh the risks of laboratory accidents causing pandemics.*

For decades, a confident assumption governed the most dangerous corner of biological research: that deliberately engineering pathogens to be more transmissible or more virulent was worth the risk because the knowledge gained would help humanity prepare for the next pandemic. The assumption had a clear intellectual architect. Anthony Fauci, director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases for nearly four decades, argued in a 2012 paper that the benefits of gain-of-function experiments outweighed the risks, reasoning that a natural pandemic was far more likely than a laboratory accident and that scientists needed to stay ahead of the virus. The argument had genuine force in the context of H5N1 avian influenza, which killed more than half the humans it infected but had not yet acquired efficient airborne transmission. When Ron Fouchier of Erasmus University and Yoshihiro Kawaoka of the University of Wisconsin independently demonstrated in 2011 and 2012 that a small number of mutations could make H5N1 airborne between ferrets, proponents treated the results as vindication: the research had identified a pandemic threat before nature produced it. Critics like Marc Lipsitch of Harvard saw the same results differently. The work had demonstrated, in a laboratory, how to make one of the world's deadliest bird flu strains transmissible between mammals, and the claimed predictive value was undermined by the randomness of viral evolution and the poor generalizability of strain-specific results.

The assumption spread through the funding and publication structures of academic virology with remarkable efficiency. NIAID grants shaped research agendas, and the National Academies convened workshops that amplified the benefits case through presentations on surveillance, vaccine development, and zoonotic threat assessment. Researchers at institutions like St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, operating as both an NIAID-funded Center of Excellence and a WHO collaborating center, argued that gain-of-function experiments were essential for identifying transmissibility markers and selecting vaccine strains. The CDC incorporated gain-of-function-derived data into its Influenza Risk Assessment Tool, embedding the research's outputs into operational public health decisions. Peter Daszak, president of EcoHealth Alliance, became the primary conduit for federal funds flowing to the Wuhan Institute of Virology for bat coronavirus research. In 2018, his organization submitted a proposal to DARPA, code-named DEFUSE, that outlined plans to insert furin cleavage sites into bat coronaviruses at a BSL-2 facility, a biosafety level roughly equivalent to a dentist's office. DARPA rejected the proposal on biosafety grounds. The work it described, however, was consistent with research that had already been funded through other channels. When COVID-19 emerged, Daszak organized a letter in The Lancet condemning lab-leak theories as conspiracy thinking without disclosing his organization's direct involvement in coronavirus research at the Wuhan lab. Papers in Cell argued there was no evidence of prior furin cleavage site insertion research, a claim the later leak of the DEFUSE proposal directly contradicted.

The policies produced by this assumption followed a pattern of nominal caution and practical permissiveness. A voluntary moratorium on H5N1 transmissibility research lasted roughly a year after 2012 and ended without fundamental regulatory change. The Obama administration imposed a broader funding pause in 2014 on gain-of-function research involving influenza, MERS, and SARS, affecting eighteen laboratories. The pause followed a series of biosafety incidents at federal facilities: the CDC exposed 75 staff to anthrax through procedural failures and separately contaminated benign flu samples with H5N1. Yet NIAID continued to fund EcoHealth Alliance for bat coronavirus research during the pause, maintaining that the work did not meet the agency's definition of gain-of-function, a definition that Richard Ebright of Rutgers criticized as so narrow and malleable that it excluded research that plainly qualified. The HHS established a formal review process in 2017, and the White House issued a unified policy in 2024 narrowing oversight to the highest-risk pathogens. Florida banned gain-of-function research outright in 2023. These measures arrived long after the most consequential experiments had already been conducted.

The harm was not hypothetical. The laboratory accident record alone should have given pause: a 1978 smallpox leak in Birmingham killed Janet Parker after the disease had been eradicated in the wild; a 1979 anthrax leak from a Soviet facility in Sverdlovsk killed dozens despite extensive biosafety protocols; the 2014 CDC incidents demonstrated that even the most heavily regulated facilities in the world could not guarantee containment. Chimeric coronaviruses produced at the Wuhan Institute of Virology using bat spike proteins replicated to concentrations ten thousand times higher in mouse lungs than the parent strain. In November 2019, researchers at the Wuhan lab fell ill with a mysterious respiratory illness. By December, Wuhan's morgues were overwhelmed. COVID-19 went on to become one of the five deadliest pandemics in recorded history, killing millions worldwide. Multiple U.S. intelligence agencies assessed a laboratory origin as the most likely explanation. The virus's distinctive furin cleavage site, the very feature the DEFUSE proposal had planned to engineer into bat coronaviruses, remained at the center of the origins debate. The White House officially declared that COVID-19 originated from the Wuhan laboratory.

The assumption began to collapse under the weight of disclosures it was never designed to withstand. The leak of the DEFUSE proposal in 2021 revealed that a federal agency had already judged EcoHealth Alliance's planned coronavirus engineering too risky to fund, and that the proposal described inserting exactly the genomic feature that distinguished SARS-CoV-2 from its closest known relatives. Congressional hearings and FOIA requests exposed the definitional games that had allowed NIAID to fund chimeric coronavirus work while denying it constituted gain-of-function research. At the scientific level, an influential minority of researchers argued that the foundational claims had not held up: evolutionary randomness meant laboratory results did not generalize reliably, surveillance gaps rather than missing gain-of-function data had caused prediction failures, and vaccine manufacturers themselves disagreed about whether the research was essential to their work. Christophe Fraser of Imperial College noted that the value of gain-of-function data during actual outbreaks was unclear. Lipsitch stated flatly that there was no demonstrated route by which such studies had informed the key questions that arise during a pandemic. The confident institutional consensus that once made the risk-benefit calculation seem obvious has not survived contact with the evidence. Growing evidence suggests the research community wagered the world's safety on containment assumptions that the historical record had already disproven, and that the benefits invoked to justify the gamble were, in significant part, speculative.
---

## Mass Graves Found at Residential Schools

*Ground-penetrating radar discovered mass graves of hundreds of Indigenous children at former residential school sites.*

In May 2021, the Tk'emlúps te Secwépemc First Nation announced that ground-penetrating radar had detected what it described as the remains of 215 children buried on the grounds of the former Kamloops Indian Residential School in British Columbia. The announcement was rooted in decades of Survivor testimony and a genuine, documented history of institutional abuse. Canada's Truth and Reconciliation Commission had reported in 2015 that at least 4,120 children died while attending residential schools, mostly from diseases like tuberculosis, and had called the system "cultural genocide." Its Calls to Action, including demands for record-sharing, burial site research, and a papal apology, had gone largely unimplemented for six years. When the radar results arrived, they seemed to confirm what survivors had been saying all along. Within hours, the phrase "soil anomalies" became "mass graves," and the number 215 became a symbol of systematic murder and concealment. The distinction between what GPR actually detects, which is soil disturbance and density variation, and what the public was told it had found was real, consequential, and almost immediately lost.

The story traveled at the speed of outrage. The New York Times, CNN, NPR, and the BBC reported the findings in terms that implied confirmed human remains. Similar announcements from over twenty other sites followed within weeks, each amplified by coverage that treated radar anomalies as discovered graves. Prime Minister Justin Trudeau ordered flags at half-staff on all federal buildings. His government pledged $238.8 million for searches, though the funding covered only detection equipment and excluded exhumation, DNA analysis, or legal costs. Pope Francis undertook what he called a "pilgrimage of penance" to Canada in July 2022, kneeling before survivors at a former school site in Maskwacis, Alberta, calling forced assimilation a "deplorable evil," and begging forgiveness. The Canadian government agreed to C$40 billion in compensation for affected First Nations children. In the United States, Secretary of the Interior Deb Haaland launched the Federal Indian Boarding School Initiative, which documented at least 973 child deaths across more than 400 schools, and President Biden issued a formal apology in October 2024. The institutional response was enormous, and it was built on a narrative that growing evidence suggests was significantly overstated in its most specific and inflammatory claim.

The harm was immediate and multidirectional. Twenty-four churches burned in arsons over two years, including churches that served First Nations congregations with no institutional connection to the residential school system. Trudeau declined to condemn the arsons directly, calling the underlying anger "real and fully understandable." Canada Day celebrations were canceled in multiple cities. Within academic and media circles, critics who questioned whether GPR anomalies constituted confirmed mass graves were labeled practitioners of "residential school denialism," a framing that collapsed the distinction between denying documented abuse and questioning unverified radar findings. For survivors and their families, the cycle was especially cruel: the promise of answers was followed by years of bureaucratic delay, funding restrictions, and record access barriers that communities described as a continuation of the indifference they had always faced. The Catholic Bishops had promised C$30 million for healing; they raised C$4.6 million. Kimberly Murray, appointed as Independent Special Interlocutor in 2022, documented five persistent concerns from communities, including insufficient funding, delays of up to eight months for records, and the complete absence of legal protections for potential burial sites on private land.

The assumption began to unravel not with a dramatic revelation but with a quiet absence. Excavations at sites where GPR had detected anomalies found no human remains. This happened repeatedly, across multiple sites, without producing the confirmations that the original announcements had led the public to expect. The Kamloops survey itself had covered only two acres of a 160-acre site; as of 2024, no human remains had been excavated or confirmed there. The Canadian Archaeological Association had warned from the start that GPR identifies soil disturbances, not bodies, and that excavation was required before conclusions could be drawn. That warning had been buried beneath the headlines. The Canadian government's decision to quietly end funding for the graves committee in 2025, without a press conference or an official acknowledgment that the narrative had been overstated, was an institutional admission delivered in the only language governments truly speak: budget lines.

What remains is a genuine and well-documented history of abuse, death, and institutional cruelty at residential and boarding schools in both Canada and the United States, a history that the TRC, the Federal Indian Boarding School Initiative, and decades of Survivor testimony have established beyond serious dispute. Thousands of children died. Many were buried in ways their families were never told about. The records are incomplete, the institutions were negligent or worse, and the intergenerational trauma is real. None of that is in question. What growing evidence now challenges is the specific, incendiary claim that ground-penetrating radar had discovered mass graves containing hundreds of secretly buried children. That claim drove a papal pilgrimage, billions in settlements, a wave of arsons, and a cultural convulsion that reshaped Canadian public life. It also appears, on the evidence produced so far, to have outrun what the evidence actually showed. The documented horrors of the residential school system did not require embellishment. They received it anyway, and the embellishment has made the truth harder, not easier, to reckon with.
---

## Social Media Safe for Adolescents

*Social media is a reasonably safe consumer product for children and adolescents even when used heavily.*

For roughly two decades, the working assumption among technology companies, regulators, and a significant fraction of social scientists was that social media was a reasonably safe consumer product for children and adolescents, even when used heavily. The assumption rested on several pillars. The Children's Online Privacy Protection Act of 1998 had set thirteen as the age at which children could contract with online platforms, imposing no verification requirement and creating a global default that treated a self-reported birthdate as sufficient proof of maturity. Platforms adopted this standard and did nothing to enforce it. When correlational studies began linking heavy social media use to rising rates of depression and anxiety among adolescents, a chorus of researchers and executives repeated the same refrain: correlation does not prove causation. The argument was not frivolous. Reverse causality and confounding variables were genuine methodological concerns. But it was deployed less as a scientific caution than as a rhetorical shield, one that Meta's CEO Mark Zuckerberg was still invoking in 2024 Senate testimony even as his company sat on the results of at least thirty-one internal studies, some of them randomized controlled trials, showing that its products caused measurable harm.

The assumption spread through institutional inertia, financial incentive, and social pressure. Tech companies earned an estimated eleven billion dollars in 2022 from advertising directed at children and adolescents. TikTok's leadership, according to internal documents, was aware of compulsive usage patterns linked to losses in analytical skills, memory, and empathy, but declined to implement fixes that would affect core engagement metrics. Snapchat's director of security engineering acknowledged that the platform had tolerated a broken system for a decade, one that enabled child sexual abuse material, drug sales, and roughly ten thousand sextortion reports from minors every month. Platforms spent heavily to oppose regulatory restrictions. Parents, meanwhile, were caught in a collective action trap: children claimed "everyone else has one," and the available parental controls were poorly designed, binary in their options, and no match for products engineered around infinite scroll, intermittent rewards, and notification-driven compulsion. Sweden mandated digital tools in its national preschool curriculum in 2019, and its public health institute issued no screen-time guidelines until 2024. Questioning screens was dismissed as moral panic, akin to historical anxieties about comic books or rock music, a comparison that ignored the fundamental difference between passive media and algorithmically optimized, interactive platforms designed to maximize time on site.

The harms, when they were finally catalogued, were staggering in scale. Anxiety among eighteen-to-twenty-five-year-olds rose approximately 139 percent between 2010 and the early 2020s. Emergency room visits for self-harm among girls aged ten to fourteen increased 188 percent; suicides in the same group rose 167 percent. By 2022, 46 percent of teenagers reported being online almost constantly, averaging eight hours daily, with documented addiction symptoms including irritability, insomnia, and measurable changes in brain regions governing cognitive control. Instagram's own internal research found that in a weekly sample of thirteen-to-fifteen-year-olds, eleven percent were bullied, thirteen percent were sexually advanced upon, nineteen percent were exposed to explicit content, and twenty-one percent felt worse about themselves. Swedish fifteen-year-olds posted their lowest math and reading scores in a decade on the 2022 PISA assessment. In the United Kingdom, half of thirteen-year-olds had seen pornography, and one in eight thirteen-to-fifteen-year-olds reported weekly unwanted sexual advances on Instagram. The case of Roxy Longworth, sextorted at thirteen and driven to self-harm, eating disorders, psychosis, and multiple suicide attempts, was not an outlier; a fourteen-year-old girl in the same area died by suicide days earlier from an identical pattern. Physical consequences accumulated alongside the psychological ones: over 250 million children worldwide were obese, outdoor play fell to minutes per day, time spent with friends halved, and teachers across multiple countries reported that smartphones had destroyed attention, stamina, and the capacity for sustained learning.

The assumption began to crack in 2017, when psychologist Jean Twenge published data in The Atlantic correlating smartphone and social media adoption with the collapse of adolescent mental health. Social psychologist Jonathan Haidt expanded the case in his 2024 book The Anxious Generation, marshalling longitudinal studies, MRI evidence of brain changes in heavy users, and classroom observations to argue that the phone-based childhood was rewiring adolescent development during puberty, a sensitive period of intense neural plasticity. Frances Haugen's 2021 leak of thousands of internal Meta documents, including the results of Project Mercury, a randomized controlled trial showing that deactivating Facebook and Instagram for a month reduced depression, anxiety, and loneliness, demolished the company's public claim that the evidence was merely correlational. Three high-quality longitudinal studies provided forward predictability evidence: earlier social media use predicted later depression, not merely the reverse. Candice Odgers and other good-faith skeptics continued to argue for the reverse predictability hypothesis, and the scientific debate remains active, but growing evidence now favors the causal interpretation, and an influential minority of researchers treats the question as largely settled.

The policy response, when it finally arrived, was swift and broadly popular. Australia enacted legislation restricting social media access for users under sixteen; its December 2025 rollout closed 4.7 million underage accounts with platform compliance and massive public support, proving that restrictions were both technically feasible and politically viable. Sweden reversed its preschool screen mandate, announced a nationwide school phone ban for 2026, and redirected funding from digital devices to physical textbooks. The United Kingdom's Online Safety Act of 2023, built on Beeban Kidron's Age Appropriate Design Code, imposed a duty of care on platforms enforceable by Ofcom, which confirmed that platforms vastly reduced recommendations of pornography and self-harm content to children after implementation. Haidt praised Australia's move as a breakthrough in which private knowledge became common knowledge. The old framework, in which thirteen-year-olds were treated as adults capable of navigating algorithmically optimized environments designed to maximize engagement, is now being dismantled country by country. Whether the replacement framework will prove adequate to the scale of the damage already done is a separate question, and not an encouraging one.
---

## Willpower Builds Long-Term Success

*Exercising state self-control through willpower in the moment reliably leads to long-term success and better life outcomes.*

For decades, a seemingly sturdy chain of reasoning held sway across psychology, education, public health, and self-improvement culture: studies showed that people with high trait self-control earned more money, stayed healthier, performed better in school, and committed fewer crimes. The inference seemed obvious. These people must be exerting more willpower, more frequently, in the moments that mattered. And if willpower was the mechanism, then teaching people to exercise it should produce the same outcomes. The assumption drew support from famous longitudinal studies, most notably Walter Mischel's marshmallow test at Stanford, which tracked children who delayed gratification and found them earning higher SAT scores and reporting better life outcomes years later. It drew support from Roy Baumeister's ego depletion theory, which posited willpower as a depletable but trainable resource, demonstrated through a radish-versus-cookie paradigm that launched hundreds of follow-up studies. It drew support from Angela Duckworth's work on grit at the University of Pennsylvania, co-authored with James Gross at Stanford, which framed self-control as a predictor of success on par with intelligence. And it drew support from Malcolm Gladwell's 2008 bestseller Outliers, which distilled K. Anders Ericsson's 1993 study of violin students into the "10,000-hour rule," a formula suggesting that disciplined, deliberate practice was the dominant ingredient in greatness. Across all these threads, the message converged: success belongs to those who push through.

The assumption spread through peer-reviewed journals, TED talks, bestselling books, and personal advice columns. Social psychologists promoted it by failing to distinguish between trait self-control (a stable personality characteristic closely overlapping with conscientiousness, a construct personality psychologists had already named) and state self-control (the effortful, in-the-moment act of resisting temptation). The shared terminology made the conflation invisible. Ericsson's violin study was cited over 9,800 times. Gladwell's popularization reached millions. Cultural moralizing reinforced the message: excess was moral failure, discipline was virtue, and anyone who fell short simply hadn't tried hard enough. Psychology Ph.D. programs taught the framework as settled science. Publication bias ensured that confirming studies accumulated while null results were dismissed, sometimes with remarkable candor; Baumeister reportedly told graduate students whose replications failed that they lacked an essential but indescribable "flair" for running the experiments. Paul Meehl, as president of the American Psychological Association in 1967, had warned about exactly this pattern: flawed theory-building sustained by ad hoc dismissal of disconfirming evidence. The warning went largely unheeded for half a century.

The policies that followed were extensive. Intervention developers created educational programs to build self-regulatory skills through behavioral norms, curricula, and direct delay-of-gratification training, targeting disadvantaged children on the premise that altering their willpower would alter their life trajectories. West Point used grit measures in cadet selection. Chicago public schools applied similar frameworks to student retention. The U.S. Army Recruiting Command operated for years on the organizational equivalent of the willpower thesis, assuming that shortfalls could be closed through harder effort, involuntary assignments, and late-year surges rather than structural adaptation to a changing labor market. Abstinence-based addiction treatment programs, most prominently Alcoholics Anonymous, became the default in rehabilitation facilities nationwide, sidelining pharmacological alternatives like naltrexone on the premise that quitting alcohol required willpower and moral resolve rather than medical intervention. Head Start, the federal preschool program launched in 1965, was designed primarily to boost IQ, treating non-cognitive traits like self-control as secondary or developmental, something children would simply outgrow.

The harm was both personal and systemic. People spent years on state self-control strategies, white-knuckling their way through diets, exercise regimens, and sobriety programs, only to relapse to baseline as the short-term gains evaporated. Willpower interventions produced modest immediate results that eroded over time, wasting resources across psychology labs and clinical settings. The narrative created what amounted to a moral meritocracy: conscientious people felt superior for traits that were substantially genetic and environmental, while others internalized their failures as evidence of deficient character. The 10,000-hour rule generated exhausting pressure to achieve greatness through sheer volume of practice, fostering self-deprecation in people who practiced extensively and stayed average. Abstinence-based treatment for alcoholism carried roughly a ninety percent relapse rate, prolonging suffering and costing lives while effective pharmacological alternatives sat unused. The Army's end strength dropped to its lowest level since 1940. Research agendas across psychology were distorted for years, chasing ego depletion and delay-of-gratification training while sidelining more promising approaches like habit formation and temptation avoidance. Unpublished failed replications accumulated in file drawers, leaving the scientific record unbalanced.

Growing evidence now suggests the entire causal chain was wrong at its most critical link. A review by Michael Inzlicht of the University of Toronto and Brent Roberts of the University of Illinois, both of whom had spent careers within the self-control paradigm, laid out the case plainly: people with high trait self-control engage in less state self-control, not more. They succeed not by resisting temptation but by arranging their lives to encounter less of it, through planning, routinization, and the pursuit of goals they autonomously want rather than goals imposed on them. Experience sampling studies confirmed that high self-control individuals face fewer cognitive conflicts and rely on habits rather than effortful restraint. A preregistered multi-site replication by 36 laboratories with 3,531 participants found no evidence for ego depletion. A separate multilab replication with 23 laboratories and 2,141 participants reached the same conclusion. Watts et al. (2018), in a preregistered study of 702 participants followed to age 26, found that the marshmallow test's predictive power for achievement, health, and behavior became nonsignificant once background factors like socioeconomic status and early cognitive ability were controlled. The children who waited longer for the marshmallow were, in the main, smarter and wealthier, not more disciplined. A preregistered 2019 replication of Ericsson's violin study found practice accounted for only 26 percent of performance differences, with elite violinists often logging less solo practice than average good ones; a 2016 meta-analysis across 33 sports studies put the figure at 18 percent. Longitudinal analysis of personality changes showed that dramatic short-term trait improvements reverted to baseline over seven years. Evidence from the Sinclair Method exposed the superiority of targeted pharmacology over abstinence for alcohol use disorder. Inzlicht and Roberts now propose abandoning the term "self-control" in favor of conscientiousness, and emphasizing planning over exertion as the actual mechanism behind long-term success. The field is not yet unanimous, but an influential minority is making a case that is becoming difficult to dismiss: willpower was never the engine. It was, at best, the exhaust.
---

## Black Doctors Double Black Newborn Survival

*For high-risk Black newborns, having a Black physician more than doubles the likelihood that the baby will live.*

In 2020, a study published in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences examined over a million Florida births spanning more than two decades and reported that Black newborns cared for by Black physicians had significantly lower mortality rates. The researchers controlled for sixty-five common comorbidities, insurance status, and hospital characteristics. The finding seemed powerful and clean: racial concordance between doctor and patient appeared to save Black babies' lives. Media outlets including the Washington Post, CNN, and Nature ran with the conclusion. The Association of American Medical Colleges folded it into an amicus brief submitted to the Supreme Court. Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson cited the claim in her dissent in Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard, arguing that racial diversity in the medical profession produces measurable, life-saving benefits and therefore justifies race-conscious admissions. The statistic entered the legal record of one of the most consequential affirmative-action decisions in a generation.

The study had a hole in it. Very low birth weight, defined as under 1,500 grams, accounts for roughly two-thirds of White neonatal deaths and over four-fifths of Black neonatal deaths. Black infants are more than 2.5 times as likely as White infants to fall into this category. The original paper never controlled for it. The sixty-five comorbidities it did adjust for were the most common diagnoses in the dataset, but very low birth weight spans about thirty rare ICD-9 codes that did not make that list. In the Florida data, the sickest and smallest Black newborns were disproportionately treated by White physicians. The study's models therefore compared healthier Black babies under Black doctors to sicker Black babies under White doctors and attributed the mortality difference to the doctor's race.

The assumption produced real policy consequences. Medical schools and their professional associations cited the finding to defend race-conscious admissions, arguing that training more Black physicians would directly reduce Black infant mortality. Harvard's admissions process used race at every stage of review, from first-reader scores through subcommittee deliberations to the final lop list. The University of North Carolina followed a similar structure, with readers instructed to assign race-based pluses. Both institutions pointed, in part, to health-outcome evidence like the concordance study to justify these practices. The Supreme Court ultimately struck down both programs as violations of the Equal Protection Clause, but the study's influence had already shaped years of argument.

The harm ran in two directions. On one side, the claim diverted attention from the actual driver of the racial gap in neonatal mortality: the disproportionate incidence of very low birth weight and prematurity among Black infants. Policy energy flowed toward race-matching physicians rather than toward reducing the underlying conditions that kill newborns regardless of who is in the room. On the other side, headlines warning that White doctors endangered Black babies corroded trust in medical care on the basis of a statistical artifact. The Nature article framed bias as a lethal force in the delivery ward. Patients and hospitals may have altered decisions accordingly, though the full extent is hard to measure.

The unraveling began with a clarification letter from Norton Rose Fulbright, the law firm that had filed the amicus brief, acknowledging that the brief had misstated the study's actual numbers. Ted Frank published a Wall Street Journal op-ed detailing the mathematical error, and legal commentator Jonathan Turley questioned the lack of scrutiny given to amicus data. The decisive blow came from economists George J. Borjas of Harvard and Robert VerBruggen of the Manhattan Institute, who reanalyzed the identical Florida dataset with direct controls for very low birth weight. When those variables were added, the racial concordance effect shrank to statistical insignificance. A replication exercise first reproduced the original results using only the top sixty-five comorbidities, then sequentially added birth-weight indicators and watched the coefficient collapse. PNAS published the reanalysis on September 16, 2024, placing the journal in the position of having endorsed both the original claim and its refutation. Growing evidence suggests the assumption was built on a confound, though debate over residual effects has not entirely closed.
---

## Great-Because-Girl Stories Succeed Commercially

*Injecting 'great-because-girl' tropes and 'bad-because-boy' dynamics into male-centric franchises will maintain or expand audience appeal and box office success.*

The assumption was straightforward: take franchises built around male heroes, insert female protagonists who succeed through innate superiority rather than earned struggle, diminish the male characters to make room, and audiences would follow or even grow. The belief seemed credible because it arrived wrapped in data. A study produced between 2018 and 2019 by Creative Artists Agency and the consultancy Shift7, in partnership with the Time's Up advocacy organization, analyzed 350 films released between 2014 and 2017 and claimed that female-led films at budgets above $100 million averaged $586 million in worldwide gross against $514 million for male-led films. Amy Pascal, former chairman of Sony Pictures, declared the findings "powerful proof that audiences want women represented on screen." Geena Davis compressed the claim into a slogan: "seeing women on screen is good entertainment and good business." Christy Haubegger of CAA argued that the data did not support the industry assumption that female-led films were generally less successful. The Bechdel Test was cited as further evidence; every film grossing over $1 billion since 2012 had passed it. What received less attention was that the study's methodology classified leads by first billing in press materials, introducing a selection bias toward films studios had already identified as commercially promising. "Star Wars: The Force Awakens" was coded as male-led because Harrison Ford appeared first in the billing block, despite the film being built around Daisy Ridley's character. The Bechdel Test correlation was more plausibly explained by ensemble size than by gender dynamics. The study was not peer-reviewed academic research but an industry document produced by organizations with a direct interest in its conclusions.

The assumption spread through institutional channels that made dissent professionally costly. As women reached critical mass in Hollywood development, production, and marketing roles, objecting to a female protagonist's implausible competence or a male character's gratuitous humiliation became risky in ways that objecting to bad plotting in other contexts was not. Deadline published the study results as news. Vanity Fair framed them as myth-busting evidence against studio conservatism. The New York Times interviewed the research team and spread the claim that female leads were a marketing asset. None of these outlets subjected the methodology to the scrutiny that would have been applied to research reaching opposite conclusions. The publishing industry, approximately 80 percent female in its editorial workforce, reinforced the dynamic by drying up the pipeline of male-authored source material that had historically fed Hollywood adaptations. In gaming, the propagation mechanism was more confrontational: following the Gamergate controversy of 2014, mainstream media adopted a framing in which criticism of feminist incursions into game design was treated as evidence of misogyny rather than as a legitimate aesthetic or commercial objection. Critics who raised concerns about narrative quality were grouped with the genuinely abusive minority who sent threats, making it possible to dismiss the entire range of objections by association. Meta committed $50 million to a two-year program embedding diversity into game design, treating the commercial case as established. Stacy L. Smith of the USC Annenberg Inclusion Initiative declared 2024 "the first time gender equality has reached the top-grossing films," attributing the shift to advocacy and DEI initiatives.

The policies that followed were consequential. Disney, having acquired Lucasfilm for $4 billion, produced a Star Wars sequel trilogy that deliberately diminished Han Solo and Luke Skywalker while centering a female protagonist whose abilities required no training and no struggle. The live-action "Mulan" remake removed the central dramatic tension of the 1998 original, replacing a protagonist who struggled and grew with one who was already exceptional. Disney canceled "The Acolyte," a $100 million Star Wars series featuring a diverse and queer cast, after a single season without public explanation, a silence that allowed competing narratives to fill the vacuum. Hollywood productions began implementing extra security for actors like Kaitlyn Dever based on threat assessments tied to diverse casting. Anheuser-Busch implemented an influencer marketing partnership with transgender content creator Dylan Mulvaney for Bud Light, based on the assumption that identity-inclusive marketing would not damage the brand with existing consumers. Brendan Whitworth, the company's CEO, later said the partnership had "never intended to be part of a discussion that divides people," a statement that satisfied no one.

The harm was both commercial and human. The Star Wars sequel trilogy declined from $2.1 billion to $1.3 billion to $1.1 billion in worldwide gross across its three installments, on production budgets of $245 million to $317 million per film. Kelly Marie Tran deleted her social media presence entirely after sustained harassment. John Boyega stated publicly that Disney had sidelined his character to appease fans. Amandla Stenberg faced racist death threats after her casting in "The Acolyte." Bud Light sales dropped more than 23 percent in a single week, Modelo Especial displaced it as the top-selling beer in America, and Anheuser-Busch's stock was downgraded. The company's attempt to distance itself from Mulvaney drew a secondary backlash from LGBTQ+ groups, leaving the brand exposed from both directions. In gaming, Matthew Karch, CEO of Saber Interactive and former COO of Embracer Group, stated publicly that games burdened with "imposed morals" made him "want to cry." Developers and journalists faced harassment over character design decisions in titles like "Star Wars Outlaws," where the protagonist Kay Vess was attacked as deliberately uglified by diversity consultants despite no evidence that advocacy firm Sweet Baby Inc had any involvement.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption is falling apart across multiple industries simultaneously. The Star Wars sequel trilogy's declining arc is increasingly difficult to attribute to anything other than audience attrition driven by narrative choices. In gaming, "Warhammer 40,000: Space Marine 2" peaked at over 225,000 concurrent players by focusing on fun and spectacle without moral messaging, while "Black Myth: Wukong" similarly prioritized gameplay over ideological signaling; both succeeded where messaging-heavy titles had struggled. The Bud Light sales collapse provided the clearest single data point: a 23 percent weekly decline and the loss of a market leadership position held for over two decades constituted a measurable commercial verdict. The CAA-Shift7 study's methodological weaknesses, the selection bias in lead classification, the Star Wars coding anomaly, the conflation of Bechdel Test passage with causation, have been identified by critics and commenters with increasing specificity. An influential minority of industry figures, Karch among them, now argue openly that the assumption reflected a systematic misreading of how existing franchise audiences respond when familiar narrative values are replaced with identity-based signaling. The successes that did occur, "Wonder Woman," "Black Panther," "Beauty and the Beast," succeeded precisely because their protagonists earned their competence through struggle, the very quality the "great-because-girl" template had been designed to eliminate.
---

## Brain Differences Are Caused by Structural Racism

*Race differences in brain structure serve as a reminder of structural racism's public health effects.*

The assumption was straightforward: observed differences in brain structure between Black and White Americans are products of structural racism, not biology. Researchers at Harvard Medical School and McLean Hospital, led by Nathaniel Harnett and E. Kate Webb, with senior co-author Kerry Ressler, published reviews arguing that racism-related stress dysregulates the HPA axis, alters neural threat circuits, and disrupts self-regulation systems in ways that show up on brain scans. Harnett's neuroimaging studies identified subtle differences in stress-response systems between Black and White children as young as nine or ten, and blunted threat reactivity in Black adults, which he attributed to disproportionate adversity rooted in systemic inequity. A separate team at Vanderbilt, led by Carolina Gonçalves and Velma McBride Murry with Harvard's David R. Williams, proposed a Biopsychosocial Integrative Systems Model linking historical racism to cascading effects on Black youth brain functionality. The intellectual lineage was long. Carmichael and Hamilton coined "structural racism" in 1967. Ashley Montagu had argued in 1942 that race is a social concept with no genetic basis. Richard Lewontin's 1972 study, showing 85 percent of human protein variation falls within rather than between populations, became the empirical cornerstone for treating racial categories as biologically meaningless. These threads converged into an orthodoxy: race is a social construct, group differences in outcomes must therefore be environmental, and brain scans showing racial disparities must reflect the toll of oppression.

The assumption spread through the usual channels of elite academic production. Harnett co-authored nearly 30 papers in two years on racism's brain impacts, amplified by Harvard Catalyst's promotional infrastructure and a dedicated two-year fellowship in diversity inclusion. The journal Neuropsychopharmacology published narrative reviews synthesizing the claimed neurological effects for clinical and research audiences. Theoretical frameworks from Critical Race Theory, Bronfenbrenner's bioecological model, and Bonilla-Silva's systemic racism concept were woven into the research, giving it the appearance of interdisciplinary consensus. In parallel, Anthony Greenwald and Mahzarin Banaji's Implicit Association Test had already primed institutions to accept the idea that racism resides in individual brains as measurable neural associations. The IAT's popularity during the Decade of the Brain helped normalize the claim that racial bias could be detected through cognitive instruments and, by extension, that its effects could be read off brain scans. Media outlets amplified the findings without deep scrutiny because they fit prevailing narratives about race and health disparities.

The policies that followed were substantial. Harvard Catalyst funded Harnett's fellowship specifically to advance the hypothesis that racism-driven adversity alters brain development in measurable ways.
