Implicit bias training programs, built on IAT evidence, were implemented across hospitals, corporations, universities, and government agencies, consuming billions of dollars on interventions whose effectiveness remained unproven. Richmond City Council passed a resolution in 2020 declaring racism a public health crisis, directing its Health Department to develop anti-racism strategies, collect disparity data, and integrate anti-racism into operations, all during the early months of COVID-19 and without additional funding. The academic reviews called for family-centered preventive interventions and refined clinical practices based on structural racism models, influencing how health policy dollars were allocated for resilience programs targeting Black youth. The entire apparatus rested on the premise that racial disparities in brain structure had one acceptable explanation.

The harm was diffuse but real. Attributing elevated rates of PTSD, depression, anxiety, substance use, and early chronic disease in Black youth primarily to structural racism diverted attention and resources from other contributing factors. Implicit bias training, by over-individualizing and over-neuralizing racism, obscured the structural and environmental conditions it claimed to address, substituting cognitive remediation for systemic change. Richmond's Health Department was saddled with a new mandate during a pandemic without the budget to fulfill it. Researchers who questioned the framework faced professional risk. J. Philippe Rushton, decades earlier, had been subjected to calls for dismissal by Ontario's Premier, a formal police investigation, and university attempts to fire him for studying race differences in brain size and intelligence; the chilling effect on subsequent researchers was considerable. The orthodoxy that genetic differences among populations are trivial created, paradoxically, a void that was sometimes filled by crude racialist claims from figures like Nicholas Wade and James Watson, who cited modern genetics without the nuance the data required.

A substantial body of evidence now challenges the assumption. The researchers themselves acknowledged limited neuroimaging literature, critical gaps, and the difficulty of measuring structural racism's mechanisms, concessions buried in the same papers that advanced sweeping causal claims. The studies were overwhelmingly cross-sectional and correlational, relying on self-reports and proxies like poverty and neighborhood disadvantage that confound classism with racism and cannot isolate a unique causal role for racial discrimination. Philosopher Nathan Cofnas drew public attention to the logical leap from brain scan differences to structural racism, using social media sarcasm to highlight what peer review had not caught. More fundamentally, advances in DNA sequencing over two decades have enabled precise measurement of genetic ancestry, revealing real population-level differences in traits from height to disease susceptibility. Studies of gene expression in brain tissue found that among Black Americans, genes affected by ancestry explained 60 percent of differences in expression while environmental factors explained 15 percent. Genome-wide studies have identified genetic variations predicting educational attainment and cognitive test performance, with allele frequencies expected to differ across populations. Prostate cancer risk factors more common in West African ancestry fully account for higher rates in African Americans, demonstrating that ancestry-race correlations are not artifacts of social construction. None of this settles the question of how much of any given disparity is genetic versus environmental. But significant evidence challenges the premise that observed brain differences between racial groups can be attributed to structural racism while biological explanations are ruled out in advance.
---

## No sexual assault increase in Europe from Immigration

*The increase in sexual assaults in Europe is not due to immigration.*

For the better part of two decades, the official position in Sweden and across much of Western Europe held that the rise in reported sexual assaults had nothing to do with immigration. The assumption rested on a set of interlocking beliefs: that socioeconomic deprivation, substance abuse, psychiatric illness, and systemic bias in law enforcement could fully account for any overrepresentation of immigrants in crime statistics. Acculturation researchers reinforced the idea by arguing that separation from the host culture produced poor mental health outcomes, and that better integration policy would close whatever gaps remained. The OECD shaped the discourse further through reports that framed welfare, education, and labor market programs as the path to equal outcomes. These explanations sounded reasonable and aligned neatly with the political consensus that favored large-scale asylum intake. In 2015, Sweden accepted 160,000 asylum seekers, the highest per capita figure in the European Union, on the explicit premise that immigration posed no heightened risk of sexual crime.

The assumption spread in part because the research environment made it difficult to challenge. Scholarly literature on Swedish rape offenders was remarkably thin, focusing on victim-offender relationships and offender age while steering clear of immigrant status as a predictor. Social pressure inside universities and funding bodies treated the topic as sensitive, best left to independent institutions that could present facts without professional consequences. Politicians and commentators reinforced the taboo by warning that any attempt to link immigration and rape would serve extremist forces. Swedish media internalized the prohibition so thoroughly that the subject became, by wide acknowledgment, the single largest forbidden topic in public discourse. Even when SVT, the state broadcaster, aired data from its Mission Investigation program showing that 58 percent of rape convicts were foreign born, its chief editor Ulf Johansson insisted the figures represented only a small fraction of all immigrants and that no conclusions could be drawn. Mustafa Panshiri, a former Afghan police officer resettled in Sweden, warned publicly that deep cultural differences regarding the autonomy of women would produce more sexual offenses unless addressed honestly. He was largely ignored.

Policy followed the assumption faithfully. Sweden amended Chapter 6 of its Criminal Code on sexual offenses six times after 1965, broadening the definition of rape and introducing consent-based and negligent categories. Each expansion inflated reported figures, which officials then cited as unrelated to immigration. Integration programs poured resources into socioeconomic interventions without any requirement to measure their effect on sexual offending rates among the foreign born. Preventive policies avoided targeting immigrant-specific risk factors because doing so would have contradicted the official stance. The result was a policy architecture built on an untested premise. Meanwhile, the raw numbers told a stark story: of 3,039 offenders convicted of raping adults between 2000 and 2015, 59.2 percent had an immigrant background and 47.7 percent had been born outside Sweden. In stranger-rape cases, the proportion of foreign-born offenders exceeded 80 percent.

The human cost was substantial. Sweden recorded 4,810 rapes of adult women in 2022 yet secured only 325 convictions. Ten percent of the population and 18 percent of women reported frequent fear of sexual victimization. Immigrants born outside Sweden constituted 50.6 percent of rape convicts while making up only 18 percent of the matched control population, and they showed markedly higher rates of welfare receipt, neighborhood deprivation, substance use disorders, psychiatric disorders, and prior criminal records. The overrepresentation persisted even after statistical adjustment for all of these factors, a finding that undercut the entire explanatory framework on which policy had been built. The gap between official rhetoric and recorded outcomes widened with each new cohort of arrivals, and the taboo on discussion meant that neither victims nor communities received a clear account of the forces at work.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption is now falling apart, though debate continues among some experts. A nationwide case-control study of 4,032 rape convictions matched against 20,160 controls found that immigrant background remained strongly associated with conviction after adjusting for every standard confounder. Odds ratios stayed significantly elevated for all immigrant subgroups: 6.9 for those with fewer than five years of residency, 6.2 for those who arrived at age 15 or older. Controlling for socioeconomic status, substance use, personality disorders, and criminal history left odds ratios between 2.4 and 6.9 times those of native-born Swedes. The persistence of these figures after full adjustment exposed the limits of the socioeconomic explanation that had governed policy for a generation. SVT's broadcast cracked the wall of silence; the peer-reviewed regressions that followed shifted the burden of proof onto those who still insisted the two phenomena were unrelated. The Swedish National Council for Crime Prevention, BRÅ, acknowledged the pattern in a 2021 report while noting that causes remained inadequately explored. That formulation, strong overrepresentation with inadequate exploration of why, is a fair summary of where the matter stands: the old assumption has lost its empirical foundation, even if a full replacement has yet to be agreed upon.
---

## Mississippi Miracle is Real

*Mississippi's rise in fourth-grade NAEP reading scores reflects real improvement.*

The assumption was straightforward: Mississippi's dramatic rise in fourth-grade NAEP reading scores, from 49th in the nation in 2013 to tied for 8th by 2024, represented a genuine educational transformation driven by the state's 2013 Literacy-Based Promotion Act. The law mandated phonics-based instruction, teacher training in structured literacy, and the retention of third-graders who failed reading proficiency standards. Reformers pointed to the modest cost, roughly $111 extra per pupil annually from a $15 million state investment, as evidence that smart, targeted policy could accomplish what decades of spending had not. Education economics experts like Harry Anthony Patrinos called Mississippi a model for global literacy reform. Noah Spencer published econometric evidence in 2024 arguing the turnaround was real. Wikipedia entries and media outlets from the New York Post to advocacy organizations like ExcelinEd presented the results as an unqualified success. The narrative crossed ideological lines: conservatives saw vindication for standards-based reform, and Diane Ravitch, a longtime critic of test-based accountability, cited the state's demographic-adjusted rankings as proof that poor, predominantly Black states could compete with wealthy ones.

The skeptical counter-argument was technically serious and historically grounded. Howard Wainer, Irina Grabovsky, and Daniel H. Robinson, writing in the journal Significance, argued that the retention policy mechanically truncated the low end of the fourth-grade score distribution. By holding back seven to ten percent of third-graders who failed reading standards, Mississippi changed who sat for the fourth-grade NAEP: the weakest readers were simply absent from the test-taking pool. The resulting gain of more than 0.25 standard deviations, the statisticians argued, could be explained almost entirely by this truncation. Andrew Gelman amplified the critique through his widely read blog at Columbia, bringing the selection-bias question to a broader audience of researchers and policy analysts. The skeptics drew on a grim American tradition. Prior education miracles in Houston, Atlanta, Washington D.C., and El Paso had each been celebrated and then exposed as fraud or statistical manipulation. In Atlanta, Superintendent Beverly Hall won National Superintendent of the Year in 2009 on the strength of rising scores that turned out to be the product of organized cheating; a 2013 grand jury indicted 35 people on racketeering charges, and approximately 180 teachers were implicated. The parallel to New Orleans, where Hurricane Katrina's displacement of the poorest students had inflated post-storm averages, seemed uncomfortably close to what retention was doing in Mississippi.

The assumption that the miracle was entirely real spread through researcher papers, advocacy reports, education journalism, Wikipedia edits, and international policy forums, while the skeptical critique remained largely confined to academic blogs and psychometric journals. The gap between these audiences meant that the technical objections rarely reached the legislators and policymakers who were considering whether to replicate Mississippi's approach. The Literacy-Based Promotion Act became a template: other states looked to Mississippi as proof that ending social promotion and mandating phonics instruction could produce rapid, measurable gains. The illusion claim, meanwhile, fueled opposition to third-grade retention gates in states where such policies were under debate, sustaining social promotion practices that left children reading below grade level with no intervention at all.

The harms ran in both directions. The most direct cost fell on the retained children themselves, disproportionately from the poorest families, who repeated a grade with all the attendant risks of social stigma and increased dropout probability. Whether they benefited from the extra year of reading instruction remained an open empirical question. The harm from the skeptical overcorrection was subtler but potentially larger in scale: if the gains were substantially real, then states that dismissed Mississippi's experience as a statistical artifact and continued with failing policies condemned millions of children to illiteracy. In 2011, roughly 80 percent of Mississippi fourth-graders scored below proficiency in reading. New Mexico ranked last in unadjusted NAEP scores, trailing leaders by 25 points. Oregon's eighth-grade math fell from eight points above the national average in 2000 to four points below by the mid-2020s. These were not abstract numbers; they were children who could not read or compute at basic levels, in states that had chosen not to act.

A substantial body of experts now rejects the claim that the Mississippi gains were purely real or purely illusory. The skeptical case began to crack when Mississippi First's analysis showed the largest NAEP gains occurred between 2013 and 2015, before retention rates were high enough to produce significant truncation; the 2018 cohort behind the celebrated 2019 results had a retention rate below five percent. Urban Institute demographic adjustments ranked Mississippi first in the nation in the 2024 NAEP average. Mississippi's Black fourth-graders scored eight points above the national Black average in reading, a within-group comparison that demographic composition cannot explain away. Andrew Ho, a Harvard testing expert and former NAEP board member, examined the data and found no evidence of gaming. But the eighth-grade numbers remained stubborn: Mississippi ranked 42nd in eighth-grade reading and near the bottom in eighth-grade math in 2024, exactly what a selection-bias story would predict. The Wainer paper's factual error on math rankings, claiming Mississippi ranked 50th when the actual figure was considerably better, weakened the skeptical presentation without resolving the underlying question. The honest assessment, which the evidence increasingly supports, is that Mississippi's gains were a mixture of real instructional improvement and statistical artifact from retention-induced truncation, in proportions that no one has yet been able to determine with confidence. The miracle was not a fraud. It was not a fairy tale either. It was something more common and less satisfying: a genuine partial success wrapped in a narrative that outran the data.
---

## Trump Colluded With Russia

*Donald Trump colluded with Russia during the 2016 election.*

The belief that Donald Trump had colluded with the Russian government during the 2016 presidential election was, for roughly three years, treated as something close to established fact by much of the American press, by significant figures in federal law enforcement, and by a substantial portion of the public. The assumption rested on several foundations that appeared solid at the time: Russia had genuinely interfered in the election through hacking and social media manipulation, Trump had made public statements unusually favorable to Vladimir Putin, and a series of memos compiled by Christopher Steele, a former head of the Russia Desk at MI6, alleged a "well-developed conspiracy" of cooperation between the Trump campaign and the Kremlin. Steele's personal credibility was the load-bearing element. His thirty years of Russia experience and his prior work on FIFA corruption, which had led to real arrests, had earned him genuine trust within the FBI and the State Department. When his dossier arrived, institutions that had found his previous work reliable extended that trust to seventeen memos labeled by their own author as "raw intelligence, not established facts." The distinction between Russia interfering to help Trump and Trump coordinating with Russia was analytically significant, but the coverage collapsed it routinely.

The dossier's origins were political, though this was not widely understood at first. Glenn Simpson, a former Wall Street Journal reporter who co-founded the opposition research firm Fusion GPS, traveled to London to commission Steele's work. The project was funded through the law firm Perkins Coie, whose lawyer Marc Elias represented both the Hillary Clinton campaign and the Democratic National Committee. The Clinton campaign's role in financing the research was not publicly disclosed at the time and was initially denied. The FBI incorporated dossier material into its Crossfire Hurricane investigation and used it to support applications for surveillance warrants under the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act. BuzzFeed News published the full text of the unverified dossier in January 2017, against Steele's wishes, ensuring that every specific allegation, including those later shown to be wrong, entered permanent public circulation. CNN's Carl Bernstein had broken the news of the dossier's existence days earlier. Luke Harding of the Guardian wrote a book called "Collusion" that presented Steele's self-assessed accuracy rate of 70 to 90 percent as though it were a meaningful metric. The New York Times and the Washington Post drove the narrative through hundreds of stories across news and opinion sections, and both papers received Pulitzer Prizes for their coverage. Estimates placed the total number of articles produced during the Mueller investigation at over half a million.

The policies that followed were proportionate to the seriousness of the charge and disproportionate to the evidence supporting it. Robert Mueller was appointed special counsel in May 2017. His investigation lasted nearly two years, employed nineteen lawyers and roughly forty FBI agents, issued more than 2,800 subpoenas, and interviewed approximately 500 witnesses. The FISA warrants targeting Carter Page, a Trump campaign adviser, relied in part on dossier material that the Justice Department's Inspector General later found had been presented with seventeen significant errors and omissions. The collusion framework also shaped the response to the Hunter Biden laptop story in October 2020. The FBI's Foreign Influence Task Force had been briefing Twitter executives about potential hack-and-leak operations for months. Yoel Roth, Twitter's head of trust and safety, had attended these warnings and had participated in an Aspen Institute tabletop exercise that rehearsed how to handle exactly such a scenario. When the New York Post published its reporting, Twitter invoked its hacked materials policy to block the story. James Baker, Twitter's deputy general counsel and a former top FBI lawyer, pushed to suppress it as potentially hacked or faked. The FBI had possessed the laptop since December 2019 and said nothing about its authenticity. Fifty-one former intelligence officials signed a letter stating the story had "all the classic earmarks of a Russian information operation." Nina Jankowicz, a self-described disinformation expert, amplified the claim on social media and later misrepresented the contents of an intelligence community report to support it. The Biden administration subsequently appointed her to lead the Department of Homeland Security's Disinformation Governance Board.

The harm was extensive and operated on multiple levels. American trust in media fell to 26 percent, the lowest among 46 nations surveyed by the Reuters Institute in 2022. Eighty-three percent of Americans told Rasmussen they considered fake news a serious problem; 56 percent agreed the media were "the enemy of the people," a phrase coined by the very man the coverage had targeted. The collusion narrative did not destroy Trump; it armed him. Three years of coverage built on an unproven premise gave him a grievance story in which he was the victim of a politically motivated conspiracy involving the FBI, the Clinton campaign, and the press. That story was not entirely wrong. The suppression of the laptop reporting in October 2020 limited public access to information about the Biden family's business dealings during the final weeks of a presidential election. The Pulitzer Prizes awarded for the collusion coverage became a recurring embarrassment; the Pulitzer board reviewed the matter and declined to rescind them. Bob Woodward, whose Watergate credentials were beyond question, said the Russia coverage "wasn't handled well" and that readers had been "cheated." Dean Baquet, the Times' executive editor, acknowledged being "a little tiny bit flat-footed" but pointed to the prizes as proof of quality. He did not commission a postmortem.

The assumption fell apart in stages. Mueller's report, delivered in March 2019, stated plainly that the investigation "did not establish that members of the Trump campaign conspired or coordinated with the Russian government." The Inspector General's report documented the FBI's mishandling of Steele and the FISA applications, including the alteration of an email by an FBI lawyer to conceal that Carter Page had been a CIA source. The Durham investigation exposed the unreliability of the dossier's sourcing: Igor Danchenko, Steele's primary sub-source, had passed along information originating from casual conversations and, in some cases, from a source with connections to the Clinton campaign. The dossier's most sensational claims, the alleged "pee tape" and Michael Cohen's purported trip to Prague, were never corroborated. Fiona Hill, a Russia expert who served on Trump's National Security Council, described the dossier as a "rabbit hole" and suggested Steele may have been fed disinformation by his own sources. The Hunter Biden laptop was authenticated by forensic analysis; Director of National Intelligence John Ratcliffe stated there was no intelligence indicating it was a Russian fabrication. The Twitter Files, released after Elon Musk's acquisition of the platform, revealed the internal debates and FBI briefings that had preceded the suppression. The DHS Disinformation Governance Board was disbanded within weeks of its creation. No single revelation killed the collusion assumption. It was worn down by the accumulation of things that turned out not to be true, and by the slow, reluctant acknowledgment that institutions had treated speculation as evidence and prizes as vindication.
---

## WPATH SOC-8 Reflects Scientific Consensus

*WPATH’s Standards of Care Version 8 was developed through the rigorous Delphi consensus process based on evidence and expert deliberation.*

For years, the World Professional Association for Transgender Health presented its Standards of Care Version 8 as the product of rigorous scientific deliberation. The document, released in 2022, was said to reflect a Delphi consensus process in which expert panels voted on recommendations, with a 75% threshold required for approval. Major medical organizations, federal officials, and journalists cited SOC-8 as proof that youth gender medicine rested on solid evidentiary ground. The American Academy of Pediatrics, the American Medical Association, and the Endocrine Society pointed to it and to one another in a circular chain of authority. Science magazine described it as reflecting consensus from "hundreds of experts based on systematic reviews." Clinics across the United States adopted its framework. Courts received it as evidence. The Biden administration integrated it into federal health policy. The assumption was plain: SOC-8 was science, not politics.

Growing evidence suggests this assumption was false, or at minimum severely compromised. Internal documents unsealed in the case of Boe v. Marshall revealed that Admiral Rachel Levine, the Assistant Secretary for Health at HHS, pressured WPATH leadership to remove age minimums for adolescent medical transitions and to accelerate the document's release to align with the administration's political calendar. The American Academy of Pediatrics reportedly issued an ultimatum: delete the age floors or face withheld endorsement and public opposition. The age recommendations had been developed through the Delphi process itself; their removal bypassed it. James Cantor, a Canadian sex researcher serving as expert witness in the Alabama case, documented the pressure campaign through WPATH's own emails. Marci Bowers, WPATH's president and a surgeon, promoted the resulting guidelines as settled science. Meanwhile, WPATH's own commissioned systematic reviews, including one led by Kellan Baker of Whitman-Walker, rated the evidence for hormone therapy as low or insufficient, yet concluded the treatments were "likely associated with mental health improvements." An independent review published in the Journal of the Endocrine Society found insufficient studies to draw conclusions about suicide outcomes. WPATH acknowledged in SOC-8 itself that "the number of studies is still low, and there are few outcome studies that follow youth into adulthood."

The assumption spread through an interlocking network of institutions, media outlets, and advocates who treated the guidelines as beyond question. CNN adopted near-verbatim editorial language describing gender-affirming care as "medically necessary, evidence-based care," inserting it into over three dozen articles by different reporters. The Science Vs podcast told listeners the treatments were uncontroversial, citing endorsements from major medical organizations whose own citations, when checked, linked to studies lacking outcome data or contradicting the claims made on their behalf. A study by Diana Tordoff and colleagues at the University of Washington, published in JAMA Network Open, was promoted by the university's press office as showing a "60% reduction in depression" among youth receiving gender-affirming care. Supplementary data tables told a different story: the treatment group showed no statistically significant improvement over time. The apparent benefit derived from the comparison group worsening, and that group shrank to six or seven participants through differential attrition. A study in the New England Journal of Medicine reported small improvements on some self-reported measures but omitted six of eight preregistered key mental health variables, including suicidality and gender dysphoria. Two suicides occurred in the 315-participant cohort within twelve months of starting hormones, a rate of 317 per 100,000 patient-years, far exceeding the general youth rate of 14.2. The journal published the paper without requiring explanation for the omissions. When Yale Law School's Integrity Project, led by Meredithe McNamara and co-authored by Johanna Olson-Kennedy, Jack Turban, and others, published a white paper attacking the UK's Cass Review as scientifically flawed, it failed to disclose that its authors earned hundreds of dollars per hour as expert witnesses defending youth gender medicine, that their own studies had been rated low quality by the Cass systematic reviews, and that Olson-Kennedy had withheld publication of a government-funded study on puberty blockers because the results were politically inconvenient.

The harm was not abstract. Clinics at Children's Hospital Los Angeles, Boston Children's, UCSF, and Lurie Children's prescribed puberty blockers and cross-sex hormones to adolescents on the basis of this evidence framework. Olson-Kennedy referred patients like Clementine Breen for puberty blockers during a first appointment, before the child had seen a therapist; Breen received blockers, hormones, and a double mastectomy, later detransitioned, and filed a negligence lawsuit substantiated by the clinic's own notes. At Karolinska University Hospital in Sweden, one of the world's most prestigious medical institutions, minors received blockers and hormones after quick diagnoses; a documentary by investigative journalist Carolina Jemsby exposed that doctors knew of patient harms, including lowered bone density and skeletal damage, and failed to disclose them. Karolinska halted new hormone treatments in May 2021, citing lack of scientific basis. Parents were pressured into consent by inflated suicide statistics drawn from flawed retrospective surveys and activist organizations like the Trevor Project. Journalists who sought balanced sources, like Benjamin Ryan at the New York Times, had their articles killed and received death threats; Steven Thrasher, occupying a social justice chair at Northwestern's Medill School of Journalism, publicly urged colleagues not to cooperate with reporters seeking critical perspectives. Science-Based Medicine, a site built on skeptical inquiry, retracted a favorable review of Abigail Shrier's critical book by founding editor Harriet Hall and replaced it with articles by advocates containing invented quotes, false claims about researchers like Ray Blanchard and Kenneth Zucker, and the assertion that sex exists on a spectrum. McMaster University scholars who conducted SEGM-funded systematic reviews finding low-certainty evidence were pressured by colleagues and activists to disavow the collaboration; the Southern Poverty Law Center labeled SEGM a hate group. Gordon Guyatt, the man who coined the term "evidence-based medicine," signed a statement declaring youth gender-affirming care medically necessary despite his own team's findings of low evidence, later revealing the decision was shaped by institutional pressure.

The assumption is now fracturing from multiple directions. National health systems in Finland, Sweden, Norway, and England conducted independent evidence reviews and reached the same conclusion: the evidence base for youth gender medicine is remarkably weak. The UK's NHS restricted puberty blockers to clinical research settings. The Cass Review's systematic reviews, conducted through the University of York, rated the literature as plagued by bias and poor quality. The U.S. Supreme Court, in United States v. Skrmetti, upheld Tennessee's ban on youth gender treatments in a 6-3 decision, ruling the law regulated medical care rather than classifying by sex. A 2025 HHS review summarized the lack of supporting evidence. Public opinion has shifted: nearly 70 percent of Americans now oppose puberty blockers for trans-identifying minors. Fact-checks by researchers like James Cantor and Kathleen McDeavitt exposed citational errors in the AAP's foundational 2018 policy statement. The WPATH Files, detransitioner lawsuits, whistleblower accounts from clinics like the one Jamie Reed provided from Washington University, and the exposure of suppressed data have collectively eroded the claim that SOC-8 represented anything close to a scientific consensus. What it represented, an influential minority of researchers now argues, was a political document dressed in the language of evidence, produced under government pressure, defended by institutional inertia, and enforced through social coercion of the kind that science is supposed to resist.
---

## Blue Zones Confer Exceptional Longevity

*Certain regions known as Blue Zones around the world feature significantly higher rates of centenarians and exceptional longevity due to unique lifestyle factors.*

The idea was simple and appealing: certain places on Earth produce an unusual number of people who live past 100, and the reason is how they live. Dan Buettner, working with National Geographic and the National Institute on Aging, identified five such regions, which he called Blue Zones: Okinawa, Sardinia, Ikaria, the Nicoya Peninsula, and Loma Linda, California. Demographic data from these areas showed centenarian rates ten times higher than the United States average. Buettner distilled nine shared lifestyle traits, his "Power 9," from fieldwork in these communities: natural movement, plant-heavy diets, moderate wine consumption, strong social bonds, a sense of purpose. The Danish Twin Study was cited to argue that genes account for only 20 percent of lifespan, leaving 80 percent to lifestyle. The numbers looked official. The story made intuitive sense. People wanted it to be true.

The concept spread fast and far. Buettner published bestselling books and cookbooks. Netflix aired "Live to 100: Secrets of the Blue Zones." National Geographic lent its brand. Blue Zones LLC, Buettner's company (later sold to Adventist Health in 2020), partnered with policymakers to redesign American communities around Power 9 principles, optimizing neighborhoods within a five-mile radius for walking and healthy eating. Media coverage was overwhelmingly credulous. Tourists flocked to poor villages in Greece and Sardinia, generating a cottage industry of yoga retreats and longevity pilgrimages. Scientists, meanwhile, devoted years to explaining the supposed phenomenon, studying diets, genetics, and social structures in these regions.

Saul Justin Newman, a postdoctoral researcher first at the Australian National University and later at Oxford's Institute of Population Ageing, had been warning for years that the data was rotten. He found that the highest rates of extreme old age were predicted not by vegetable consumption or community bonds but by poverty, lack of birth certificates, and short average life expectancy. The Blue Zones of Sardinia, Okinawa, and Ikaria were regions with low incomes, low literacy, and high crime. Only 18 percent of "exhaustively validated" supercentenarians worldwide had a birth certificate; in the United States, the figure was zero. Birthdates in the records clustered on days divisible by five, a telltale sign of fabrication. When US states introduced birth certificates at different times, supercentenarian claims in those states dropped by 69 to 82 percent. The pattern was consistent: better records meant fewer miraculously old people.

Government investigations confirmed the picture. In 2010, the Japanese government reviewed its centenarian rolls and found that 82 percent of citizens listed as over 100 were already dead. In 2012, Greece discovered that 72 percent of centenarians claiming pensions had died. Italy had uncovered pension fraud involving 30,000 dead recipients as early as 1997, concentrated in southern regions like Sardinia. Yet Okinawa and Sardinia remained Blue Zones in the popular imagination. Newman's research, which connected these dots, was rejected by nine peer reviewers and denied publication for years. The scientific community, invested in the longevity narrative, treated his findings as an affront rather than a correction. His work eventually won an Ig Nobel Prize at MIT, a recognition that arrived only after prolonged institutional resistance.

The harm was not trivial. Public health advice built on Blue Zones research promoted daily wine drinking as a longevity secret, a recommendation with obvious risks. It sold Mediterranean and Okinawan diets as proven life-extenders while ignoring inconvenient facts, such as Greece's high obesity rates or the reality that Okinawans ate the fewest vegetables and sweet potatoes of any Japanese prefecture. Poor regions with below-average life expectancy were repackaged as longevity paradises, diverting attention from the deprivation that actually characterized them. Old-age poverty rates explained over half the regional variation in reported remarkable ages in England and France. Pension fraud from inflated age records cost governments real money. Growing evidence now suggests that Blue Zones were never zones of exceptional longevity at all, but zones of exceptional record-keeping failure, and that the lifestyle principles extracted from them rest on a foundation of demographic error and, in some cases, outright fraud.
---

## Iraq Invasion Would Stabilize Region

*Overthrowing Saddam Hussein would replace his government with a stable pro-US regime without causing chaos or fragmentation.*

The assumption was straightforward: overthrow Saddam Hussein and a stable, pro-American government would take his place. The Iraqi people, freed from tyranny, would greet American soldiers as liberators. Democracy would flower in Mesopotamia, and its bloom would spread across the Middle East. This belief rested on several pillars, each of them rotten. Proponents cited Iraq's high literacy rate and considerable oil wealth as evidence the country was ripe for democratic governance. They invoked Kant's democratic peace theory, popularized in modern form by scholars like Michael Doyle and Francis Fukuyama, to argue that a democratic Iraq would be a peaceful Iraq. They dismissed alternatives to invasion, including bolstered containment, formal deterrence, covert action, and airpower support for Iraqi opposition, as no longer realistic. And they insisted, on the basis of intelligence assessments later proved entirely wrong, that Saddam possessed weapons of mass destruction and was on the verge of acquiring nuclear weapons. What they ignored was everything that mattered: Iraq's deep sectarian fractures among Sunni, Shi'a, and Kurd; its arbitrary colonial borders; its tribal loyalties reinforced by rates of cousin marriage running between 46 and 53 percent in Baghdad alone; and the complete absence of any institutional foundation for liberal democracy.

The most damning fact about the invasion is that its chief architect had already explained, in precise detail, why it would fail. Dick Cheney, as Secretary of Defense in 1991, advised President George H.W. Bush against occupying Iraq after the liberation of Kuwait. He warned of a quagmire, of no viable replacement government, of pieces of Iraq flying off. In a 1994 interview, he laid out the risks with clinical clarity: no Arab allies would follow the United States into Baghdad, the region would destabilize, and the question of who would govern was unanswerable. Bush senior followed this advice, and the restraint paid dividends in the form of defense savings and the economic expansion of the 1990s. Then something changed. During the Clinton years, Cheney and his wife took up residence at the American Enterprise Institute, where neoconservatives had grown obsessed with removing Saddam. The Project for the New American Century sent a letter to President Clinton calling for regime change. Donor money flowed toward war agitation. By the time Cheney became Vice President in 2001, he had converted completely. He prioritized Iraq as the main topic for national security briefings before Bush was even inaugurated. Deputy Secretary of Defense Paul Wolfowitz pushed for war against Iraq immediately after September 11. Condoleezza Rice identified Iraq as a key danger. Cheney's one percent doctrine held that even a one percent chance of Saddam aiding al-Qaeda with nuclear weapons must be treated as certainty. It was a formula for acting on fear rather than evidence, and it hit the wrong country.

The assumption spread through an ecosystem of elite validation. Kenneth Pollack, a former CIA analyst and National Security Council official, published "The Threatening Storm: The Case for Invading Iraq" through the Council on Foreign Relations. The book argued that the United States had little choice but to invade, topple the regime, eradicate its weapons programs, and rebuild Iraq as a stable society. It received glowing reviews in the New York Times, Foreign Affairs, the Washington Post, and National Review. Fareed Zakaria praised it as balanced analysis. Gideon Rose of Foreign Affairs called it the starting point for all serious debate. General Anthony Zinni endorsed it as essential reading. Pollack and Daniel Byman of Georgetown and Brookings published work arguing democracy was feasible in post-Saddam Iraq. Meanwhile, Tony Blair's 1999 doctrine of humanitarian intervention provided moral cover from across the Atlantic, and Lord Goldsmith, the UK Attorney General, shifted his legal advice to support the invasion despite private doubts about its UN basis. The mainstream American media whipped up patriotic fervor. Sunday talk shows gave Cheney a platform to promise liberation days before the bombs fell. Dissenting voices existed but were marginalized. German Chancellor Gerhard Schröder warned the Bush administration directly. Steve Sailer published "The Cousin Marriage Conundrum" in January 2003, arguing that Iraq's clan-based social structure would defeat nation-building. Adam Garfinkle warned that imposing democracy on the Arab world would fail and stoke anti-Americanism. They were right, and they were ignored.

The United States invaded Iraq in March 2003. The regime fell quickly, which only confirmed the proponents' confidence and concealed the catastrophe to come. The Coalition Provisional Authority implemented de-Ba'athification, purging the government of experienced administrators. It disbanded the Iraqi military, putting 400,000 combat-trained men on the street with no income and no loyalty to the new order. Looting consumed Basra and Baghdad. By 2004, a full-blown insurgency was underway. The sectarian violence that Cheney had predicted in 1991 arrived on schedule: Shi'a revenge killings against Sunnis, Kurdish separatism, foreign fighters pouring across porous borders. The invasion killed 4,563 Americans and wounded over 32,000. Iraqi deaths were far higher, though the precise toll remains disputed. The fiscal cost will burden American taxpayers until mid-century through veteran care alone, running into the trillions. American prestige was squandered. International law was undermined. The 2008 financial crisis compounded the ruin. In 2008, President Bush himself signed the Status of Forces Agreement mandating full U.S. withdrawal by December 31, 2011, a fact that demolishes the later neoconservative claim that President Obama alone was responsible for the retreat. The withdrawal enabled further deterioration. By 2014, ISIS had seized vast swaths of Iraq and Syria, producing a refugee crisis that destabilized Europe. In Afghanistan, the parallel experiment in democratic nation-building ended with the Taliban overrunning government defenses faster than anyone in Washington had projected, collapsing the entire enterprise.

The assumption fell apart because reality matched the warnings that had been available all along. Post-invasion Iraq splintered along exactly the lines Cheney had described in 1991 and 1994: no viable government, sectarian civil war, pieces of the country breaking off. No weapons of mass destruction were found. No meaningful links to al-Qaeda existed before the invasion, though the invasion itself created them. Pollack, to his credit, later acknowledged that winning the war did not ensure peace and that post-conflict stabilization required far more than combat victory. The five objections raised by skeptics before the war, including societal fragmentation, the peril of political transition, the likelihood of Shi'a domination, and America's well-documented fickleness in long-term commitments, were validated one by one. Joseph Henrich's 2020 book "The WEIRDest People in the World" provided scholarly confirmation of what Sailer had argued in 2003: that Western civic institutions developed over centuries in societies where the Catholic Church had broken down clan structures by banning cousin marriage, and that transplanting those institutions into societies organized around kinship networks was not a matter of will or resources but of deep structural incompatibility. The media that had promoted the war largely declined to hold its promoters accountable, omitting Cheney's earlier warnings from coverage and allowing the architects of the disaster to resurface as commentators when Iraq descended into chaos in 2014. The false assumption did not merely fail as policy. It was a known falsehood, articulated as such by the very man who later sold it to the nation.
---

## Transgenderism Reveals True Inner Self

*Sudden transgender and nonbinary identifications among youth represent authentic innate gender identities finally freed for expression.*

For roughly a decade beginning around 2013, a powerful assumption took hold in Western medicine, media, and activism: that the sudden surge of transgender and nonbinary identifications among adolescents represented the emergence of authentic, innate gender identities finally liberated by social acceptance. The logic seemed straightforward. If society had long suppressed gay identities, it stood to reason that transgender identities had been similarly hidden, and the new openness was simply letting people be who they always were. Mainstream medical organizations endorsed the concept of innate gender identity. The New York Times columnist M. Gessen argued in an op-ed that a teenager's transgender self-knowledge was no different from knowing one is gay, Jewish, or Black. Gender clinics multiplied. The American Academy of Pediatrics issued a 2018 policy statement, lead-authored by psychiatrist Jason Rafferty, endorsing gender-affirming care and claiming growing evidence that it reduced mental health concerns. The premise generated a cascade of sub-beliefs: that children as young as three possessed a coherent, articulable gender identity; that puberty blockers were a safe pause button with decades of pediatric use; that without immediate affirming treatment, dysphoric youth would kill themselves. Each of these claims, examined closely, rested on thin or misrepresented evidence.

The assumption spread through an interlocking network of clinicians, activists, journalists, and institutions that proved remarkably effective at suppressing dissent. Science-Based Medicine, a site founded by Yale neurologist Steven Novella and managed by surgical oncologist David Gorski, published articles presenting youth gender medicine as scientifically settled and retracted a favorable review of Abigail Shrier's critical book "Irreversible Damage" by longtime editor Harriet Hall. The ACLU served as lead counsel challenging state bans on youth gender treatments, with attorney Chase Strangio arguing before the Supreme Court. Admiral Rachel Levine championed the cause from within the Biden Administration. Andrea Long Chu received an almost 8,000-word New York magazine cover story promoting unrestricted access to sex-changing medical care for everyone, including children. At Children's Hospital Los Angeles, Dr. Johanna Olson-Kennedy referred patients for puberty blockers on their first visit before they had seen a therapist, while co-chairing a trans youth support group called Transforming Family with the very therapist later assigned to those patients' families. When researcher Lisa Littman published a 2018 paper positing that rapid-onset gender dysphoria (ROGD) might arise from psychosocial factors rather than innate identity, activist researchers demanded retraction. Springer retracted and then republished the paper under pressure, applying standards inconsistently compared to affirmation studies that relied on the same parental-report methodology. Ray Blanchard, the University of Toronto psychologist who coined the concept of autogynephilia in the 1980s to describe a distinct motivation for male-to-female transition, found his work effectively blacklisted from prestige media. The New York Times, despite extensive coverage of "The White Lotus" season three, refused to mention autogynephilia even when Sam Rockwell's character delivered a monologue portraying it on screen.

The policies that followed were sweeping. Clinical guidelines shifted toward gender affirmation, telling children they were born in the wrong body and providing hormones and surgery based on self-identification. Transgender-identifying youth rose to roughly two percent of the adolescent population, with tenfold increases in clinic referrals. The AAP's policy statement, reaffirmed in 2023, guided pediatricians toward puberty blockers and cross-sex hormones despite citational flaws that fact-checkers James Cantor and Kathleen McDeavitt later exposed. Yale Law School's Integrity Project published a white paper attacking the UK's Cass Review, a comprehensive evidence assessment, without disclosing that its authors included clinicians like Olson-Kennedy and Jack Turban whose own studies had been rated low quality by the Review's systematic analyses, and who earned hundreds of dollars per hour as expert witnesses defending youth gender medicine. The Biden Administration litigated against state bans. Liberal institutions adopted inclusive language policies; the ACLU edited a Ruth Bader Ginsburg quote, replacing "woman" with "person," and Congresswoman Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez used "menstruating person" on national television, a linguistic shift that sparked backlash and weakened abortion-rights messaging by replacing relatable terms with stilted ones.

The harm was substantial and, in many cases, irreversible. Adolescents, disproportionately girls with pre-existing mental health conditions, received puberty blockers that led to near-universal progression to cross-sex hormones without resumption of natal puberty, causing permanent infertility and unknown long-term effects. A patient named Breen received blockers, hormones, and a double mastectomy through Olson-Kennedy's clinic with minimal prior therapy, later detransitioned, and filed a lawsuit in 2024 that exposed lost therapy notes and ignored clinical red flags. Parent-child relationships deteriorated; over half of caregivers in one study felt pressured to support transition. The suicide narrative, promoted without causal evidence, risked becoming a self-fulfilling prophecy by heightening distress in vulnerable youth. Aggressive enforcement of orthodoxy destroyed careers. Researchers like Kenneth Zucker, the Toronto clinician who had warned that social transition would lock in persistence rates higher than natural desistance, were pushed out of their positions. Science-Based Medicine published false claims about both Zucker and Blanchard, later issuing corrections only after journalist Jesse Singal's persistent public critiques.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption is now collapsing. Survey data analyzed by psychologist Jean Twenge showed that trans and nonbinary identification among 18-to-22-year-olds dropped 43 percent between 2022 and 2024, from 9.5 percent to 5.4 percent, confirming researcher Eric Kaufmann's earlier warning that the trend was a generational fad confined to younger cohorts. The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to uphold Tennessee's ban on youth gender treatments. European systematic reviews in Finland, Sweden, England, and Norway found the evidence base for youth gender medicine lacking, leading to treatment restrictions. Olson-Kennedy herself admitted withholding puberty blocker data for political reasons. Annelou de Vries, a leading clinician at the Dutch clinic that had pioneered the blocker protocol, warned in 2020 that new adolescent-onset cases called into question the applicability of prior positive outcomes. The TransYouth Project's own data showed identity instability rising from 6 percent at five years to 18.4 percent at eight years. A 2023 study of 1,655 cases by Suzanna Diaz and J. Michael Bailey corroborated ROGD with post-puberty onset, peer clusters, and mental health problems predating dysphoria. Studies consistently showed 85 percent desistance of childhood gender dysphoria after puberty, often resolving into gay or bisexual outcomes. Public opinion shifted decisively, with almost 70 percent opposing puberty blockers for trans-identified minors. The 42 most popular reader comments on the New York Times' coverage of the Supreme Court decision all opposed the trans movement's position. The spell, as an influential minority of researchers and clinicians had long warned, was breaking.
---

## Rising Diagnoses Signal Mental Illness Epidemic

*Sharp increases in mental health diagnoses among children reflect a genuine surge in the prevalence of mental disorders.*

For roughly two decades, a consensus hardened among pediatricians, public health officials, and the press: children were becoming mentally sicker at an alarming rate. The numbers looked devastating. Claims data from Medicaid and CHIP in 22 states showed diagnoses of any mental health or neurodevelopmental disorder among publicly insured children rising from 10.7 percent in 2010 to 16.5 percent in 2019. The National Survey of Children's Health, conducted by the U.S. Census Bureau and funded by HRSA's Maternal and Child Health Bureau, reported that the prevalence of any diagnosed mental or behavioral health condition among adolescents climbed from 15.0 percent to 20.3 percent between 2016 and 2023, a 35 percent increase. Anxiety diagnoses alone rose 61 percent. A separate study co-authored by Joseph Tkacz of Avalere Health and Brenna L. Brady of IBM Watson Health, drawing on the MarketScan database, claimed a 34.6 percent prevalence increase from 2012 to 2018, with eating disorders up 96 percent and anxiety up 95 percent. The American Academy of Pediatrics joined two other organizations in declaring a child mental health emergency. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimated that 13 to 20 percent of children aged 3 to 17 had diagnosable mental disorders. The New York Times and other outlets framed these figures as proof that American children were profoundly unwell. Researchers at Xiangya Hospital, using Global Burden of Disease modeling, extended the narrative internationally, claiming a genuine pandemic-driven surge. The story seemed airtight.

It was not. A substantial body of experts now reject the premise that rising diagnosis rates reflect a genuine increase in the prevalence of mental illness among children. The most important structural cause was the progressive broadening of diagnostic categories. Allen Frances, who chaired the DSM-IV task force in the early 1990s, approved the inclusion of Asperger's syndrome as a good-faith measure to help children with subtler symptoms access services. Task force studies predicted only a modest increase in diagnoses. Instead, autism-related diagnoses rose over sixteen-fold, from one in 2,500 to one in 150 within a decade, reaching one in 31 today. Frances himself became one of the sharpest critics of the expansion he had authorized. The DSM-5 later allowed autism spectrum disorder and ADHD to coexist for the first time, further inflating numbers. No biomarkers exist for most mental illnesses, making diagnoses inherently subjective and, in practice, unfalsifiable. Only one in four pediatricians reported relying on DSM criteria for ADHD diagnosis. The surveys and claims databases that generated the alarming statistics captured billing codes and parent reports, not validated clinical assessments. The NSCH, for instance, simply equated parent-reported diagnosed conditions with prevalence. A systematic review found direct evidence of ADHD overdiagnosis and overtreatment in children and adolescents.

The assumption spread through a web of aligned incentives. Schools operating under performance-based funding discovered that diagnosing students with ADHD or other disabilities could secure accommodations, extra testing time, medication to calm disruptive behavior, or outright exclusion of low scorers from aggregate results. Parents clamored for diagnoses to gain competitive advantages in resource allocation. Clinicians, facing principal-agent misalignment, had financial reasons to prefer billable interventions over free alternatives like exercise. Social contagion amplified the trend: symptom narratives spread through peer imitation and online memification, reducing stigma to the point where disability became a rewarded identity. The Mental Health Parity Act of 2008 and Affordable Care Act provisions expanded insurance coverage for mental health, facilitating more screenings and diagnoses. Annual NSCH data briefs, published through government channels with raw files and interactive queries, gave journalists and advocates a steady supply of alarming statistics. Post-2020 COVID data on rising emergency visits and hospitalizations added fuel, though the upward trends predated the pandemic.

The harm was considerable and ran in multiple directions. Children with mental illness diagnoses incurred $6,055 in annual medical costs in 2018, nearly four times the $1,629 for matched controls. Mental health emergency room visits rose 55 percent from 2012 to 2016 among pediatric patients. An estimated one million extra ADHD diagnoses accumulated in six years, many resulting in unnecessary medications with side effects like insomnia. Diagnosed adolescents were three times as likely to be disengaged from school, five times as likely to miss eleven or more school days, twice as likely to be bullied, and ten times as likely to have serious difficulty making or keeping friends. Among those needing treatment, 61 percent had difficulty accessing it in 2023, up 35 percent since 2018. The flood of milder cases created a public goods problem: scarce mental health resources spread thin, and children with severe impairments were increasingly ignored unless suicidal. In England, 508 of 752 GPs surveyed said there was rarely or never enough quality adult mental health help; 640 worried specifically about youth services. Meanwhile, 447 GPs reported routinely prescribing antidepressants due to lack of therapy options, knowing the benefits were short-term at best.

The assumption began to crack from several directions at once. A BBC questionnaire found that 442 of 752 GPs believed over-diagnosis was a genuine concern, with many stating plainly that grief, stress, and ordinary unhappiness are not illnesses. Professor Victoria Tzortziou Brown, chair of the Royal College of GPs, warned publicly against medicalizing normal feelings. Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, briefly raised the alarm about over-diagnosis before retracting under political pressure, a sequence that illustrated the social cost of dissent. In the research literature, methodological critiques exposed the fragility of the evidence. Sibling fixed-effects reanalysis of CNLSY data eliminated an apparent Flynn effect that had been cited as evidence of real cognitive shifts, revealing it as a compositional artifact of smarter mothers delaying fertility. Scrutiny of the GBD models showed they relied on surveys prone to the same diagnostic inflation they purported to measure. The recognition of perverse incentives across schools, parents, clinicians, and the pharmaceutical industry, combined with Frances's persistent critiques and the stubborn absence of biomarkers, has left the epidemic narrative in serious doubt. Significant evidence now challenges the claim that children are genuinely sicker. What rose, substantially, was the willingness and the financial motivation to call them so.
---

## Undocumented Immigrants Number 11 Million

*There are approximately 11 million undocumented immigrants residing in the United States.*

For nearly three decades the number 11 million served as the fixed point around which American immigration policy revolved. The figure originated in residual estimation methods applied to the Census Bureau's annual American Community Survey, which subtracted known legal residents from total foreign-born respondents and declared the remainder undocumented. The Pew Hispanic Center refined and popularized the count, publishing regular updates that placed the unauthorized population between 11 and 12 million and describing it as stable after 2008. Government agencies, news organizations, academic researchers, and presidential candidates all treated the number as settled demographic fact. It appeared in budget projections, enforcement planning documents, and congressional testimony without qualification. The figure seemed credible because it came from the same survey apparatus that measured the rest of the population. What it could not measure was the behavior of people who had every reason to avoid a government interviewer knocking on their door.

The number shaped policy in concrete ways. Immigration enforcement budgets, amnesty proposals, welfare restrictions, and service provisions were all scaled to a population of roughly 11 million. Newt Gingrich cited it during the 2012 Republican primary when he proposed local citizen juries to decide legal status for undocumented immigrants with twenty-five years of residency, U.S.-born children, and church membership. He told a Latino forum that Republicans simply could not deport that many people and advocated residency short of full citizenship. The Pew Hispanic Center calculated that about 3.5 million adults might qualify under his plan, and the New York Times published the analysis as neutral data. The Southern Baptist Convention passed a resolution calling for a just and compassionate path to legal status, a position that carried the 11 million baseline into religious discourse and encouraged churches to minister without regard to immigration status. In each case the modest-sounding number made gradualist approaches seem proportionate and mass enforcement seem absurd.

Growing evidence now suggests the conventional estimate was substantially wrong. In 2018, Mohammad Fazel-Zarandi, Jonathan Feinstein, and Edward Kaplan of Yale published a demographic model built not on household surveys but on operational data: deportation records, visa overstays, and border apprehensions from 1990 to 2016. Their mean estimate was 22.1 million undocumented immigrants, with a range extending to 29.5 million. The confidence intervals did not overlap with the old 11.3 million figure. A 2005 Bear Stearns analysis had reached a similar conclusion years earlier, estimating the population could be as high as 20 million. Steven Camarota of the Center for Immigration Studies dismissed the Yale study as unsupportable and defended the Census-based surveys, illustrating how institutional commitments to a single methodology could outlast accumulating contrary evidence. The broader expert community has not reached full consensus, but the challenge to the old number is serious and has not been refuted on its own terms.

The harm caused by the undercount was not abstract. Crime rate calculations for undocumented immigrants were distorted because the same number of offenses was spread across a population potentially twice as large, skewing the per capita figures that informed public debate. Job displacement pressures on low-skilled American workers, minorities, ex-convicts, and the disabled were miscalculated because roughly eight million unauthorized workers were competing for jobs in an economy whose planners assumed a smaller labor pool. Housing, education, and health service costs for millions of unacknowledged residents went unbudgeted. The low figure discouraged aggressive enforcement and prolonged political arguments over half-measures, narrowing the options presented to voters and leaving officials unprepared for the actual scale of the problem. Candidates who adopted hard-line positions faced pressure to moderate because deporting "only" 11 million seemed both cruel and impractical; deporting 22 million was simply not discussed.

The assumption has not collapsed so much as eroded. The Yale model exposed the systematic undercount inherent in asking a hidden population to identify itself on government forms. Earlier government materials on removal operations were quietly archived and marked as no longer reflective of current practice. Inside the Southern Baptist Convention, narrow defeat of anti-amnesty amendments revealed that even supporters of the resolution harbored doubts about language that critics called an Orwellian denial of amnesty. The old number still circulates in policy discussions, but it now competes with estimates that suggest the United States has been making decisions about immigration for a generation based on a population figure that may have been off by a factor of two. Whether the true count is closer to 16 million or 29 million remains uncertain. What is increasingly difficult to maintain is that 11 million was ever good enough.
---

## Border Surge Not Key Voter Issue

*A surge at the border from lax immigration policies would not be an important issue to most voters, and stronger enforcement would alienate Latino and progressive voters.*

For most of the 2010s and into the early 2020s, a bipartisan consensus held that border surges from permissive immigration policies would not rank among voters' top concerns, and that any move toward stricter enforcement would alienate Latino and progressive constituencies essential to electoral success. Democrats believed this because their activist base equated restriction with racism, and because the "emerging Democratic majority" thesis, popularized by John B. Judis and Ruy Teixeira, forecast that demographic shifts would deliver a permanent progressive coalition. Republicans believed a version of it too: Karl Rove's circle argued the GOP would lose some white votes per immigrant but recoup them through sheer volume of new minority voters, leading to strategies like George W. Bush spending $20 million in unwinnable California in 2000. The assumption seemed credible in part because Latino population growth was real, and because Trump's family separation policy in 2018 had generated enormous backlash. Steve Sailer warned as early as November 2000, using exit poll data, that the Electoral College math did not support the volume theory, and Pete Wilson had already demonstrated in 1994 that campaigning against illegal immigration subsidies could erase a 20-point deficit. Both were dismissed or marginalized.

The assumption shaped policy most consequentially during the Biden transition and presidency. Immigration experts briefed the incoming team via Zoom in late 2020, warning that Biden's humane pledges threatened to trigger a border surge and political crisis. Biden appeared to grasp the risk but did not act on the recommended deterrents. His chief of staff Ron Klain, along with senior advisers Mike Donilon, Jennifer O'Malley Dillon, and Anita Dunn, underestimated both the scale of potential migration and the political reaction, convinced that enforcement measures would alienate key voter blocs. Biden pledged to rescind the Migrant Protection Protocols, halt border wall construction, and expand refugee admissions. His DHS nominee, Alejandro Mayorkas, the architect of DACA, viewed immigrants as a benefit and prioritized reversing Trump-era restrictions. Senator Richard Durbin captured the prevailing optimism by insisting the crisis was a product of Trump's executive orders and could be undone by similar orders. Rodolfo Karisch, a retired Border Patrol sector chief, and Theresa Cardinal Brown of the Bipartisan Policy Center warned that abrupt reversal would invite chaos, but their counsel was rebuffed.

The assumption spread through mutually reinforcing channels. Democratic operatives and progressive organizations like Way to Win treated immigration and cultural concerns as distractions manufactured by the right, insisting that economic populism alone would suffice. National media bundled diverse groups, from South Texas Tejanos to Miami Cubans, under a monolithic "Latino" label and assumed this constructed bloc would punish any party that toughened border policy. In Britain, a parallel dynamic played out: left-wing Labour MPs like Clive Lewis denounced Denmark's Social Democrats for adopting strict immigration rules, and unions like Unison demanded reversal of hardline measures after a byelection loss, treating the result as proof that enforcement alienated voters. Economists reinforced the framework by treating immigration as a purely economic question, dismissing cultural and institutional absorption concerns. The "Great Awokening" among younger Democratic staff further entrenched the view that restriction was morally disqualifying.

The harm was extensive and measurable. Migrant encounters at the southern border doubled after Biden's inauguration and kept climbing, peaking above 300,000 in December 2023. Cities like New York, which housed over 60,000 migrants in shelters, and Denver faced severe strains on social services. Recidivism under Title 42 rose from 7 percent in fiscal year 2019 to 27 percent in 2021, while "gotaways" reached 73,500 in a single month. The undocumented population, already grown from 3 million in 1986 to 12 million by 2008 despite massive enforcement buildups, expanded further. Politically, the damage was severe: Biden's approval on border security fell to 23 percent, and immigration surged to the top of Gallup's "most important problem" list in February 2024, with 28 percent of Americans naming it, the highest since 2019. A record 55 percent viewed large-scale illegal immigration as a critical threat. Concern rose most sharply among Democrats (up 27 points), seniors, Hispanics, Black voters, and women, groups the assumption had treated as indifferent or sympathetic to permissive policies. The anger helped return Donald Trump to the presidency.

Growing evidence suggests the assumption was wrong on nearly every count. Denmark's Social Democrats drove asylum claims to a 40-year low with strict policies, kept the far-right marginal, and won elections, a model now studied by UK Labour Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood. More In Common polling showed that majority Labour and Green voters supported hardline proposals, contradicting claims of alienation. In the United States, Trump won the border county of Zapata, Texas, after a century of Democratic dominance, and gained among non-white voters broadly. FOIA data obtained by the Cato Institute revealed that Biden had in fact tripled interior detention, increased border detention twelvefold, and removed 3.3 million crossers, triple Trump's total, yet the political narrative of laxity proved indelible because the surge itself was real and visible. A working paper by law professors Adam Cox, David Hausman, and Mary Hoopes, analyzing nine prominent enforcement actions from Obama through Biden, found few large immediate effects on border numbers, suggesting that labor demand, global internet access, and conditions in sending countries drove flows more than any president's posture. The crisis did not end because of a dramatic policy shift; encounters halved before Biden's June 2024 executive order, as labor markets cooled. What the assumption's proponents failed to understand was simpler than any economic model: voters could see the chaos, and they blamed the people who had promised it would not matter.
---

## Trauma Lodges in Body Tissues

*Traumatic experiences embed in the body and tissues, causing ongoing harm relieved by somatic processing.*

The assumption that traumatic experiences literally embed themselves in body tissues, lodging there until released through somatic processing, gained enormous popular traction in the early twenty-first century. Its most prominent champion was Bessel van der Kolk, a Dutch-born psychiatrist whose 2014 book "The Body Keeps the Score" spent more than six years on the New York Times bestseller list and became something close to a sacred text in therapy culture. Van der Kolk argued that trauma encodes in the viscera, that it causes autoimmune disorders and muscular problems, and that body-focused therapies like yoga, EMDR, and neurofeedback are necessary to dislodge it. The idea seemed credible on its face. Traumatized people do feel things in their bodies: racing hearts, clenched muscles, chronic pain. The Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACE) studies had already demonstrated dose-response links between early trauma and later physical illness. Bodyworkers reported clients releasing "emotional energy" during sessions. Peter Levine, developer of Somatic Experiencing, argued that trauma could be resolved by completing thwarted protective responses through gradual bodily focus. Clinicians like Cynthia Price at the University of Washington promoted interoceptive interventions as a way to change brain plasticity from the bottom up. The logic felt intuitive, and intuition proved more persuasive than evidence.

The assumption spread through multiple channels simultaneously. Therapists required patients to read van der Kolk's book and adopted its methods, sometimes steering clients away from talk therapy toward acupressure tapping and other somatic techniques. Universities including the University of Southern California and Rutgers assigned the book in social work and psychology courses. Van der Kolk himself conducted trainings for mental health professionals, educators, policymakers, and law enforcement. Popular psychologists like Nicole LePera broadcast the idea to large online audiences, claiming that unremembered childhood trauma is stored in the body. The World Health Organization's call for non-cognitive strategies amid global mental health needs lent further legitimacy to body-based interventions like the Community Resiliency Model, developed by the Trauma Resource Institute. Academic publications in journals like Frontiers in Psychology and Frontiers in Neuroscience gave the framework a veneer of scientific rigor. Researchers at Western University's Schulich School of Medicine proposed hierarchical models linking somatic sensory processing dysfunction to PTSD. The National Child Traumatic Stress Network, a Congressionally mandated initiative that van der Kolk helped establish, funded 150 centers for trauma interventions, including somatic ones.

The policies that followed were substantial. Trauma-informed care programs proliferated across schools, hospitals, and social services, many emphasizing somatic practices grounded in the body-storage model. Critical Incident Stress Debriefing, designed to process trauma immediately after a crisis, was adopted by emergency services nationwide. Official guidelines eventually had to forbid CISD after studies showed it failed to prevent PTSD and in some cases worsened symptoms. Meanwhile, the APA, NICE, the VA, and the Department of Defense promoted cognitive therapies like Prolonged Exposure and Cognitive Processing Therapy as first-line PTSD treatments, creating a strange split: institutional medicine went one direction while popular culture, armed with van der Kolk's book, went another.

The harm was real and varied. Survivors who absorbed the body-storage framework reported feeling ashamed, hopeless, and pathologized, convinced their PTSD was lifelong and physically embedded. Some pursued unproven somatic treatments instead of evidence-based talk therapy. Richard McNally, a Harvard psychologist, described recovered memory therapy inspired by van der Kolk's approach as "arguably the most serious catastrophe to strike the mental health field since the lobotomy era," and the somatic trauma framework risked repeating those harms by encouraging troubled people to adopt false narratives about their pasts. Van der Kolk's book stigmatized female survivors as "addicted to trauma" while largely ignoring nonwhite victims. Existing treatments already failed 30 to 50 percent of patients, with dropout rates of 26 to 40 percent; directing people toward therapies with no proven efficacy only widened that gap. The authors of the foundational Somatic Experiencing theory paper admitted that no peer-reviewed evidence for SE existed: no case studies, no trials, no mechanism tests. Their key illustrative case was a composite drawn from multiple patient files, with the authors themselves noting possible selection bias.

A substantial body of experts now reject the core claim. Neuroscientist Lisa Feldman Barrett argued that the brain constructs all emotional experience from predictions, that trauma and emotions are not stored in the body, and that the body-as-independent-scorekeeper model is biologically untenable. Researchers whose work van der Kolk cited pushed back publicly: Jennie Noll of the University of Rochester stated he had mischaracterized her study on incest survivors, noting that her paper contained no mention of asthma despite van der Kolk's claim that traumatized children had fifty times the asthma rate. George Bonanno of Columbia University, a co-author on Noll's study, criticized van der Kolk's assertions about abused girls' social development as unsupported by their data. Van der Kolk was fired from his own Trauma Center in 2017 for creating a hostile work environment amid allegations of sexual mistreatment, a fact that did little to slow book sales but did expose a certain irony. A 2025 expert consensus review found broad agreement that each basic physiological assumption of polyvagal theory, one of the scientific pillars van der Kolk relied upon, is untenable. Cellular memory research, such as studies showing kidney cells learning chemical patterns, provides some biological plausibility for body-wide adaptation, but significant evidence challenges the leap from cellular responses to the claim that trauma memories reside in muscles and organs awaiting release. The body does respond to stress. The question is whether that response constitutes storage, and whether the prescribed treatments do anything more than the simpler mechanism of benign exposure already explains. On both counts, the evidence is thin.
---

## Trans Skepticism Causes Suicide

*Skepticism of transgender ideology drives trans people to suicide and equates to genocide.*

The assumption was straightforward and, for a time, nearly universal in polite society: skepticism of transgender ideology kills transgender people. To question the evidence for youth gender medicine, to doubt that puberty blockers are safe and reversible, to resist the language of gender identity, was to drive vulnerable people toward suicide. The stronger version, promoted by activists and echoed by mainstream outlets, held that such skepticism constituted a form of genocide. This belief seemed credible through sheer repetition. Over roughly a dozen years, major media organizations, medical associations, and advocacy groups hammered the message that trans people are uniquely fragile, that affirmation is the only ethical response, and that doubt is violence.
